name: Yellowstone National Park
description: Yellowstone is the world's first national park and one of the most geologically active places on Earth. It sits atop a supervolcano caldera and is home to more than half the world's hydrothermal features — over 10,000 geysers, hot springs, fumaroles and mud pots. The park covers 8,983 square kilometres across three US states and protects one of the last intact temperate ecosystems on the planet. Wildlife includes bison, grizzly bears, wolves, elk, pronghorn and bald eagles. The Grand Prismatic Spring, with its vivid rings of orange, yellow and green caused by heat-loving microbes, is one of the most photographed natural features in the world.
history: When explorers first described Yellowstone's geysers and boiling mud in the 1800s, most people back East assumed they were lying. A series of government surveys in the early 1870s confirmed the wonders, and in 1872 President Ulysses S. Grant signed the Yellowstone National Park Protection Act — making it the world's first national park. The park concept, born here, would spread across the globe. Old Faithful geyser, which erupts roughly every 90 minutes, has been watched by millions of visitors since the park opened. In 1988 catastrophic wildfires burned nearly a third of the park, but the subsequent ecological recovery became a landmark study in natural resilience. The reintroduction of wolves in 1995 famously triggered a trophic cascade that transformed the entire ecosystem.

name: Yosemite National Park
description: Yosemite is a place of extraordinary granite grandeur in the Sierra Nevada mountains of California. The Yosemite Valley — just 11 kilometres long and barely a kilometre wide — is flanked by sheer cliffs including El Capitan (2,307m above sea level) and Half Dome. The park contains some of the world's tallest waterfalls, ancient giant sequoia groves, sub-alpine meadows and over 800 kilometres of hiking trails. Ansel Adams' iconic black-and-white photographs of Yosemite are among the most recognised landscape images in history, and helped galvanise the US conservation movement.
history: John Muir, the Scottish-American naturalist who would become the father of the US national parks movement, first visited Yosemite in 1868 and declared it a temple of nature. President Abraham Lincoln signed the Yosemite Grant in 1864, protecting the valley from development — the first time the US government set aside land purely for public enjoyment. It became a national park in 1890. Theodore Roosevelt famously camped here with Muir in 1903, sleeping under the stars near Glacier Point, and returned to Washington inspired to dramatically expand protected lands across America.

name: Grand Canyon National Park
description: The Grand Canyon is one of the seven natural wonders of the world — a colossal gorge carved by the Colorado River over millions of years. It stretches 446 kilometres long, up to 29 kilometres wide and drops over 1,800 metres to the canyon floor. The exposed rock layers read like a geological textbook, with the oldest rocks at the bottom dating back nearly two billion years. Over five million people visit each year, drawn by the canyon's constantly shifting colours — crimson, gold, purple and white — as light changes through the day. The canyon is also sacred to eleven Native American tribes who have lived here for thousands of years.
history: Native Americans have lived in and around the canyon for at least 4,000 years. Spanish explorers became the first Europeans to see it in 1540, but it remained largely unknown to the outside world until John Wesley Powell led the first river expedition through the canyon in 1869. President Theodore Roosevelt visited in 1903 and famously declared: 'Leave it as it is. You cannot improve on it.' He designated it a national monument in 1908, and it became a national park in 1919. Today it is one of the most visited natural landmarks on Earth.

name: Banff National Park
description: Banff is Canada's oldest and most visited national park, sitting in the heart of the Canadian Rockies. It is famous for its impossibly turquoise glacial lakes — Lake Louise and Moraine Lake among them — dramatic mountain peaks, ancient glaciers and abundant wildlife including grizzly bears, wolves, elk and bighorn sheep. The Icefields Parkway, one of the world's most scenic drives, runs 230 kilometres through the park past towering peaks and ancient ice fields. Banff town itself, nestled in the mountains, is a year-round destination for skiing, hiking and wildlife viewing.
history: In 1883, three Canadian Pacific Railway workers discovered hot springs at the base of Sulphur Mountain and attempted to claim the land. The federal government instead set aside 26 square kilometres as a public reserve in 1885 — the seed of what would become Banff National Park in 1930. The arrival of the transcontinental railway transformed the remote wilderness into an accessible tourism destination, with the iconic Fairmont Banff Springs Hotel opening in 1888. Today the park covers 6,641 square kilometres and is a UNESCO World Heritage Site as part of the Canadian Rocky Mountain Parks.

name: Kruger National Park
description: Kruger National Park is one of Africa's largest game reserves and the flagship of South African conservation, covering nearly 20,000 square kilometres — roughly the size of Wales. It is home to an extraordinary concentration of wildlife including the Big Five (lion, leopard, elephant, buffalo and rhino), as well as cheetah, wild dog, giraffe, hippo, crocodile and over 500 bird species. The park spans two biomes and nine distinct landscape zones, from open savanna and dense bush to rocky kopjes and riverine forest. Self-drive safaris give visitors an immersive, independent wildlife experience unlike anywhere else in Africa.
history: The area was first protected in 1898 when Transvaal President Paul Kruger proclaimed the Sabi Game Reserve to halt the rampant hunting that had devastated wildlife populations. After the Anglo-Boer War, conservation efforts resumed and in 1926 the reserve was proclaimed the Kruger National Park — one of the first in Africa. It opened to the public in 1927 with a handful of cars bumping along dirt tracks. Today it receives over a million visitors per year and is central to South Africa's multi-billion-rand ecotourism economy. Kruger is also a cornerstone of the Great Limpopo Transfrontier Park, one of the world's largest cross-border conservation areas.

name: Serengeti National Park
description: The Serengeti is the site of one of the greatest wildlife spectacles on Earth — the annual Great Migration, in which over 1.5 million wildebeest, 250,000 zebra and hundreds of thousands of gazelle thunder across the open plains in search of fresh grass and water. The park covers 14,750 square kilometres of seemingly endless savanna, acacia woodland and kopjes (rocky outcrops), and supports the highest concentration of large predators in Africa, including lions, leopards, cheetahs, hyenas and wild dogs. Hot air balloon safaris at dawn over the golden plains are a bucket-list experience.
history: The Maasai people named these endless grasslands 'Siringet' — the place where the land runs on forever. The first European to visit was Austrian explorer Oscar Baumann in 1892. The Serengeti became a game reserve in 1929 and a national park in 1951. American photographer Alan Root's groundbreaking wildlife films in the 1960s brought the Serengeti to global living rooms for the first time. It was declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1981, recognised as one of the greatest remaining natural ecosystems on the planet. The annual migration follows a circular route of roughly 3,000 kilometres driven by rainfall and pasture.

name: Table Mountain National Park
description: Table Mountain National Park protects one of the world's most recognisable natural landmarks — the iconic flat-topped Table Mountain overlooking Cape Town — along with the entire Cape Peninsula down to Cape Point, and Boulders Beach where a colony of African penguins has made its home among granite boulders. The park lies at the heart of the Cape Floristic Region, one of the world's six plant kingdoms and the smallest yet most diverse, containing over 9,000 plant species — more than in the entire United Kingdom. The summit of Table Mountain, often wreathed in its famous 'tablecloth' of cloud, can be reached by cable car or on foot.
history: Table Mountain has been a landmark for sailors for centuries — it is visible from 200 kilometres out to sea and was used as a navigational reference point on the spice route to India. The first recorded ascent was by Portuguese explorer António de Saldanha in 1503. The Cape Peninsula was declared a nature reserve in stages from 1939, and the national park was formally proclaimed in 1998. In 2011, Table Mountain was named one of the New Seven Wonders of Nature, receiving over 440 million votes worldwide. The Cape Floristic Region is a UNESCO World Heritage Site — one of only a handful of biodiversity hotspots to receive this recognition.

name: Torres del Paine National Park
description: Torres del Paine is Patagonia at its most spectacular — a wilderness of granite towers, turquoise lakes, ancient glaciers and windswept pampas at the southern tip of South America. The three iconic granite towers that give the park its name rise dramatically to 2,850 metres above the steppe, their near-vertical faces beloved by climbers from around the world. The park contains Grey Glacier, which calves icebergs into a milky-blue lake, and is home to guanacos, condors, pumas, foxes and the rare huemul deer. The famous 'W' and 'O' trekking circuits are among the world's great multi-day hikes.
history: The area has been inhabited by indigenous Kawésqar and Aonikenk peoples for thousands of years. European settlers arrived in the late 19th century, bringing sheep farming that dramatically altered the landscape. The park was established in 1959 and declared a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve in 1978. A series of devastating fires between 2005 and 2012 burned significant sections of the park, prompting major conservation and reforestation efforts. Today Torres del Paine is Chile's most visited national park, drawing trekkers, photographers and nature lovers from across the globe.

name: Galápagos National Park
description: The Galápagos Islands are a living laboratory of evolution — a volcanic archipelago 900 kilometres off the coast of Ecuador where extraordinary creatures evolved in isolation, producing species found nowhere else on Earth. Giant tortoises that can live over 150 years, marine iguanas (the world's only sea-going lizard), blue-footed boobies, flightless cormorants and Darwin's finches are among the unique wildlife that astonished Charles Darwin during his 1835 visit. The islands straddle the equator where three major ocean currents meet, creating an unparalleled marine ecosystem above and below the waterline.
history: Charles Darwin arrived aboard HMS Beagle in September 1835 and spent five weeks observing the islands' unique fauna. His observations — particularly of how species varied between islands — became central to his theory of natural selection, published in On the Origin of Species in 1859. The islands became a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1978, one of the first ever inscribed. Ecuador established strict visitor controls and the Galápagos National Park in 1959, on the centenary of Darwin's famous publication. Today the islands face ongoing pressure from invasive species, climate change and limited tourism, managed under one of the world's most sophisticated conservation regimes.

name: Fiordland National Park
description: Fiordland is New Zealand's largest national park and one of the most dramatic wilderness areas on Earth — a vast region of ancient rainforest, soaring peaks, plunging waterfalls and deep, still fiords carved by glaciers over millions of years. Milford Sound, the park's most visited destination, is famously described as the eighth wonder of the world: sheer granite walls rise 1,200 metres straight from the dark water, while waterfalls cascade hundreds of metres in silvery threads. The park is also home to the rare kiwi, the endangered takahē and the flightless kakapo, one of the world's rarest birds.
history: Fiordland's remoteness kept it largely unexplored by Europeans until the late 19th century. Māori knew the region as Piopiotahi and used it for seasonal journeys to collect pounamu (greenstone). The explorer Donald Sutherland discovered the Sutherland Falls — once thought to be the world's tallest waterfall — in 1880. The park was established in 1952 and became part of the Te Wahipounamu UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1990, alongside three other South Island parks. Rudyard Kipling's description of Milford Sound as the eighth wonder of the world is inscribed at the fiord's entrance.

name: Plitvice Lakes National Park
description: Plitvice Lakes is Croatia's oldest and largest national park and one of Europe's most magical natural landscapes. Sixteen terraced lakes of vivid turquoise, green and blue are connected by a series of waterfalls and cascades that tumble over natural travertine barriers — living rock formations built by mosses and algae that add a few millimetres each year. Wooden boardwalks thread between the lakes at water level, giving visitors an intimate experience of the rushing water and lush forest. The colours shift with the light and season, from deep emerald in summer to gold and russet in autumn.
history: The area has been protected since 1928, making it one of the earliest conservation areas in the Balkans, and was officially declared a national park in 1949. UNESCO inscribed it as a World Heritage Site in 1979, recognising its outstanding natural beauty and the unique geological processes that continue to shape it. During the Croatian War of Independence in 1991, the park was the site of the first armed clash of the conflict and was occupied for several years. After the war, major restoration work returned the park to its pre-war state, and it reopened to international visitors in 1996.

name: Etosha National Park
description: Etosha is one of the great wildlife parks of Africa, centred on the vast Etosha Pan — a flat, white salt desert so large it is visible from space. During the dry season, animals from across the region converge on the park's waterholes in extraordinary concentrations, making wildlife viewing effortless and thrilling. Elephants, lions, leopards, cheetahs, black and white rhinos, giraffes, zebras and over 340 bird species all inhabit the park. The ghostly white landscape of the pan at dawn, with its shimmering mirages and stark horizons, is unlike anywhere else in Africa.
history: The Etosha Pan was once a massive inland lake fed by the Kunene River — millions of years ago, geological shifts caused the river to change course, and the lake slowly evaporated, leaving the great salt pan we see today. The area was first designated a game reserve in 1907 by the German colonial administration, and became a national park in 1958. In its early years, Etosha was one of the world's largest protected areas at 99,000 square kilometres — it has since been reduced but still covers over 22,000 square kilometres. Conservation efforts have seen black rhino populations recover significantly from near-extinction.

name: Komodo National Park
description: Komodo National Park is home to the Komodo dragon, the world's largest living lizard, which can grow up to three metres long and weigh 70 kilograms. These prehistoric-looking reptiles, which have lived on these volcanic islands for millions of years, hunt deer, pigs and water buffalo using a combination of venom and bacteria-laced saliva. The park encompasses three large islands and numerous smaller ones, with dramatic volcanic landscapes, savanna grasslands, white sand beaches, mangroves and some of the richest and most diverse marine environments in the world — including manta ray cleaning stations and exceptional coral reefs.
history: The Komodo dragon was unknown to Western science until 1910, when Dutch colonial officers received reports from fishermen of a 'land crocodile' on the island of Komodo. A Dutch expedition confirmed the animal's existence in 1912. The islands became a reserve in 1938 specifically to protect the dragons. Komodo National Park was established in 1980 and designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1991, recognised for both its unique terrestrial fauna and its extraordinary marine biodiversity. The park became one of the New Seven Wonders of Nature finalists in 2011.

name: Uluru-Kata Tjuta National Park
description: At the spiritual heart of Australia rises Uluru — a colossal sandstone monolith 348 metres high and 9.4 kilometres in circumference, glowing red, ochre and violet as the light changes from dawn to dusk. Sacred to the Anangu people for over 10,000 years, Uluru is one of the world's most recognisable natural icons. Nearby Kata Tjuta (The Olgas), a group of 36 domed rock formations, is equally sacred and equally spectacular. The landscape around these geological wonders is a living cultural document — every rock, waterhole, plant and animal features in Anangu Tjukurpa (Dreaming) law that has been passed down through countless generations.
history: The Anangu people have inhabited this desert landscape for at least 10,000 years and consider Uluru and Kata Tjuta to be deeply sacred ancestral sites. European explorer William Gosse was the first non-Aboriginal person to see Uluru, in 1873, naming it after South Australian Premier Henry Ayers. The rock was handed back to the Anangu people in 1985 under a landmark joint management agreement. In 2019, climbing Uluru was permanently banned at the request of the Anangu, who had long asked visitors not to climb their sacred site. The park was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1987, first for its natural values and later for its profound cultural significance.

name: Amboseli National Park
description: Amboseli offers one of Africa's most iconic safari images: large herds of free-ranging elephants moving across open plains with the snow-capped summit of Mount Kilimanjaro — Africa's highest peak at 5,895 metres — rising magnificently on the horizon across the Tanzanian border. The park covers 392 square kilometres of dry savanna, seasonal swamps, woodlands and open plains, and is famous for its large elephant population, which has been the subject of one of the longest-running wildlife studies in the world. Huge herds of buffalo, wildebeest, zebra, giraffe and various antelope species share the plains, while predators include lions, cheetahs and leopards.
history: The area around Amboseli has been home to the Maasai people for centuries — they still live alongside and around the park, their cattle grazing the same plains as the wildlife. The name Amboseli comes from the Maasai word meaning 'salty dusty place', referring to the dried-up lake bed at the centre of the park. It was declared a national reserve in 1948 and a national park in 1974, a move that caused significant tension with the Maasai who were excluded from their traditional lands. Decades of conservation efforts and community benefit schemes have helped improve relations. The long-term Amboseli Elephant Research Project, begun in 1972 by Cynthia Moss, has transformed our understanding of elephant society and intelligence.

name: Acropolis of Athens
description: The Acropolis of Athens is the supreme expression of ancient Greek civilisation — a sacred rock rising 156 metres above the city, crowned by the Parthenon temple and three other major monuments: the Erechtheion, the Propylaia and the Temple of Athena Nike. The Parthenon, dedicated to the goddess Athena, is the finest example of Doric architecture ever built. Its proportions, refined by optical illusions — slightly curved floors, tapering columns — give it a sense of perfect balance. The Acropolis Museum at the foot of the rock houses priceless original sculptures and provides one of the finest archaeological museum experiences in the world.
history: The rock has been inhabited since Neolithic times, around 4000 BC. The great monuments were built under the statesman Pericles between 461 and 429 BC, using the finest Pentelic marble and the most skilled craftsmen in Greece. The Parthenon was originally painted in vivid colours. Over 2,500 years it served as a temple, a Christian church, a mosque, and an ammunition store — which exploded in 1687 during a Venetian bombardment, causing serious damage still visible today. The Elgin Marbles, sculptural friezes removed by British diplomat Lord Elgin in 1801, remain in the British Museum despite ongoing Greek requests for their return.

name: Colosseum, Rome
description: The Colosseum is the largest amphitheatre ever built and the most enduring symbol of the Roman Empire. Its elliptical form, 188 metres long, 156 metres wide and 57 metres high, could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators for gladiatorial contests, animal hunts, public executions, re-enactments of famous battles and even mock sea battles when the arena was flooded. The complex engineering included a retractable awning (the velarium) to shade the crowd, an elaborate underground network of tunnels and cages for animals and gladiators, and a drainage system so sophisticated that floodwater could be cleared within minutes.
history: Construction began under Emperor Vespasian around 70 AD and was completed by his son Titus in 80 AD. The inaugural games lasted 100 days, during which over 9,000 animals were killed. Gladiatorial contests continued for nearly 400 years until Emperor Honorius banned them in 404 AD. The last recorded animal hunts took place in 523 AD. During the Middle Ages, the Colosseum was converted into housing and workshops, and its stone was plundered for building material — about two-thirds of the original structure was eventually removed. In the 18th century, Pope Benedict XIV declared it a sacred site in memory of the Christians martyred there, halting further demolition.

name: Taj Mahal
description: The Taj Mahal is widely considered the world's most perfect building — a monument of sublime beauty and heartbreaking love. The ivory-white marble mausoleum stands on the south bank of the Yamuna River in Agra, its reflection shimmering in the long ornamental pool of its formal garden. The central dome reaches 73 metres; four slender minarets frame the composition. Every surface is covered with intricate inlaid stonework — pietra dura — of semi-precious stones including lapis lazuli, jade, crystal, turquoise and coral, forming flowers, calligraphy and geometric patterns of extraordinary delicacy. The colour appears to change throughout the day, from pinkish at dawn to brilliant white at noon and golden by moonlight.
history: The Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan built the Taj Mahal between 1632 and 1653 as a mausoleum for his beloved wife Mumtaz Mahal, who died during childbirth in 1631. He is said to have been so devastated by her death that his hair turned grey overnight. Over 20,000 workers and 1,000 elephants were employed in construction. Shah Jahan himself was later deposed by his own son Aurangzeb and spent the last eight years of his life imprisoned in Agra Fort, where he could gaze across the river at his wife's tomb. He was eventually buried beside her in 1666. The Taj Mahal was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1983.

name: Great Wall of China
description: The Great Wall of China is the longest man-made structure ever built — a series of fortifications, watchtowers, beacon towers and garrison stations that snake across mountains, deserts and plains for over 21,000 kilometres from the Bohai Sea in the east to the Gobi Desert in the west. Contrary to the popular myth, it is not visible from space with the naked eye, but it is an engineering marvel of extraordinary ambition. The wall was built to protect Chinese states and empires from nomadic invasions from the north, and also served as a trade and immigration control point along the Silk Road.
history: Construction began as early as the 7th century BC, when individual Chinese states built their own defensive walls. After Qin Shi Huang united China in 221 BC, he ordered the existing walls connected and extended — this first great wall was built largely by forced labour, including soldiers, peasants and prisoners, hundreds of thousands of whom died and were reportedly buried within the wall itself. The most famous and best-preserved sections visible today — including at Badaling near Beijing — were built during the Ming Dynasty between 1368 and 1644. The wall was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1987.

name: Machu Picchu
description: Machu Picchu is the most iconic and mysterious site of the ancient Inca civilisation — a 15th-century citadel perched at 2,430 metres on a ridge above the Urubamba River in the Andes, surrounded by cloud forest and dramatic mountain peaks. The site contains over 200 structures including temples, palaces, residences, terraced agricultural fields and astronomical observatories, all built without mortar using precisely cut stone that has withstood earthquakes for 500 years. The Intihuatana stone — a precisely carved ritual stone that serves as a solar clock and calendar — is the most sacred feature. The site was likely a royal estate and ceremonial centre rather than a military fortress.
history: Built around 1450 on the orders of the Inca emperor Pachacuti, Machu Picchu was occupied for only about 100 years before being abandoned, possibly due to Spanish conquest and smallpox epidemics. The site was unknown to the outside world until 1911, when American historian Hiram Bingham was led to it by a local farmer. His announcement caused a global sensation — the 'Lost City of the Incas' captured imaginations worldwide. UNESCO designated it a World Heritage Site in 1983, and it was named one of the New Seven Wonders of the World in 2007. Visitor numbers are now strictly controlled to protect the fragile site.

name: Petra
description: Petra is one of the world's most astonishing archaeological sites — a vast city carved directly from rose-red sandstone cliffs by the Nabataean civilisation over 2,000 years ago. The famous approach is through the Siq, a narrow slot canyon barely 3 metres wide and 1.2 kilometres long, which opens dramatically to reveal the Treasury (Al-Khazneh) — a 43-metre-high facade of extraordinary intricacy carved from the sheer rock face. Beyond lies an entire city: royal tombs, temples, a colonnaded street, a Roman amphitheatre, markets, baths, a monastery and thousands of cave dwellings, all hewn from the multicoloured stone.
history: The Nabataeans, an Arab nomadic people who became wealthy traders controlling the spice and silk routes, established Petra as their capital around the 4th century BC. At its peak it was home to 30,000 people. The Nabataeans developed a sophisticated water management system of dams, cisterns and channels that made a city possible in this arid desert. Petra was absorbed into the Roman Empire in 106 AD, and gradually declined after an earthquake in 363 AD damaged its water systems. The city was abandoned and forgotten by the Western world until Swiss explorer Johann Ludwig Burckhardt rediscovered it in 1812. It was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1985.

name: Angkor Wat
description: Angkor Wat is the largest religious monument in the world — a 12th-century temple complex covering 162 hectares, built by the Khmer Empire as a representation of Mount Meru, the home of the gods in Hindu cosmology. The temple's five towers, mirrored in the surrounding moat, are the symbol of Cambodia and appear on its national flag. Every surface is covered with some of the finest bas-relief sculpture in existence, depicting Hindu epics, celestial dancing apsaras, battle scenes and everyday Khmer life in extraordinary detail. At sunrise, the towers rise from the mist above their reflection in the moat in one of the world's great visual experiences.
history: Built by King Suryavarman II in the early 12th century as a Hindu temple dedicated to Vishnu, Angkor Wat was gradually converted to Buddhism in the 14th century and has been in continuous religious use ever since — making it the world's oldest living religious monument. The Khmer Empire, which built Angkor as its capital, was one of the greatest civilisations of Southeast Asia, stretching from Myanmar to Vietnam. After the empire's decline, the jungle reclaimed Angkor, and its temples lay largely forgotten by the outside world until French explorer Henri Mouhot described them in 1860. Major restoration work by UNESCO and international partners has been ongoing since the 1990s.

name: Great Pyramids of Giza
description: The Great Pyramids of Giza are the oldest and only surviving wonder of the original Seven Wonders of the Ancient World — and after 4,500 years, still the most awe-inspiring structures ever built by human hands. The Great Pyramid of Khufu, the largest, originally stood 146 metres tall and was the world's tallest man-made structure for over 3,800 years. Built with 2.3 million stone blocks weighing between 2.5 and 80 tonnes each, its construction remains one of history's great engineering mysteries. The complex also includes the Great Sphinx — a 73-metre-long statue with a lion's body and human face — and the smaller Pyramids of Khafre and Menkaure.
history: The pyramids were built as royal tombs during the Fourth Dynasty of the Old Kingdom, around 2580–2510 BC. Contrary to popular myth, they were not built by slaves but by paid workers — archaeological evidence including workers' villages, medical facilities and accounting records has confirmed this. The interior chambers, accessed by narrow passages, contained the pharaoh's mummified body, treasures and everything needed for the afterlife. All three pyramids were robbed of their contents in antiquity. The smooth white limestone casing stones that once covered the pyramids were stripped off during the Middle Ages to build Cairo's mosques and palaces. The site was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1979.

name: Stonehenge
description: Stonehenge is one of the world's most mysterious and recognisable prehistoric monuments — a circle of massive standing stones on the open Wiltshire plain, built and rebuilt over a period of 1,500 years between 3000 and 1500 BC. The largest stones, the sarsens, weigh up to 25 tonnes and were transported from Marlborough Downs, 32 kilometres away. The smaller bluestones originated in the Preseli Hills in Wales, 250 kilometres distant — an extraordinary logistical feat for Neolithic people. The monument is precisely aligned with the midsummer sunrise and midwinter sunset, confirming its use as a solar calendar and ceremonial site.
history: Stonehenge was built in several distinct phases over 1,500 years. The first phase, around 3000 BC, involved building the circular earthwork enclosure. The bluestones were erected around 2500 BC, followed by the massive sarsen trilithons. Who built it and exactly why remains debated, but it was almost certainly a site of religious ceremony, ancestor worship and astronomical observation. In the medieval period it was believed to have been built by the wizard Merlin. Scientific excavation from the 18th century onward gradually revealed its true age and origins. It was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1986 along with the surrounding Avebury stone circles.

name: Victoria Falls
description: Victoria Falls — known locally as Mosi-oa-Tunya, 'The Smoke That Thunders' — is one of the world's greatest natural spectacles: the Zambezi River, over a kilometre wide, plunges over a sheer 108-metre cliff in a continuous curtain of white water, creating a permanent cloud of spray and a roar audible from 40 kilometres away. The spray creates a permanent rainforest around the falls, a vivid green contrast to the surrounding dry savanna. At peak flood in April, over 500 million litres of water per minute plunge into the gorge below. The Devil's Pool, a natural infinity pool at the lip of the falls on the Zambian side, is one of the world's most thrilling swimming experiences.
history: The falls have been sacred to the local Kololo and Ndebele people for centuries. Scottish missionary and explorer David Livingstone became the first European to see them on 16 November 1855, arriving by canoe. He wrote that the scene was 'so lovely it must have been gazed upon by angels in their flight' and named them after Queen Victoria. The falls became a major tourist attraction after the Cape to Cairo Railway reached the Zimbabwean bank in 1905, when the Victoria Falls Bridge was built directly over the gorge. Both Victoria Falls and the surrounding national parks were inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1989.

name: Mont-Saint-Michel
description: Mont-Saint-Michel is one of France's most extraordinary architectural achievements — a medieval abbey, village and fortifications built atop a granite tidal island in the bay between Normandy and Brittany. At high tide, the sea surrounds the island completely, cutting it off from the mainland in an image of almost supernatural beauty. The Gothic abbey at the summit, with its soaring spires and flying buttresses, seems to grow organically from the rock. The surrounding bay has the highest tidal range in continental Europe — up to 15 metres — and the speed of the incoming tide has been compared to that of a galloping horse.
history: According to legend, the Archangel Michael appeared to the Bishop of Avranches in 708 AD and commanded him to build a church on the rock. Construction of the great abbey began in the 11th century and continued for 500 years, with each era adding its own architectural layer. During the Hundred Years' War, the abbey held out against English sieges for 30 years, becoming a symbol of French resistance. After the Revolution it was converted into a prison, housing over 14,000 inmates over 60 years. Restoration as a religious and heritage site began in 1863. It was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1979 and today receives over 2.5 million visitors a year.

name: Alhambra
description: The Alhambra is the greatest surviving example of Moorish architecture in the world — a palace and fortress complex of breathtaking beauty sitting on a forested hill above Granada in southern Spain. Its name means 'the red one' in Arabic, referring to the reddish hue of the hill's iron-rich soil. Inside, a series of royal palaces, gardens and courtyards creates a world of extraordinary refinement: the Court of the Lions with its famous fountain supported by 12 marble lions; the Hall of the Abencerrages with its astonishing stalactite ceiling; the Generalife gardens with their cooling water channels and rose-filled terraces. Every surface is covered with intricate geometric tilework, carved plasterwork and Arabic calligraphy.
history: The Alhambra's origins date to the 9th century, but the palatial complex was built primarily in the 13th and 14th centuries by the Nasrid Sultans of Granada — the last Muslim rulers of Al-Andalus. Boabdil, the last Nasrid sultan, surrendered Granada to the Catholic Monarchs Ferdinand and Isabella on 2 January 1492 — the same year Columbus set sail for the Americas. Legend says Boabdil wept as he rode away and his mother rebuked him: 'You weep like a woman for what you could not defend as a man.' The pass where this is said to have happened is still called El Suspiro del Moro — the Moor's Sigh. Washington Irving wrote his famous Tales of the Alhambra while living in the palace in 1829.

name: Chichen Itza
description: Chichen Itza was one of the largest and most powerful cities of the ancient Maya civilisation, flourishing between 600 and 1200 AD in the Yucatan Peninsula. Its most iconic structure is El Castillo (the Castle), also known as the Temple of Kukulcan — a 30-metre pyramid with four stairways of 91 steps each, plus a top platform, totalling 365 steps for the days of the year. At the spring and autumn equinoxes, the setting sun casts a shadow of a serpent undulating down the northern staircase, a phenomenon of astronomical precision that draws thousands of visitors twice a year. The Great Ball Court, the largest in Mesoamerica, hosted ritual ball games that may have ended in human sacrifice.
history: Chichen Itza was founded by the Maya around 600 AD and became one of the dominant political and religious centres of the Yucatan. Around 1000 AD, a Toltec warrior people from central Mexico appear to have invaded or intermarried with the ruling class, introducing new architectural styles and the feathered serpent god Quetzalcoatl (Kukulcan to the Maya). The city was largely abandoned by 1400 AD, possibly due to drought, warfare or political collapse. Spanish conquistadors arrived in the 1520s and described its temples as already in decline. Large-scale archaeological excavation began in the early 20th century. It was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1988 and named one of the New Seven Wonders of the World in 2007.

name: Robben Island
description: Robben Island sits in Table Bay 11 kilometres off Cape Town — a small, flat island that for centuries served as a place of exile, punishment and imprisonment. It is best known as the place where Nelson Mandela was held for 18 of his 27 years in prison — in a tiny cell, doing hard labour in the lime quarry, cut off from family and normal life. Today the island is a living museum, and the most powerful tours are led by former political prisoners who themselves served time here. From the ferry approaching the island, Table Mountain and the Cape Peninsula form a stunning backdrop to one of history's most resonant sites of resistance and redemption.
history: Robben Island's history as a place of confinement stretches back to the 17th century, when the Dutch East India Company (VOC) used it to exile African and Asian leaders who resisted colonial rule, including the king of the Khoikhoi. In the 19th century it housed a leper colony. During apartheid, from the 1960s, it became the most notorious political prison in South Africa. Nelson Mandela arrived in 1964, sentenced to life imprisonment for sabotage and conspiracy to overthrow the state. He and his comrades spent their years organising, studying and quietly educating their fellow prisoners. After release in 1990 and his election as South Africa's first democratic president in 1994, Mandela said Robben Island had taught him that 'the human spirit cannot be crushed.' It was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1999.

name: Palace of Versailles
description: The Palace of Versailles is the supreme expression of French royal power — a monument to absolute monarchy on a scale that stuns visitors even today. The palace contains 2,300 rooms including the dazzling Hall of Mirrors, a 73-metre gallery lined with 357 mirrors reflecting the light of 20,000 candles, where the Treaty of Versailles ending World War I was signed in 1919. The formal gardens extend for 800 hectares, designed by André Le Nôtre with miles of symmetrical parterres, fountains, canals and statuary. At its height, the palace housed the entire French royal court and government — some 20,000 people lived within its walls.
history: Louis XIII built a small hunting lodge here in 1623. His son Louis XIV, the Sun King, transformed it from 1661 into the greatest palace in Europe, moving the French court and government permanently to Versailles in 1682. Every detail was designed to project royal power and keep the nobility busy with court rituals rather than political intrigue. The palace was the centre of French political life for over a century until the Revolution of 1789, when Louis XVI and Marie Antoinette were forced by a mob to return to Paris — the last time a French monarch lived at Versailles. It was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1979.

name: Dubrovnik Old City
description: Dubrovnik's Old City is one of the most perfectly preserved medieval urban ensembles in the world — a compact city of baroque churches, Renaissance palaces, limestone-paved streets and terracotta rooftops, enclosed within 1,940 metres of massive stone walls. The city walls, up to 6 metres thick and 25 metres high, can be walked in their entirety, offering extraordinary views over the Adriatic Sea and the city's rooftops. Known as the 'Pearl of the Adriatic', Dubrovnik was for centuries an independent maritime republic that rivalled Venice in wealth and sophistication, with a tradition of liberating its own slaves as early as 1418.
history: Founded in the 7th century by refugees from the Roman city of Epidaurum, Dubrovnik (then known as Ragusa) gradually developed into one of the most important maritime trading powers in the Mediterranean. At its peak in the 15th and 16th centuries, the Republic of Ragusa maintained diplomatic relations with most of the world's powers and had an ambassador in London before England had one there. A catastrophic earthquake in 1667 killed 5,000 people and destroyed much of the city; the baroque architecture visible today was largely built during the reconstruction. During the Croatian War of Independence in 1991–92, the city was besieged and shelled — 68% of buildings in the old town were hit. Extraordinary restoration work has made it almost impossible to see the damage today.

name: Cappadocia (Göreme)
description: Cappadocia is one of the world's most surreal landscapes — a dreamlike region of central Anatolia where millions of years of volcanic eruption, erosion and wind have sculpted the soft tuff rock into thousands of extraordinary formations called fairy chimneys: tall, thin cones of rock with harder caps balanced on top. Into this strange landscape, early Christian communities carved an astonishing civilisation: hundreds of rock-cut churches decorated with Byzantine frescoes, entire underground cities capable of housing 20,000 people, and cave dwellings still inhabited within living memory. At dawn, hundreds of hot air balloons rise above the valleys in a spectacle unique to this landscape.
history: The volcanic eruptions of Mount Erciyes and Hasan Dağ over ten million years ago blanketed the Cappadocian plateau in deep layers of soft tuff and harder basalt. As the softer rock eroded, the harder caps were left perched on pinnacles — the fairy chimneys. Humans have inhabited caves in this landscape for at least 4,000 years. Early Christians fleeing Roman persecution carved underground cities here as early as the 2nd century AD; the city of Derinkuyu descends 18 storeys and could shelter 20,000 people complete with stables, churches, wine presses and ventilation shafts. The Göreme Open Air Museum, with its 10th-century rock-cut churches, was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1985.

name: Borobudur Temple
description: Borobudur is the world's largest Buddhist temple and one of the greatest monuments of any civilisation — a 9th-century stone mandala rising nine terraced levels from the Kedu Plain, topped with 72 stupas each containing a statue of the seated Buddha. The entire structure is a representation of the Buddhist cosmological universe, with the base representing the world of desire, the middle levels the world of forms, and the summit the formless world of Nirvana. The walls and balustrades are decorated with 2,672 individual relief panels depicting the life of the Buddha and Buddhist teachings — the world's largest and most complete ensemble of Buddhist reliefs.
history: Borobudur was built during the Sailendra Dynasty between 750 and 850 AD, during a golden age of Javanese civilisation. Construction required 55,000 cubic metres of stone and the labour of tens of thousands of workers over 75 years. Within two centuries of its completion, the temple was mysteriously abandoned — possibly due to a volcanic eruption or a shift of political power to East Java — and gradually buried under volcanic ash and jungle. It remained hidden for nearly a thousand years. Dutch colonial engineer Herman Cornelius Hartmann rediscovered it in 1814 under instructions from Thomas Stamford Raffles. A massive UNESCO restoration project between 1975 and 1982, costing 25 million dollars, restored it to its current state.

name: Meteora Monasteries
description: Meteora — meaning 'suspended in the air' — is one of the most visually dramatic religious sites in the world: six functioning Eastern Orthodox monasteries perched atop near-vertical pillars of rock rising up to 400 metres from the plain of Thessaly. The monasteries, built from the 14th century onward, were accessible originally only by rope ladders and nets that had to be hauled up by the monks — an effective deterrent to both bandits and worldly temptation. Today stairs are carved into the rock. The monastic frescoes inside are masterpieces of Byzantine art. The landscape of towering rock formations, ancient forests and monasteries suspended against the sky is utterly unlike anywhere else on Earth.
history: Hermit monks first sought solitude on the rocks of Meteora as early as the 11th century, living in caves and hollows. The first monastery, the Great Meteoron, was founded around 1356 by the monk Athanasios Koinovitis, who hauled construction materials up the rock face by net and rope. At the movement's peak in the 16th century, 24 monasteries clung to the rocks. Wars, earthquakes and the difficulty of the life gradually reduced the number; today six remain active and inhabited by monks and nuns. The site was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1988, recognised both for its natural geological formations and its outstanding cultural and spiritual heritage.

name: Easter Island (Rapa Nui)
description: Easter Island — Rapa Nui in the indigenous language — is one of the most remote and enigmatic inhabited places on Earth: a tiny volcanic island in the South Pacific, 3,700 kilometres from the Chilean coast, home to 900 monumental stone statues called moai. The moai, carved from volcanic tuff, range from 2 to 10 metres tall and weigh up to 82 tonnes; the largest ever attempted stands unfinished in its quarry at 21 metres. Each statue has an elongated head, prominent brow ridges and a compressed nose — and they were originally topped with red stone topknots and had inlaid coral-and-obsidian eyes that gave them an almost living gaze. How the islanders moved these massive statues across the island remains a subject of research and debate.
history: Polynesian voyagers settled Rapa Nui around 300–1200 AD, arriving in ocean-going canoes from the Marquesas Islands. At its peak, a population of perhaps 15,000 people lived on the island, organised into competing clans who commissioned the moai as representations of deified ancestors. The environmental collapse of the island — caused by deforestation to move the statues and other demands — contributed to a catastrophic population decline before European contact. Dutch explorer Jacob Roggeveen arrived on Easter Sunday 1722, giving the island its European name. Further devastation followed: Peruvian slave raids in 1862 carried off nearly half the surviving population. The remaining moai were toppled during inter-clan warfare in the late 18th century. The site was inscribed as UNESCO World Heritage in 1995.

name: Bwindi Impenetrable National Park
description: Bwindi Impenetrable National Park is a UNESCO World Heritage Site in southwestern Uganda covering 331 km² of ancient rainforest. Home to nearly half the world's remaining mountain gorillas, Bwindi is one of Africa's most biodiverse ecosystems. The forest has been continuously inhabited by humans for over 34,000 years and contains more than 200 tree species, 120 mammal species, and 350 bird species. Gorilla trekking permits make this one of the most sought-after wildlife destinations on the continent, with permit revenues directly funding conservation and local communities.
history: The forest was gazetted as a Crown Forest Reserve in 1932 and upgraded to a national park in 1991. UNESCO inscribed it as a World Heritage Site the same year. The Batwa pygmy people, who lived in the forest for millennia, were displaced upon its protection — an ongoing point of reconciliation between conservation and indigenous rights that continues to shape park governance today. Community revenue-sharing programmes now return 20% of park fees to local villages.

name: Virunga National Park
description: Africa's oldest national park, Virunga spans 7,800 km² in eastern DRC along the borders with Rwanda and Uganda. It shelters the critically endangered mountain gorilla, the rare okapi, and hundreds of hippos. The park contains five volcano massifs including the active Nyiragongo, whose lava lake is one of the world's largest. Despite decades of armed conflict, Virunga remains one of the continent's most ecologically important protected areas and a symbol of conservation under extraordinary adversity.
history: Founded as Albert National Park by King Albert I of Belgium in 1925, making it Africa's first national park. Renamed Virunga after Congolese independence in 1960. UNESCO listed it as a World Heritage Site in 1979 and added it to the danger list in 1994. Over 200 rangers have been killed protecting its wildlife in recent decades — the park's conservation story is one of extraordinary human courage in the face of conflict and resource exploitation.

name: Daintree National Park
description: The Daintree is the world's oldest continuously surviving tropical rainforest at an estimated 180 million years old, located in Queensland, Australia. Covering over 1,200 km², it is a UNESCO World Heritage Site sheltering over 430 bird species, 12,000 insect species, and rare primitive flowering plants that predate the separation of Gondwana. Mossman Gorge and Cape Tribulation draw visitors to pristine rivers, beaches and ancient canopy walks through the oldest living forest on Earth.
history: The Daintree was logged commercially until the 1980s when a landmark conservation campaign led to its protection. After years of conflict between timber interests and conservationists, the Wet Tropics was listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1988. The traditional custodians, the Kuku Yalanji people, have lived in the rainforest for tens of thousands of years and continue to co-manage cultural sites within the park boundaries as recognised traditional owners.

name: Jiuzhaigou Valley
description: Jiuzhaigou Valley in Sichuan Province is renowned for its series of vibrant turquoise, emerald and sapphire lakes connected by spectacular waterfalls. The valley stretches over 720 km² and its multi-hued mineral lakes, snow-capped peaks and ancient forests make it one of China's most photographed landscapes. Giant pandas and Sichuan takins roam the upper forest zones in one of Asia's most visually spectacular and scientifically important protected areas.
history: The valley was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1992 and a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve in 1997. A 7.0 magnitude earthquake in August 2017 caused significant damage to the site's lakes and waterfalls, forcing a multi-year closure for ecological recovery. The park reopened to limited visitors in 2019 with strict capacity controls to prevent further degradation of its unique hydrology and limestone lake chemistry.

name: Olympic National Park
description: Olympic National Park in Washington State encompasses three distinct ecosystems: glacier-capped mountains, temperate rainforest, and 100 km of wild Pacific coastline. The Hoh Rain Forest receives up to 4 metres of rainfall annually, creating cathedral groves of Sitka spruce and Douglas fir draped in moss. Olympic shelters several species found nowhere else on Earth, including the Olympic marmot, in a landscape of extraordinary ecological isolation and diversity preserved intact for future generations.
history: First protected as Olympic Forest Reserve in 1897, upgraded to a national monument in 1909 to protect Roosevelt elk, and elevated to a national park in 1938. Designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1981. The Quinault, Quileute, Hoh and Makah tribes have maintained connections to this land throughout its modern conservation history, and their treaty rights — including fishing rights on park rivers — remain legally protected and actively exercised within the park boundaries.

name: Kakadu National Park
description: Kakadu in Australia's Northern Territory is a dual UNESCO World Heritage Site recognised for both outstanding natural values and an extraordinary concentration of Aboriginal rock art. At 19,804 km² it is Australia's largest national park, sheltering one third of Australia's bird species and over 2,000 plant species. The Bininj/Mungguy people have lived in Kakadu continuously for at least 65,000 years — one of the longest unbroken cultural traditions on Earth — making this as much a living cultural landscape as a wilderness park.
history: Kakadu was listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in three stages between 1981 and 1992, among the first sites to receive dual natural and cultural listing. The park is co-managed by traditional Aboriginal owners and Parks Australia — a model that has since influenced protected area management globally. Rock art galleries at Nourlangie and Ubirr document tens of thousands of years of human occupation, ceremonial life, and changes in the animals and environment over geological time.

name: Białowieża Forest
description: Białowieża Forest is Europe's last primeval lowland forest, straddling the border between Poland and Belarus. A UNESCO World Heritage Site, it shelters the world's largest surviving herd of European bison, reintroduced after near-extinction in the wild. Ancient oaks over 500 years old, moss-covered deadwood and a 600-year royal hunting tradition make this forest uniquely preserved — the largest untouched lowland forest in Europe, a refuge for species found nowhere else on the continent.
history: The forest was a royal hunting ground for Polish and Lithuanian kings from the 15th century, inadvertently preserving it from commercial logging. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1979. The European bison was hunted to extinction in the wild by 1927; captive breeding restored the population and wild herds now exceed 6,000 individuals across Europe. Controversial logging operations in 2017 triggered European Court of Justice intervention and an emergency halt order.
