name: Denali National Park
description: Denali National Park in Alaska surrounds the highest peak in North America — Denali at 6,190 m. Covering 24,585 km² of subarctic wilderness, grizzly bears, Dall sheep, wolves, moose and caribou roam largely undisturbed across a vast expanse of tundra and boreal forest. A single unpaved road traverses the park, accessible only by designated buses, keeping visitor numbers low and wildlife encounters extraordinary against a backdrop of one of the world's great mountain massifs.
history: Established in 1917 as Mount McKinley National Park and renamed Denali in 1980 to restore the indigenous Athabascan name. The Athabascan people have used the region for hunting and gathering for over 11,000 years. The first confirmed summit in 1913 was made by Hudson Stuck accompanied by indigenous guide Walter Harper, believed to be the first person to actually stand on the highest point of the massif, though his contribution was long overlooked in historical accounts.

name: Sundarbans National Park
description: The Sundarbans is the world's largest mangrove forest, shared between India and Bangladesh. The Indian national park covers 1,330 km² and shelters the Bengal tiger, fishing cats, Irrawaddy dolphins, estuarine crocodiles and over 260 bird species. The mangrove ecosystem provides critical coastal protection for tens of millions of people in the Ganges–Brahmaputra delta — making it as important for human survival as for wildlife conservation and demonstrating the indivisibility of ecological and human wellbeing.
history: Declared a wildlife sanctuary in 1977 and a national park in 1984. UNESCO inscribed it as a World Heritage Site in 1987. Fishing and honey-gathering communities have inhabited the forest for centuries. Climate change is the park's most pressing threat as rising sea levels reduce mangrove area and saltwater intrusion alters vegetation communities — the Sundarbans is one of the most climate-vulnerable UNESCO sites in the world.

name: Corcovado National Park
description: Corcovado on Costa Rica's Osa Peninsula is described by National Geographic as the most biologically intense place on Earth. Its 424 km² contain 13 distinct ecosystems sheltering 2.5% of the world's biodiversity including jaguars, tapirs, giant anteaters, four monkey species and scarlet macaws. The park has no roads and is accessible only on foot or by boat, preserving an authenticity rare in Central American conservation. All visitors must be accompanied by a licensed guide, ensuring both ecological protection and local employment.
history: Established in 1975 to halt gold mining and logging rapidly destroying the Osa Peninsula. Corcovado became a centrepiece of Costa Rica's ecotourism model, transforming the country from one of the world's highest deforestation rates in the 1970s to a nation that has doubled its forest cover. The park's strict guide requirement provides sustainable livelihoods for local communities while ensuring that wildlife encounters remain authentic and minimally intrusive.

name: Manu National Park
description: Manu National Park in the Amazon basin of southeastern Peru is one of the most biodiverse protected areas on Earth, sheltering over 1,000 bird species, 200 mammal species and an estimated 15,000 plant species within its 17,163 km². The park spans five altitude zones from Andean grassland at 4,200 m to lowland Amazonian rainforest. Voluntary-isolation indigenous communities maintain no contact with the outside world, and the park's core zone has never been commercially exploited in recorded history.
history: Established as a national park in 1973 and inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1987. Its core zone has never been logged, making it a critical scientific baseline for Amazonian biodiversity research and carbon accounting. The park's remoteness — accessible only by river or a single mountain road — has protected it from the agricultural expansion devastating surrounding Amazon forests across Peru and Brazil.

name: Kilimanjaro National Park
description: Kilimanjaro National Park protects Africa's highest peak and the world's tallest freestanding mountain at 5,895 m. Five distinct vegetation zones — farmland, montane forest, heath, alpine desert and arctic summit — create a vertical journey through ecosystems found nowhere else in East Africa. Over 30,000 climbers attempt the summit annually via routes including Marangu, Machame and Lemosho. The retreating glaciers on Kilimanjaro's summit are a vivid and measurable symbol of climate change impacts.
history: First summited by Europeans in 1889 by German geologist Hans Meyer and Austrian mountaineer Ludwig Purtscheller. The Chagga people have farmed the fertile lower slopes for centuries. The national park was established in 1973 and inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1987. Since 1912 the mountain has lost over 80% of its ice cap, making Kilimanjaro one of the world's most visible and scientifically documented examples of accelerating glacial retreat.

name: Iguazú National Park
description: Iguazú National Park protects the Iguazú Falls — 275 individual waterfalls stretching 2.7 km along the Iguazú River. The Devil's Throat plunges 82 metres in a thunderous curtain of water and mist visible from kilometres away. The surrounding subtropical rainforest shelters jaguars, tapirs, giant otters and over 400 bird species. A UNESCO World Heritage Site receiving over 1.5 million visitors annually, sharing the falls system with Brazil's matching Iguaçu National Park directly across the river.
history: First seen by European explorer Álvar Núñez Cabeza de Vaca in 1541. The national park was established in 1934 and inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1984. The Guaraní people inhabited the region for centuries before European contact. Eleanor Roosevelt, visiting in 1959, reportedly exclaimed 'Poor Niagara!' — cementing Iguazú's international reputation as the world's most spectacular waterfall system.

name: Volcanoes National Park
description: Volcanoes National Park in northwest Rwanda is the most accessible sanctuary for mountain gorillas in the world, home to around 300 habituated individuals that can be visited on trekking permits. The park protects the Rwandan sector of the Virunga volcano chain and was made famous by Dian Fossey's landmark gorilla research from 1967. Golden monkeys, forest elephants and buffalo also inhabit its bamboo and Hagenia forest zones, making it one of Africa's premier wildlife destinations.
history: Established in 1925 as part of Albert National Park — one of Africa's first protected areas. Dian Fossey established the Karisoke Research Centre within the park in 1967. Her work and murder in 1985 drew global attention to gorilla conservation. Today gorilla tourism generates over US$17 million annually and is Rwanda's top foreign exchange earner — a model showing that conservation and economic development can be successfully and sustainably aligned.

name: Zhangjiajie National Forest Park
description: Zhangjiajie in Hunan Province is China's first national forest park, famous for its towering quartzite sandstone pillars that inspired the floating Hallelujah Mountains in the film Avatar. Over 3,000 columns rise from the forest floor, many exceeding 200 metres, within a landscape found nowhere else on Earth. The world's longest passenger cable car at 7.5 km and a glass-floored bridge suspended over a 300-metre chasm draw millions of visitors to this extraordinary geological formation annually.
history: Established as China's first national forest park in 1982. The wider Wulingyuan area was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1992 for its extraordinary concentration of sandstone formations unique in the world. The region has been home to the Tujia and Miao ethnic minorities for over a thousand years. The Avatar connection has dramatically increased visitor numbers, requiring strict daily capacity management to protect the fragile ecosystem beneath the famous columns.

name: Patagonia National Park
description: Patagonia National Park in Chilean Patagonia protects wild steppe, turquoise lakes and mountains in one of the world's last great wilderness regions. Guanacos, pumas, Andean condors and the endangered huemul deer roam its open landscapes. Created through a historic land donation by Kristine Tompkins, it forms part of the Route of Parks — a chain of 17 national parks stretching 2,800 km through Chilean Patagonia forming one of the world's great conservation corridors.
history: Established progressively from 2014 through donations by Tompkins Conservation and expansion by the Chilean government, reaching its current scale of over 700,000 hectares in 2018. One of the largest private land-to-park donations in history, this demonstrated that private capital could catalyse large-scale national park creation and transform former sheep-farming estancias into permanent protected wilderness open to all Chileans and international visitors.

name: Snowdonia National Park
description: Snowdonia (Eryri in Welsh) is Wales's largest and oldest national park covering 2,142 km² of rugged mountain terrain in northwest Wales. Snowdon at 1,085 m is the highest peak in England and Wales. Over 60% of park residents speak Welsh as a first language, making it a living centre of Welsh language and culture. Over 4 million visitors annually hike, climb and ride the historic mountain railway to the summit of this iconic Welsh landmark.
history: Designated as a national park in 1951, the third in the UK. Settled since Mesolithic times, with Bronze Age cairns, Iron Age hill forts and Roman roads visible across the landscape. The region's slate quarries at Dinorwig and Penrhyn powered the British industrial revolution, employing tens of thousands of Welsh quarrymen. Their remarkable terraced quarry landscape was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2021 for its outstanding testimony to industrial heritage.

name: Rwenzori Mountains National Park
description: The legendary Mountains of the Moon contain Africa's third-highest peak, Mt Stanley at 5,109 m. The national park covers 996 km² of afroalpine moorland, glaciers and equatorial rainforest. Giant heathers, lobelias and groundsels grow to tree size in otherworldly high-altitude vegetation zones, creating a prehistoric landscape sheltering chimpanzees, colobus monkeys, forest elephants and the rare Rwenzori turaco found nowhere else on Earth.
history: First documented by Henry Morton Stanley in 1888, though the Bakonzo people had inhabited the foothills for millennia. The park was gazetted in 1991 and inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1994. The range's glaciers are among the most studied evidence of climate change in Africa — since 1906 the glaciers have lost approximately 82% of their area and are projected to disappear entirely by 2030, leaving the Mountains of the Moon permanently without ice.

name: Great Smoky Mountains National Park
description: The Great Smoky Mountains on the Tennessee–North Carolina border is the most visited national park in the United States, welcoming over 12 million visitors annually. A UNESCO World Heritage Site and International Biosphere Reserve, it shelters over 19,000 documented species including more tree species than all of northern Europe. Ancient Appalachian forests of oak, hickory and tulip poplar blaze with extraordinary colour in autumn, drawing visitors from across North America and beyond.
history: Established in 1934 after a campaign to preserve heavily logged Appalachian forests. Unlike most US national parks, the land was purchased from private owners — requiring fundraising from states, local governments and John D. Rockefeller Jr. The Cherokee people, forcibly removed in the 1830s Trail of Tears, maintain a sovereign reservation adjacent to the park. The Qualla Boundary is now an important cultural tourism destination celebrating Cherokee heritage and sovereignty.

name: Okavango Delta
description: The Okavango Delta is a unique inland delta — one of the world's largest — where the Okavango River fans into the Kalahari Desert, creating a vast wetland that supports extraordinary wildlife. This UNESCO World Heritage Site covers 20,000 km² of permanent and seasonal wetlands sheltering Africa's Big Five plus African wild dogs and cheetahs. The delta changes shape seasonally as floodwaters pulse in from Angola, creating an ever-shifting mosaic of channels, islands and floodplains of breathtaking and constantly changing beauty.
history: Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2014, described by the committee as 'one of the very few large scale alluvial fans in the world without an outlet to the sea.' The San people have lived in and around the delta for tens of thousands of years. Modern conservation relies heavily on community-based natural resource management, with local villages operating as primary custodians of wildlife concessions — generating income while protecting the ecosystem that sustains them.

name: Namib-Naukluft National Park
description: Namib-Naukluft is the largest game park in Africa at 49,768 km², covering the ancient Namib Desert — one of the oldest deserts on Earth. Iconic red sand dunes at Sossusvlei rise over 300 metres among the highest in the world, while the Deadvlei pan with its bleached clay and skeletal camelthorn trees is a photographer's icon. Oryx, springbok, ostriches and desert-adapted elephants survive in one of Earth's most extreme environments through remarkable evolutionary adaptation.
history: The Namib Desert is estimated to have been arid for 55–80 million years — Earth's oldest desert. The park was established in 1907 by German colonial authorities. The Topnaar Nama people have inhabited the Kuiseb River valley for centuries, harvesting !nara melons that sustain both humans and wildlife in an environment that would otherwise seem too harsh for any sustained human habitation or culture.

name: Bavarian Forest National Park
description: Germany's first national park, established in 1970 along the Czech border, forms part of Central Europe's largest contiguous protected forest together with neighbouring Šumava National Park. The park's strict non-intervention philosophy — fallen trees left to decay, bark beetle allowed its natural cycle — has been vindicated by studies showing its dead-wood forests support significantly greater biodiversity than managed forests. Eurasian lynx, returned naturally, now roam across national borders.
history: Established in 1970 despite fierce local opposition, the park's 'let nature be nature' management remained controversial for decades before scientific vindication transformed public attitudes. Eurasian lynx were reintroduced in 1970 and their population has since spread into the Czech Republic, Austria and Switzerland — a rare example of a large predator re-establishing itself across multiple national borders as a direct result of a single national park programme.

name: Blyde River Canyon Nature Reserve
description: The Blyde River Canyon in Mpumalanga is Africa's largest green canyon at 26 km long and 800 m deep, carved by the Blyde River through red sandstone over millions of years. The Three Rondavels rock formation, Bourke's Luck Potholes and God's Window viewpoint are iconic landmarks. The Nature Reserve protects one of southern Africa's most species-rich areas, including hippos, crocodiles and over 1,000 plant species along the canyon floor in a landscape of extraordinary geological drama and biological diversity.
history: The Blyde River Canyon Nature Reserve was proclaimed in 1975. The canyon sits at the escarpment where the Highveld meets the Lowveld — a dramatic geological boundary creating one of southern Africa's most biodiverse zones. The area forms part of the Kruger to Canyons UNESCO Biosphere Reserve, one of the world's largest biosphere reserves, encompassing Kruger National Park and adjacent private game reserves in a single integrated conservation landscape.

name: Lalibela Rock-Hewn Churches
description: Lalibela in northern Ethiopia contains eleven medieval monolithic churches carved directly into red volcanic rock in the 12th and 13th centuries during the reign of King Lalibela. Connected by tunnels and trenches, they represent one of the greatest architectural achievements of the medieval world. The Church of Bete Giyorgis — carved into a pit and approached from above — is the masterpiece of the complex. Lalibela was built as a 'New Jerusalem' when Muslim conquests cut Christian pilgrimage routes to the Holy Land.
history: Built under King Lalibela of the Zagwe dynasty around 1200 AD, reportedly constructed with angelic assistance according to Ethiopian Orthodox tradition. UNESCO inscribed Lalibela as a World Heritage Site in 1978. The site remains an active place of worship and pilgrimage — Ethiopian Christmas draws tens of thousands of white-robed pilgrims for celebrations that have continued unbroken for eight centuries, making Lalibela both an archaeological and living religious treasure.
photo_author_orig: The original uploader was Pzbinden7 at German Wikipedia.

name: Timbuktu
description: Timbuktu in Mali was for centuries the intellectual and spiritual capital of Islamic Africa, home to three great mosques and over 150 Quranic schools at its 15th-century peak. The Sankore mosque served as a university with up to 25,000 students studying astronomy, mathematics and theology. The city's 700,000 surviving manuscripts represent one of the world's great collections of medieval scholarship, preserved in family libraries that continued to guard them through centuries of invasion and political upheaval.
history: At its peak under the Mali and Songhai Empires in the 14th–16th centuries, Timbuktu had a population of around 100,000. UNESCO inscribed it as a World Heritage Site in 1988. In 2012 Islamist militants deliberately destroyed ancient mausoleums and shrines — an act prosecuted by the International Criminal Court as a war crime, the first successful prosecution for deliberate destruction of cultural heritage in the history of international law.

name: Abu Simbel Temples
description: The temples of Abu Simbel were carved into mountainside by Pharaoh Ramesses II around 1264 BC. The Great Temple features four colossal 20-metre statues at its entrance and is aligned so that twice a year — on 22 February and 22 October — sunlight penetrates 60 metres to illuminate three of the four inner sanctum statues, leaving only the god of darkness in perpetual shadow. This astronomical precision demonstrates the extraordinary engineering and scientific knowledge of ancient Egyptian builders.
history: Rediscovered buried under sand by Swiss explorer Johann Ludwig Burckhardt in 1813. In the 1960s, the construction of the Aswan High Dam threatened to submerge them under Lake Nasser. UNESCO coordinated an international engineering effort to cut the temples into 20-tonne blocks and reassemble them 65 metres higher — a feat of salvage archaeology never attempted before or since, costing US$80 million and involving 50 countries in one of history's greatest acts of cultural preservation.

name: Hagia Sophia, Istanbul
description: The Hagia Sophia is one of the greatest architectural achievements in history — its massive dome, floating on a ring of windows, was the world's largest for nearly a thousand years after completion in 537 AD. It has served as a cathedral, mosque, museum, and since 2020 a mosque again. The surrounding peninsula also contains the Topkapı Palace, Byzantine Hippodrome and Blue Mosque in one of the world's densest concentrations of world-historical monuments spanning three great empires.
history: Commissioned by Byzantine Emperor Justinian I and completed in 537 AD. Ottoman Sultan Mehmed II converted it to a mosque in 1453 after his conquest of Constantinople. Atatürk made it a museum in 1934 as a symbol of secular Turkey. UNESCO inscribed the Historic Areas of Istanbul in 1985. The 2020 reconversion to a mosque generated international controversy, with UNESCO formally expressing concern about its impact on the site's Outstanding Universal Value and accessibility to all people.

name: Lascaux Cave Paintings
description: The Lascaux Cave complex in the Dordogne contains Palaeolithic cave paintings estimated at 17,000 years old — among the finest examples of prehistoric art ever discovered. The Hall of the Bulls alone contains paintings of aurochs, deer and horses over 5 metres long, created with ochre and mineral pigments. Discovered in 1940 by four teenagers following their dog through a hole in the ground, the paintings' sophistication and perspective challenge long-held assumptions about the cognitive abilities of early modern humans.
history: The cave was opened to the public after World War II but closed in 1963 when carbon dioxide from visitors' breath caused algae and fungal growth to damage the paintings. A replica cave, Lascaux II, opened nearby in 1983, and the more complete Lascaux IV opened in 2016. UNESCO inscribed the Vézère Valley prehistoric sites in 1979. The original cave remains closed to all but a small number of scientists granted special access under strictly controlled conditions.

name: Canterbury Cathedral
description: Canterbury Cathedral is the mother church of the Anglican Communion and one of Europe's finest examples of Gothic architecture. The spiritual heart of English Christianity since St Augustine arrived in 597 AD, it also contains St Augustine's Abbey and St Martin's Church — the oldest parish church still in use in England. Thomas Becket was murdered here in 1170, creating one of medieval Europe's most important pilgrimage destinations and inspiring Chaucer's Canterbury Tales.
history: Construction of the current cathedral began in 1070 following the Norman Conquest and expanded over centuries in Romanesque, Early English and Perpendicular Gothic styles. Becket's murder made Canterbury England's foremost medieval pilgrimage destination. UNESCO inscribed the site in 1988. The cathedral survived World War II Baedeker Blitz bombing relatively intact, though surrounding streets were devastated in raids specifically targeting England's historic cathedral cities as acts of cultural warfare.

name: Pompeii and Herculaneum
description: Two Roman cities buried by Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD and preserved intact under volcanic ash for nearly 1,700 years. Pompeii's streets, homes, bakeries, theatres, fast-food counters and brothels offer the most complete picture of everyday Roman life anywhere in existence. Plaster casts of victims frozen in their final moments and 2,000-year-old graffiti create an intimacy with the ancient world unmatched at any other archaeological site on Earth, drawing over 3.5 million visitors annually.
history: The eruption of Vesuvius on 24 August 79 AD buried both cities within hours. Pompeii was rediscovered in 1748 and systematic excavation began under Charles III of Spain. These excavations established the practice of stratigraphic archaeological excavation — Pompeii essentially created modern archaeology as a scientific discipline. UNESCO inscribed the Archaeological Areas of Pompeii, Herculaneum and Torre Annunziata as a World Heritage Site in 1997.

name: Great Barrier Reef
description: The Great Barrier Reef is the world's largest coral reef system, stretching 2,300 km along Australia's Queensland coast — larger than the United Kingdom and visible from space. The world's biggest structure made by living organisms, it shelters over 1,500 fish species, 4,000 mollusc species and six sea turtle species. A UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1981, it now appears on UNESCO's 'In Danger' list due to repeated mass bleaching events caused by rising ocean temperatures from climate change.
history: Inhabited by Aboriginal Australians and Torres Strait Islanders for at least 60,000 years. James Cook ran aground on the reef in 1770. UNESCO inscribed it in 1981. Mass bleaching events in 1998, 2002, 2016, 2017, 2020 and 2022 have each killed large portions of the reef, placing its long-term survival under serious scientific concern and making it a central and urgent symbol of the global climate change crisis and the consequences of inaction.
photo_author_orig: U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service - Pacific Region's Photo credit: Jim Maragos/U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service

name: Kathmandu Valley
description: Kathmandu Valley contains seven UNESCO World Heritage monument zones including Pashupatinath Temple — one of Hinduism's holiest sites — the Boudhanath stupa (one of the world's largest Buddhist stupas), and the medieval royal palace complexes of Kathmandu, Patan and Bhaktapur Durbar Squares. A crossroads of Buddhist and Hindu civilisations for over 2,000 years, the valley produces distinctive Newari art and architecture of extraordinary refinement alongside living heritage traditions that continue entirely uninterrupted today.
history: Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1979. The 2015 Gorkha earthquake caused widespread destruction to historic monuments including collapsed towers and severely damaged Durbar Square temples. International restoration efforts mobilised rapidly, but reconstruction has been complex, balancing seismic safety with authenticity. Many monuments remain under restoration a decade later, supported by international conservation agencies and bilateral donor governments providing technical and financial assistance.

name: Hiroshima Peace Memorial
description: The Hiroshima Peace Memorial (Genbaku Dome) is the skeletal ruin of a building left standing near the hypocentre of the world's first atomic bomb detonation on 6 August 1945. The only structure to survive near the blast, it was preserved as a permanent reminder of nuclear devastation and a call for universal disarmament. UNESCO inscribed it as a World Heritage Site in 1996. Over a million visitors annually come to reflect at this most solemn of world monuments in the heart of a rebuilt and thriving city.
history: The atomic bomb detonated 600 metres above the city at 8:15 am on 6 August 1945, killing an estimated 80,000 people instantly and perhaps 140,000 by the end of the year. City authorities preserved the Genbaku Dome in 1966 despite calls to demolish it. UNESCO's inscription in 1996 was controversially opposed by the United States and China. The surrounding Peace Memorial Park is a major global centre of atomic bomb survivor testimony and anti-nuclear advocacy.

name: Ha Long Bay
description: Ha Long Bay in northeast Vietnam contains nearly 2,000 limestone karst islands rising from the emerald Gulf of Tonkin. Vertical rock faces, caves and hidden lagoons create a seascape of extraordinary beauty spanning 1,553 km². Traditional fishing communities live on floating villages within the bay, maintaining customs and livelihoods continued for generations in this otherworldly environment. It is Vietnam's most recognisable natural attraction and one of Southeast Asia's most photographed and visited landscapes.
history: Ha Long Bay was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1994 for its outstanding scenic beauty. The name means 'descending dragon' in Vietnamese — legend holds that a dragon family descended to protect Vietnam from invaders, their tails creating the islands and channels. The bay saw significant naval action during the Vietnam War. Rapidly expanding cruise tourism has raised serious environmental concerns about waste management and ecological degradation in the enclosed bay system.

name: Kyoto Historic Monuments
description: Kyoto served as Japan's imperial capital for over a thousand years and contains a concentration of UNESCO-listed temples, shrines and gardens unmatched in East Asia. The 17 World Heritage locations include the golden Kinkaku-ji, Ryoan-ji's famous rock garden, the Fushimi Inari shrine with its thousands of vermilion torii gates, and the Nijo Castle of the Tokugawa shoguns. Kyoto was deliberately spared atomic bombing in World War II, preserving this irreplaceable cultural heritage for all humanity.
history: Founded in 794 AD as Heian-kyo, Kyoto was Japan's imperial capital until 1869. The UNESCO inscription in 1994 covers 17 constituent properties representing the full range of Japanese Buddhist and Shinto traditions alongside feudal military architecture. US Secretary of War Henry Stimson successfully argued against including Kyoto on the atomic bomb target list, having visited the city and recognising its extraordinary cultural significance — one of history's most consequential aesthetic and moral judgements.

name: Ngorongoro Conservation Area
description: The Ngorongoro Conservation Area contains the world's largest intact volcanic caldera — 19 km across and 600 m deep — sheltering the densest concentration of wildlife in Africa. Over 25,000 large animals including the Big Five, flamingos and rare black rhinos live on the crater floor year-round, visible from the rim in extraordinary concentration. Adjacent Olduvai Gorge contains some of the world's most important hominin fossil finds, documenting 2 million years of human evolution in the cradle of humanity.
history: Established in 1959 when the Maasai were displaced from the Serengeti for national park creation, with their pastoralism rights preserved in the conservation area. UNESCO inscribed it as a World Heritage Site in 1979. Mary and Louis Leakey's discoveries at Olduvai Gorge, including the 1959 find of Paranthropus boisei, fundamentally changed the scientific understanding of human origins and made Tanzania a focal point of global paleoanthropology for generations of researchers.

name: Sagrada Família
description: Antoni Gaudí's Sagrada Família in Barcelona is the most visited monument in Spain — a Catholic basilica under continuous construction since 1882 and still unfinished after 140 years. Gaudí devoted the last 43 years of his life to the project. The building's organic forms, hyperboloid structures and symbolic programme integrate Christian theology, Catalan nationalism and natural geometry into an architectural vision of mind-bending originality. Completion is projected for around 2026 — the centenary of Gaudí's death.
history: Construction began in 1882 under architect Francisco de Paula del Villar, who resigned the following year. Gaudí took over in 1883 and transformed the Gothic design entirely. Construction was interrupted by the Spanish Civil War (1936–1939) when anarchists burned Gaudí's workshop destroying his models and drawings. Reconstruction from photographs allowed work to resume. Gaudí was struck by a tram in 1926 and died three days later, having completed only a quarter of the building he considered his life's masterwork.

name: Ephesus
description: Ephesus on Turkey's Aegean coast was one of the greatest cities of the ancient world — home to 200,000 people at its Roman peak in the 1st and 2nd centuries AD. The Library of Celsus with its ornate two-storey facade, the 25,000-seat theatre, and the Terrace Houses with extraordinary preserved mosaics and frescoes make Ephesus the most complete surviving ancient Greek and Roman city accessible to visitors. The Temple of Artemis here was one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World.
history: Founded by Greek colonists around 1000 BC and one of the most important cities of the ancient Mediterranean. St Paul preached here and addressed one of his New Testament letters to the Ephesians. The Virgin Mary is believed by Catholic tradition to have lived her final years near Ephesus. The city was progressively abandoned as its harbour silted up between the 5th and 15th centuries AD. UNESCO inscribed it as a World Heritage Site in 2015.

name: Rapa Nui National Park
description: Easter Island (Rapa Nui) in the remote South Pacific contains the moai — nearly 1,000 monolithic stone statues averaging 4 metres tall carved by the Rapa Nui people between the 13th and 16th centuries. The statues represent ancestral chiefs and were transported from the Rano Raraku quarry to coastal ceremonial platforms. The island's story of societal collapse from resource depletion has made it a globally cited cautionary tale about ecological overshoot and the fragility of isolated civilisations.
history: Polynesian settlers first arrived around 1200 AD. Population collapse from deforestation, civil war and subsequent European slave raids devastated the Rapa Nui people. Dutch explorer Jacob Roggeveen became the first European to see the island on Easter Sunday 1722. UNESCO inscribed Rapa Nui as a World Heritage Site in 1995. Ongoing negotiations between the Rapa Nui people and the Chilean government concern indigenous sovereignty, repatriation of artefacts from European museums, and site management and revenue-sharing rights.

name: British Museum
description: The British Museum in London holds one of the world's greatest collections of human history and culture, with over 8 million objects spanning two million years. Its permanent collection includes the Rosetta Stone, the Elgin Marbles, Egyptian mummies, the Sutton Hoo helmet and the Lewis Chessmen. Founded in 1753, it was the world's first national public museum. Admission is free and it receives over 6 million visitors annually, making it the UK's most visited cultural attraction and a global icon of encyclopaedic collecting.
history: Founded in 1753 based on the collection of physician Sir Hans Sloane, who bequeathed 71,000 objects to the nation. It opened in 1759, making it the world's first free national public museum. The museum's vast collection reflects Britain's imperial history — calls for repatriation of the Elgin Marbles from Greece, the Benin Bronzes from Nigeria and numerous other objects have intensified significantly in recent decades, creating a defining global debate about colonial-era museum acquisitions and the ethics of cultural heritage.

name: Louvre Museum
description: The Louvre in Paris is the world's most visited museum, welcoming over 9 million visitors annually to its 73,000 m² of galleries. Its nearly 35,000 works on permanent display include the Mona Lisa, Venus de Milo, Winged Victory of Samothrace and the Code of Hammurabi. The museum occupies a former royal palace dating to the 12th century, transformed by I.M. Pei's iconic glass pyramid entrance in 1989 into one of the world's most recognisable and photographed museum buildings.
history: The Louvre Palace was built as a fortress in 1190 by King Philip II and converted to a royal residence in the 16th century. It became a public museum on 10 August 1793 during the French Revolution when the royal collections were opened to citizens for the first time. Napoleon vastly expanded the collection through military conquest across Europe and Egypt, though many works were returned after his defeat at Waterloo. The glass pyramid, initially widely controversial, is now one of Paris's most beloved architectural landmarks.

name: Metropolitan Museum of Art
description: The Metropolitan Museum of Art on New York's Fifth Avenue is one of the world's largest art museums with a permanent collection of over 1.5 million objects spanning 5,000 years. Its encyclopaedic collection covers Egyptian antiquities including a complete Temple of Dendur, European Old Masters, American decorative arts, arms and armour, and contemporary art. The Met's facade along Central Park's Museum Mile stretches four city blocks and anchors one of the world's great cultural destinations drawing millions annually.
history: Founded in 1870 by American citizens who believed the United States deserved a great art museum to rival those of Europe. It opened in 1872 and moved to its current Central Park location in 1880. The museum has been a centre of controversy over acquired antiquities — the Euphronios krater (returned to Italy in 2008) and numerous provenance-disputed objects led to sweeping acquisition policy reforms in the 1990s and 2000s that have influenced the entire international museum community.

name: Egyptian Museum Cairo
description: The Egyptian Museum in Tahrir Square houses the world's most extensive collection of ancient Egyptian antiquities, with over 120,000 items spanning 5,000 years. The treasures of Tutankhamun — including his solid gold death mask — and the Royal Mummy Room housing 27 royal mummies including Ramesses II are the celebrated centrepieces. Built in 1902, it has been supplemented by the Grand Egyptian Museum near Giza, which began a phased opening in 2023 as the world's largest archaeological museum ever constructed.
history: Established in 1835 by Auguste Mariette, French archaeologist appointed by the Egyptian government to combat looting of ancient sites. Tutankhamun's treasures, discovered by Howard Carter in 1922, transformed the museum's international status and made Egyptology a global cultural phenomenon. The Grand Egyptian Museum, under construction since 2002 with capacity for 100,000 artefacts and 5 million annual visitors, represents the most ambitious museum construction project in modern history.

name: Smithsonian Museum of Natural History
description: The Smithsonian National Museum of Natural History on Washington D.C.'s National Mall is one of the world's largest natural history museums with over 145 million specimens. The Hope Diamond — the world's largest blue diamond at 45.52 carats — is its most famous exhibit. Its Ocean Hall, fossil halls and human origins exhibits draw 7 million visitors annually at no charge. The museum's scientific research programme employs hundreds of researchers across biology, geology, anthropology and palaeontology.
history: The Smithsonian Institution was founded in 1846 with a bequest from British scientist James Smithson to create an institution for 'the increase and diffusion of knowledge.' The Natural History Museum opened in its current building in 1910. The Hope Diamond was donated by jeweller Harry Winston in 1958, who famously mailed it to the museum in a plain brown paper package via ordinary post. The museum's collection grows by approximately 500,000 new specimens annually through field research worldwide.

name: Hermitage Museum
description: The State Hermitage Museum in St Petersburg is one of the world's largest and oldest museums, housed in the magnificent Winter Palace and five adjacent buildings along the Neva River. Its collection of over 3 million items includes works by Leonardo da Vinci, Raphael, Rembrandt and the world's finest collection of Impressionist and Post-Impressionist paintings outside France. The Winter Palace's 1,057 rooms and 1,945 windows create a backdrop of imperial splendour unmatched in the museum world.
history: Founded in 1764 by Empress Catherine the Great with the purchase of 225 paintings from a Berlin merchant. The Winter Palace served as the Romanov imperial residence until 1917. During the 872-day Siege of Leningrad in World War II, museum staff lived in the basement protecting the collection while German forces surrounded the city — a story of extraordinary cultural dedication under bombardment that has become one of history's defining narratives of wartime cultural preservation.
photo_author_orig: unknown but clearly a pre-1923 photo of building

name: National Museum of India
description: The National Museum of India in New Delhi houses one of the largest collections of Indian artefacts spanning 5,000 years from the Indus Valley Civilisation to the modern era. Its 200,000 works include Buddhist sculpture, Chola bronzes, Mughal miniature paintings, tribal art, coins and textiles. The Harappan dancing girl — a 4,500-year-old bronze figure from Mohenjo-daro — stands as one of the great sculptures of the ancient world and the museum's most celebrated single object.
history: Inaugurated in 1949 two years after Indian independence. The museum was conceived as a symbol of independent India's connection to its 5,000-year cultural heritage, uniting diverse regional traditions under a common national narrative. Repatriation of Indian artefacts from British and American museums remains an ongoing diplomatic priority for the Indian government, with significant returns of objects achieved in recent years through bilateral negotiation.

name: Museo del Prado
description: The Museo del Prado in Madrid is Spain's national art museum and one of the world's greatest painting collections, displaying over 7,600 paintings. Its unrivalled holdings include Velázquez's Las Meninas (considered by many the world's greatest painting), Goya's disturbing Black Paintings, and Bosch's Garden of Earthly Delights. Flemish and Italian masterpieces from the Habsburg royal collection round out a gathering of Old Masters without equal in any single collection anywhere in the world.
history: The Prado opened as a public museum in 1819, displaying the Spanish royal collection accumulated over three centuries. During the Spanish Civil War, the Republican government arranged for the Prado's masterpieces to be evacuated to Valencia, Barcelona and then Geneva — a remarkable logistical operation that saved the collection from potential destruction in a conflict that devastated much of Spain's cultural infrastructure. Today it receives nearly 4 million visitors annually.
photo_author: Angel  de los Rios from Valladolid, Spain
photo_author_orig: Angel  de los Rios from Valladolid, Spain

name: Vatican Museums
description: The Vatican Museums contain one of the world's greatest art collections accumulated by the Catholic Church over five centuries. The Sistine Chapel ceiling frescoes painted by Michelangelo between 1508 and 1512 — depicting 343 figures across 520 m² — is the undisputed centrepiece. The Raphael Rooms, Gallery of Maps and Egyptian collections are among 54 galleries receiving over 6 million visitors annually, making Vatican City one of the most visited places on Earth relative to its very small population.
history: The Vatican Museums grew from the collection of Pope Julius II, who placed the ancient sculpture Laocoön and His Sons (discovered in 1506) in a courtyard that became the nucleus of the world's first public sculpture collection. Michelangelo's ceiling took four years to complete, painted lying on scaffolding in a physically demanding feat that matched its artistic ambition. The most recent major restoration of the Sistine Chapel frescoes was completed in 1994 after 14 years of painstaking scientific work.

name: National Palace Museum, Taipei
description: The National Palace Museum in Taipei houses the world's largest collection of Chinese imperial art — nearly 700,000 pieces spanning 8,000 years assembled by the Ming and Qing dynasties and evacuated to Taiwan by the Nationalist government in 1949. Highlights include the jadeite cabbage, the Meat-shaped Stone carved to look exactly like braised pork, and over 150,000 rare books. The Beijing and Taipei museums both claim legitimacy as the true custodian of China's imperial cultural heritage.
history: The collection was established as the Palace Museum in Beijing's Forbidden City in 1925. Following Japan's invasion in 1937, 13,000 crates were moved inland to protect them from bombing. After the Nationalist defeat in the Civil War, Chiang Kai-shek had the most prized 600,000 pieces transported to Taiwan in 1949. The National Palace Museum opened in Taipei in 1965. The split collection remains one of the world's most politically charged and symbolically loaded cultural heritage disputes.

name: Rijksmuseum
description: The Rijksmuseum in Amsterdam houses the finest collection of Dutch Golden Age art in the world. Rembrandt's Night Watch — considered the greatest Dutch painting ever made — occupies its own gallery. Vermeer's The Milkmaid, Delftware ceramics, ship models, dolls' houses and Delft Blue tiles complete a comprehensive portrait of Holland's extraordinary 17th-century cultural flowering. The neo-Gothic and Renaissance Revival building by Pierre Cuypers was extensively renovated and reopened in 2013 to extraordinary international acclaim.
history: Founded in The Hague in 1800 using works from the House of Orange-Nassau and moved to Amsterdam in 1808 at Napoleon's direction. The Night Watch was controversially trimmed to fit its original location, removing figures from all four sides. In 1975 a schizophrenic slashed the painting with a bread knife; in 1990 it was attacked with acid — both times expertly restored. The museum's 10-year renovation (2003–2013) was designed to respect the original building while making it fully accessible to modern visitors.

name: Uffizi Gallery
description: The Uffizi Gallery in Florence houses the finest collection of Italian Renaissance painting anywhere on Earth. Botticelli's The Birth of Venus and Primavera, Leonardo da Vinci's Annunciation, Michelangelo's Doni Tondo and Caravaggio's Medusa are among its masterpieces. The museum occupies the Uffizi Palace built in 1560 by Giorgio Vasari for Cosimo I de' Medici — uffizi means 'offices' — on the banks of the Arno in the heart of Renaissance Florence, the city that shaped Western civilisation.
history: The Uffizi collection was built by the Medici family over two centuries and bequeathed to the people of Florence in 1743 by the last Medici heir, Anna Maria Luisa, on condition it never leave Florence — one of history's first acts of cultural philanthropy directed at the public good rather than dynastic prestige or private collection. In 1993, a Mafia car bomb outside the museum killed five people and damaged several paintings in an attack intended to pressure the Italian government on criminal sentencing.

name: National Museum of China
description: The National Museum of China on Tiananmen Square is the world's most visited museum by annual attendance at approximately 8 million visitors. Its collection of over 1.4 million artefacts spans 5,000 years of Chinese civilisation — from Neolithic jade objects and Shang dynasty bronzes through Tang dynasty pottery horses, Song ceramics and contemporary Chinese art. Its 192,000 m² of floor space makes it one of the largest museum buildings ever constructed anywhere in the world.
history: Formed in 1959 through a merger of the National History Museum and Museum of the Chinese Revolution. Closed for a massive expansion from 2007 to 2011, doubling floor space, designed by the Hamburg-based firm gmp Architekten in collaboration with a Chinese architectural institute. The museum's exhibitions explicitly support the Chinese Communist Party's narrative of national history and the party's role in China's national renewal, making it as much a political institution as a cultural one.
photo_author_orig: Original uploader was AcidBomber at en.wikipedia

name: National Air and Space Museum
description: The Smithsonian National Air and Space Museum on Washington D.C.'s National Mall is the world's most visited museum with approximately 7 million annual visitors. Its collection includes the Wright Brothers' Flyer, Charles Lindbergh's Spirit of St Louis, the Apollo 11 command module, a touchable moon rock, and spacesuits worn by Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin on the lunar surface. The museum opened in 1976 and chronicles humanity's extraordinary journey from the first controlled flight to deep space exploration.
history: The Smithsonian began collecting aviation artefacts in 1876, acquiring the Wright Brothers' Kitty Hawk Flyer in 1948 after a dispute with British museums over where it would be displayed. The current building opened on 1 July 1976 for America's bicentennial. The Steven F. Udvar-Házy Center near Dulles Airport, opened in 2003, houses the Space Shuttle Discovery, a Concorde, the SR-71 Blackbird and the Enola Gay — the B-29 that dropped the first atomic bomb, one of history's most controversial museum display decisions.

name: NMAAHC Washington D.C.
description: The National Museum of African American History and Culture on Washington D.C.'s National Mall is the world's largest museum dedicated to African American life, history and culture. Opened in 2016 after decades of advocacy, its 37,000 artefacts include Harriet Tubman's hymnal, a slave cabin from South Carolina, Muhammad Ali's boxing robe and Emmett Till's casket. The stunning bronze-latticed building by David Adjaye draws on West African architectural tradition, making it as architecturally significant as it is culturally essential.
history: Legislation to establish a national museum of African American history was first introduced in Congress in 1915 — it took 89 years and six Congressional attempts before President George W. Bush signed the authorising legislation in 2003. President Barack Obama broke ground in 2012 and opened the completed museum in 2016. The museum received over 3 million visitors in its first year — more than any other Smithsonian museum — demonstrating the profound public appetite for this long-overlooked history being finally told at national scale.

name: Acropolis Museum
description: The Acropolis Museum at the foot of the Athenian Acropolis was designed specifically to house the sculptures and artefacts of the Acropolis. The building by Bernard Tschumi, opened in 2009, places the Parthenon Gallery on the top floor aligned precisely with the Parthenon visible through floor-to-ceiling glass — a deliberate architectural argument for reuniting the sculptures. Original Caryatid porch figures, Nike temple frieze blocks and surviving Parthenon sculptures are displayed in their original ceremonial sequence.
history: The Acropolis Museum was designed to demonstrate that Greece has a world-class facility capable of housing the Elgin Marbles — addressing the British Museum's long-standing argument that Athens lacked adequate infrastructure for their return. Construction was delayed repeatedly by archaeological discoveries during excavation; the foundations were redesigned to span over the revealed ancient city beneath the site. The museum's opening reignited global debate about the repatriation of cultural heritage acquired during the colonial era.
