name: Iziko South African Museum
description: The Iziko South African Museum in Cape Town is the oldest museum in sub-Saharan Africa, founded in 1825, and houses southern Africa's premier collections of natural history and human cultural heritage. Its Whale Well — a six-storey atrium with full whale skeletons suspended from the ceiling — is one of Cape Town's most dramatic interior spaces. The San rock art collection, Stone Age artefacts and Gondwana palaeontology collections represent southern Africa's deep natural and human history across millions of years of time.
history: Founded in 1825 by Lord Charles Somerset, British Governor of the Cape Colony, making it Africa's oldest museum. During the apartheid era, the museum's practices regarding display of indigenous peoples' remains became a major source of controversy. The museum housed the remains of Saartjie Baartman, the Khoikhoi woman displayed in Europe as the 'Hottentot Venus,' until her remains were repatriated to South Africa in 2002 after years of diplomatic effort and advocacy by the South African government.

name: Tokyo National Museum
description: The Tokyo National Museum in Ueno Park is Japan's oldest and largest museum, housing over 120,000 works representing the full breadth of Japanese and Asian art and history. The museum's five buildings display samurai armour and swords, Buddhist sculpture, Jōmon pottery, ukiyo-e woodblock prints and treasures from the imperial household. The museum is also responsible for the conservation and display of over 88 National Treasures — Japan's most legally protected cultural objects, the crown jewels of Japanese civilisation.
history: Established in 1872 as part of Japan's first national Exposition in Ueno Park. The current Honkan main building, an Imperial Crown style structure designed by Jin Watanabe, was completed in 1938. The museum's collection was significantly expanded after World War II with artefacts collected across Asia during the imperial period. Repatriation discussions with South Korea and China over some of these objects remain ongoing and diplomatically sensitive between neighbouring nations.

name: Pergamon Museum
description: The Pergamon Museum on Berlin's Museum Island houses some of antiquity's greatest architectural monuments — reconstructed at full scale inside its vast halls. The Pergamon Altar (2nd century BC), the Market Gate of Miletus (2nd century AD) and the Ishtar Gate of Babylon (6th century BC) were excavated from their original sites in Turkey and Iraq and painstakingly reassembled block by block in Berlin, creating a museum experience of architectural immersion unlike anything else in the world.
history: The Pergamon Museum opened in 1930. The Ishtar Gate was excavated by Robert Koldewey between 1899 and 1914 and shipped to Berlin in 900 crates. During World War II the collection was stored in bunkers; some pieces were seized by Soviet forces and held in Russian museums until returned to East Germany in 1958. The museum is undergoing major renovation with its main wing closed until 2037 — the longest planned museum closure in modern history — raising profound questions about the future of these extraordinary reconstructed installations.

name: Musée d'Orsay
description: The Musée d'Orsay in Paris houses the world's greatest collection of Impressionist and Post-Impressionist masterpieces in a magnificent converted railway station on the Seine. Monet's water lilies, Renoir's Moulin de la Galette, Van Gogh's self-portraits, Degas' dancers and Toulouse-Lautrec's posters make the d'Orsay essential for lovers of 19th-century art. The station's original iron and glass architecture — a soaring cathedral of Victorian engineering — provides a dramatically beautiful setting that itself is part of the experience.
history: The Gare d'Orsay was built for the 1900 Paris Exposition Universelle but became obsolete for mainline rail by 1939 due to its short platforms, unsuitable for modern trains. The building was threatened with demolition before being converted to a museum that opened in 1986. The Impressionist collection came from the Louvre and the Jeu de Paume. Orson Welles filmed The Trial in the derelict building in 1962. The station's great clocks, visible from inside and outside, are among the most photographed objects in all of Paris.

name: Göbekli Tepe
description: Göbekli Tepe in southeastern Turkey is the world's oldest known monumental structure, built approximately 11,500 years ago — 7,000 years before Stonehenge. Its T-shaped limestone pillars weighing up to 20 tonnes are carved with animal reliefs and arranged in circular enclosures. Deliberately buried around 8,000 BC, its existence overturned the fundamental assumption that monumental architecture required settled agriculture — it was built entirely by hunter-gatherers who organised massive communal building projects before developing farming.
history: First examined in 1963 by archaeologists who dismissed it as a medieval cemetery. Klaus Schmidt began systematic excavation in 1994 and recognised its extraordinary prehistoric significance, working the site until his death in 2014. UNESCO inscribed it as a World Heritage Site in 2018. Less than 5% of the site has been excavated — ground-penetrating radar surveys suggest up to 20 additional stone circles buried beneath the surrounding plateau, potentially making future discoveries even more transformative for our understanding of early human civilisation.

name: Teotihuacan
description: Teotihuacan near Mexico City was one of the largest cities in the ancient world with perhaps 125,000 people at its 400 AD peak and the most influential urban centre in pre-Columbian Mesoamerica. Its Avenue of the Dead stretches 2.4 km flanked by the Pyramid of the Sun (third-largest pyramid in the world) and Pyramid of the Moon. The city's grid plan, apartment compounds, mural paintings and obsidian trade networks influenced civilisations across Mesoamerica for a thousand years after its mysterious collapse around 550 AD.
history: Founded around 100 BC, reaching its apex between 100–650 AD. Its builders and language are entirely unknown — the city predates the Aztec Empire by over a millennium. The Aztecs named it Teotihuacan ('birthplace of the gods') when they found it already ancient and abandoned, incorporating it into their cosmological mythology. UNESCO inscribed the site in 1987. Annual visitor numbers now exceed 4 million, making it Mexico's most visited archaeological site and one of the most significant pre-Columbian monuments in the entire Americas.
photo_author_orig: Tjeerd Wiersma from Amsterdam, The Netherlands

name: Olduvai Gorge
description: Olduvai Gorge (Oldupai) in Tanzania is one of the most important paleoanthropological sites in the world — a 48-km ravine that has yielded fossils documenting nearly 2 million years of human evolution. Louis and Mary Leakey discovered the skull of Paranthropus boisei in 1959 and remains of Homo habilis in 1960 — discoveries that shifted the scientific consensus on human origins definitively to Africa. The gorge exposes 2 million years of sedimentary layers, each containing fossils and stone tools charting progressive human technological development.
history: First documented scientifically by German entomologist Wilhelm Kattwinkel in 1911. Louis Leakey first visited in 1931 and began systematic work with his wife Mary in 1959. Their discovery of OH 5 (Paranthropus boisei) on 17 July 1959 made international headlines and secured National Geographic Society funding for decades of subsequent fieldwork. The Olduvai Gorge Museum at the site commemorates the Leakeys' work and displays original fossil casts at the exact location where humanity's origin story was permanently and dramatically rewritten.

name: Tikal
description: Tikal in Guatemala's Petén jungle was one of the most powerful cities of Classic Maya civilisation, with a population of perhaps 60,000 at its peak between 200 and 900 AD. Its six towering pyramids rise above the rainforest canopy — Temple IV at 65 m is the tallest pre-Columbian structure in the Americas. The site covers 576 km² with over 3,000 structures. Tikal waged centuries of warfare against rival Maya cities, and its history of alliance, conquest and political intrigue rivals any ancient civilisation in scale and complexity.
history: Occupied from as early as 800 BC and reaching its height in the Classic period. The city's collapse around 900 AD is attributed to warfare, drought, deforestation and political instability. First reported to the outside world in 1848 and systematic excavation began in the 1950s. UNESCO inscribed Tikal as a World Heritage Site in 1979. LiDAR technology in 2018 revealed a vast network of causeways, reservoirs and agricultural canals showing the true scale of the ancient Maya landscape was far greater than the visible above-ground monuments suggest.

name: Leptis Magna
description: Leptis Magna on Libya's Mediterranean coast is arguably the best-preserved Roman city in the world — its marble columns, triumphal arches, theatre, forum and baths standing in extraordinary completeness against the backdrop of the Mediterranean and the Sahara. Embellished to imperial magnificence by the Emperor Septimius Severus, who was born here in 145 AD, Leptis Magna represents the Roman world at its most architecturally ambitious. Sand dunes preserved it for centuries before Italian colonial-era excavations revealed this remarkable Mediterranean treasure.
history: Founded by Phoenician traders from Carthage around 1100 BC, flourishing as a trading port rich from olive oil and the wild animal trade. Emperor Septimius Severus beautified the city between 202–216 AD with a magnificent new forum, basilica and triumphal arch of extraordinary scale and sophistication. Declined after Arab conquest in 643 AD. UNESCO inscribed it as a World Heritage Site in 1982, though ongoing conflict in Libya has severely hampered site protection and conservation work in recent decades.

name: Persepolis
description: Persepolis was the ceremonial capital of the Achaemenid Persian Empire — the ancient world's largest — built by Darius I beginning in 518 BC and destroyed by Alexander the Great in 330 BC. The Apadana Palace staircase reliefs depicting delegations from 23 subject nations bringing tribute represent one of antiquity's greatest sculptural programmes illustrating the sheer scale and diversity of the Persian world. The Gate of All Nations and Hundred-Column Hall demonstrate the organisational and artistic power of rulers whose empire stretched from Greece to India.
history: Construction began under Darius I around 518 BC on a rock terrace at Marvdasht. Burned by Alexander the Great in 330 BC — ancient sources debate whether it was deliberate revenge for Persian destruction of Athens or a drunken accident at a feast. UNESCO inscribed Persepolis as a World Heritage Site in 1979. Iran's Islamic Republic was initially hostile to preservation of pre-Islamic heritage but has since invested significantly in the site's conservation, recognising its importance to Iranian national identity and tourism revenue.

name: Skara Brae
description: Skara Brae on Orkney's Mainland island is northern Europe's most complete Neolithic village, occupied between 3100 and 2500 BC — older than both Stonehenge and the Great Pyramids. Eight stone houses connected by covered passageways are furnished with stone dressers, box beds, hearths and storage tanks surviving intact for 5,000 years. The village was buried under sand dunes after abandonment and rediscovered by a winter storm in 1850, giving an unparalleled and deeply intimate window into prehistoric domestic life.
history: Occupied for approximately 600 years before abandonment around 2500 BC. A catastrophic storm in 1850 stripped away the sand dunes, revealing the preserved houses. William Watt, owner of Skara Hall estate, began excavation that year. Further excavations by Gordon Childe in the 1920s established the site's Neolithic date and world significance. UNESCO inscribed Skara Brae as part of the Heart of Neolithic Orkney World Heritage Site in 1999. The site is one of Scotland's most visited prehistoric monuments and inspires awe at the sophistication of its 5,000-year-old builders.

name: Mohenjo-daro
description: Mohenjo-daro in Pakistan's Sindh province was one of the earliest and most sophisticated urban centres in the ancient world, built around 2500 BC at the height of the Indus Valley Civilisation. Its grid-planned streets, brick-built houses, covered drainage system, public granary and Great Bath represent urban planning not matched in Europe for another 4,000 years. The site once housed an estimated 40,000 people. The Indus script inscribed on thousands of seals remains completely undeciphered — one of archaeology's most tantalising and enduring mysteries.
history: Discovered in 1922 by Rakhal Das Banerji of the Archaeological Survey of India. R.E.M. Wheeler conducted major excavations in 1950. The city was abandoned around 1900 BC for reasons that remain actively debated — climate change, flooding and migration are all proposed with different degrees of supporting evidence. UNESCO inscribed the site in 1980 and placed it on the World Heritage in Danger list due to salt crystallisation damage threatening the mud-brick structures that have survived for four and a half millennia.

name: Çatalhöyük
description: Çatalhöyük in south-central Turkey is one of the world's oldest and best-preserved Neolithic towns, occupied between 7500 and 5700 BC by up to 8,000 people. Houses were entered through roof openings rather than street-level doors, and the dead were buried beneath the floors of lived-in homes. Wall paintings, figurines and animal skulls embedded in walls reflect a rich symbolic life. Remarkably, the town shows no evidence of social hierarchy — powerfully challenging assumptions about the inevitability of inequality in settled communities.
history: Excavated by James Mellaart between 1958 and 1965, whose discoveries of elaborate wall paintings and goddess figurines generated enormous popular interest in Neolithic religion. Ian Hodder led re-excavations from 1993, using post-processual archaeological methods that challenged some of Mellaart's interpretations. UNESCO inscribed Çatalhöyük as a World Heritage Site in 2012. A scandal over Mellaart's alleged forgery of Neolithic documents, revealed posthumously in 2018, has added significant controversy to the site's interpretive and academic history.

name: Karnak Temple Complex
description: The Karnak Temple Complex at Luxor is the largest religious building ever constructed — a 100-hectare complex built, expanded and modified by successive pharaohs over 1,500 years. The Hypostyle Hall contains 134 colossal columns arranged in 16 rows, each reaching 23 metres high and 10 metres in circumference. The walls covered in hieroglyphic inscriptions document the political and religious history of ancient Egypt in extraordinary detail, recording three millennia of continuous building by at least 30 successive pharaohs across Egypt's greatest dynasties.
history: Karnak was the most important temple complex of ancient Egypt and the power base of the Amun priesthood, who at their peak controlled more wealth than the pharaoh himself. Construction began under Senusret I around 2000 BC and continued until Roman times. An active place of worship for over 1,500 years. UNESCO inscribed ancient Thebes, including Karnak, as a World Heritage Site in 1979. The site attracts over 2 million visitors annually and is Egypt's second most visited ancient monument after the Pyramids of Giza.
photo_author_orig: Original uploader was Ninlil at tr.wikipedia

name: Jenne-jeno
description: Jenne-jeno (Ancient Djenné) in Mali is the earliest known city in sub-Saharan Africa, occupied from around 250 BC to 1400 AD, predating European contact by over a millennium. The archaeological mound reveals 2,000 years of continuous occupation including iron smelting, terracotta figurines and trade goods from North Africa and the Sahara — demonstrating that complex urban civilisations developed independently in West Africa without external influence. The living city of Djenné beside it has the world's largest mud-brick building, the magnificent Great Mosque.
history: Excavations by Roderick and Susan McIntosh in the 1970s and 1980s overturned the longstanding assumption that sub-Saharan Africa had no pre-colonial urban civilisation. Jenne-jeno showed that African cities developed organically from local trade networks. UNESCO inscribed the Old Towns of Djenné as a World Heritage Site in 1988. The Great Mosque was placed on the UNESCO World Heritage in Danger list in 2016 due to political instability and looting affecting the wider Sahel region in which this extraordinary site is located.

name: Caral
description: Caral in Peru's Supe Valley is the oldest known city in the Americas, dated to approximately 2600 BC — contemporary with Egypt's Old Kingdom and Mesopotamia's Ur III period. The site contains six large platform mounds (pyramids), sunken circular plazas, residential quarters and evidence of sophisticated social organisation including textiles and quipu (knotted-string record-keeping). Caral demonstrates that complex civilisation emerged independently in the Americas at the same time as in the Old World, with no contact between them — a profound statement about human ingenuity.
history: Excavated by Peruvian archaeologist Ruth Shady beginning in 1994. Carbon-14 dating confirmed its extraordinary age in 2001, triggering major revision of the timeline of American civilisation. UNESCO inscribed Caral as a World Heritage Site in 2009. Ruth Shady's work transformed scientific understanding of the Americas' independent development of complex urban civilisation and established her as one of South America's most important living archaeologists. The site faces ongoing threats from illegal squatters and agricultural expansion on its periphery.
photo_author_orig: No machine-readable author provided. Jose C. assumed (based on copyright claims).

name: Aksum
description: Aksum in northern Ethiopia was the capital of the Aksumite Empire, one of the ancient world's great powers, dominating trade between the Mediterranean and Indian Ocean from the 1st to 7th centuries AD. Its monumental stelae — granite obelisks up to 33 metres tall, some standing after 2,000 years — are among the most impressive monuments of ancient Africa. Aksum is also believed by Ethiopian tradition to house the Ark of the Covenant in the Church of Our Lady Mary of Zion, making it one of Christianity's most sacred and mysterious pilgrimage destinations.
history: The Aksumite Empire controlled the strategic Red Sea trade route, minting its own gold coins — one of only three empires worldwide to do so in antiquity alongside Rome and Persia. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1980. One of Aksum's greatest obelisks was looted by Italian colonial forces in 1937 and erected in Rome. After decades of diplomatic effort, it was returned to Ethiopia and re-erected in Aksum in 2008 — one of Africa's most significant and hard-won cultural repatriation successes.

name: Mapungubwe Cultural Landscape
description: Mapungubwe is an open savanna landscape at the confluence of the Limpopo and Shashe rivers, where South Africa meets Botswana and Zimbabwe. It was the centre of southern Africa's first indigenous kingdom, a sophisticated trading state that flourished between roughly 1075 and 1300 CE. Visitors can climb Mapungubwe Hill, view the interpretive centre's award-winning architecture, and see the landscape of baobabs and sandstone formations that the kingdom once commanded. The surrounding national park protects elephant, white rhino, and an exceptional diversity of birdlife.
history: At its height Mapungubwe was home to as many as 5,000 people and traded gold and ivory with the Swahili coast, India and China. The famous gold-foil rhinoceros, sceptre and bowl recovered from royal graves on the hilltop are among South Africa's most important archaeological treasures. The kingdom declined around 1300, with power shifting north to Great Zimbabwe as climate change altered the region. Excavated by the University of Pretoria from 1933, the site was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2003 for its evidence of an early, complex African society.

name: Cradle of Humankind
description: The Cradle of Humankind is a 47,000-hectare complex of limestone caves north-west of Johannesburg that has yielded some of the richest evidence of human origins anywhere on Earth. The Sterkfontein, Swartkrans and Kromdraai caves have produced hundreds of hominin fossils spanning more than three million years. The Maropeng visitor centre and the Sterkfontein Caves are both open to the public, offering underground tours and immersive exhibitions on human evolution. It is one of the most accessible and information-rich palaeoanthropological landscapes in the world.
history: The area's scientific fame began in 1936 when Robert Broom recovered the first adult Australopithecus fossil at Sterkfontein. In 1947 the near-complete skull nicknamed 'Mrs Ples' was found there, and in 1994 the remarkably complete 'Little Foot' skeleton was identified in the same cave system. Swartkrans preserves some of the earliest evidence of the controlled use of fire by hominins. UNESCO inscribed the Cradle of Humankind in 1999, recognising its unparalleled contribution to understanding where humanity came from.
photo_author: Pavia, Marco; Braga, José; Delfino, Massimo; Kgasi, Lazarus; Manegold, Albrecht; Steininger, Christine; Zipfel, Bernhard; Val, Aurore
photo_author_orig: Pavia, Marco; Braga, José; Delfino, Massimo; Kgasi, Lazarus; Manegold, Albrecht; Steininger, Christine; Zipfel, Bernhard; Val, Aurore

name: iSimangaliso Wetland Park
description: iSimangaliso, meaning 'a miracle' in isiZulu, stretches for some 220 kilometres along the KwaZulu-Natal coast and was South Africa's first natural World Heritage Site. It encompasses Lake St Lucia, coral reefs, coastal dunes among the highest in the world, swamps, and offshore waters where humpback whales pass. The park protects hippos, crocodiles, leatherback and loggerhead turtles, and more than 500 bird species across its mosaic of ecosystems. Visitors come for boat safaris, snorkelling, turtle-nesting tours and self-drive game viewing.
history: The park brings together areas that had been protected piecemeal since the early 20th century, including the St Lucia Game Reserve proclaimed in 1895. In the 1980s and 1990s the wetlands were threatened by proposals for dune mining, which were ultimately rejected in favour of conservation and ecotourism. UNESCO inscribed the site in 1999 for its outstanding biodiversity and the dynamic interplay of its coastal and freshwater systems. It was renamed iSimangaliso in 2007 to reflect its Zulu heritage.

name: Richtersveld Cultural and Botanical Landscape
description: The Richtersveld is a mountainous desert in the far north-west of South Africa, a stark and beautiful terrain of jagged peaks and dry riverbeds near the Orange River. It is one of the few places where the Nama people still practise their traditional semi-nomadic pastoralism, moving livestock between seasonal grazing grounds. The portable domed 'matjieshuis' reed houses of the Nama are emblematic of this living culture. The region is also a botanical wonder, part of the Succulent Karoo biodiversity hotspot with an extraordinary concentration of desert-adapted plants.
history: The Nama and their ancestors have herded in this arid landscape for thousands of years, developing a sustainable system finely tuned to its scarce water and grazing. The communal Richtersveld lands were the subject of a landmark South African land-rights case that recognised the community's ownership in 2003. UNESCO inscribed the Cultural and Botanical Landscape in 2007 as a rare surviving example of transhumant pastoralism. The community itself co-manages the World Heritage Site, a model of conservation rooted in indigenous knowledge.

name: Vredefort Dome
description: The Vredefort Dome is the eroded remnant of the largest verified meteorite impact structure on Earth, lying south-west of Johannesburg. The original crater is estimated to have been up to 300 kilometres across, dwarfing any other known impact site. What remains today is a circular range of hills and concentric ridges where the deep crust was forced upward by the blast. The Vaal River winds through the dome, and the area offers hiking, rafting and geology tours.
history: The impact occurred roughly two billion years ago, when an asteroid several kilometres wide struck what is now the Free State. The colossal energy released melted rock and overturned the geological strata, leaving a record of one of the most violent events in the planet's history. Geologists confirmed the impact origin through shatter cones and shocked quartz found across the structure. UNESCO inscribed the Vredefort Dome in 2005 as the oldest and largest clearly visible meteorite impact site on Earth.
photo_author: NASA Earth Observatory image created by Lauren Dauphin, using Operational Land Imager (OLI) on Landsat 8; data provided courtesy of the U.S. Geological Survey team.
photo_author_orig: NASA Earth Observatory image created by Lauren Dauphin, using Operational Land Imager (OLI) on Landsat 8; data provided courtesy of the U.S. Geological Survey team.

name: Maloti-Drakensberg Park
description: The Maloti-Drakensberg Park spans the basalt escarpment of the uKhahlamba-Drakensberg mountains on the border of South Africa and Lesotho. Towering buttresses such as the Amphitheatre and Cathedral Peak rise above grasslands threaded with rivers and waterfalls, including Tugela Falls, among the tallest in the world. The park is celebrated for both its alpine scenery and its dense concentration of San rock art, with thousands of paintings sheltered in caves and overhangs. It is a paradise for hikers, climbers and lovers of mountain wilderness.
history: The San people occupied these mountains for thousands of years, leaving an unrivalled record of their beliefs and daily life in delicate ochre and white paintings. The range later became a frontier between San, Zulu and European settlers in the 19th century. The South African side was inscribed by UNESCO in 2000 for its natural beauty and rock art, and in 2013 it was extended across the border to include Lesotho's Sehlabathebe, becoming a transboundary World Heritage Site.

name: Cape Floral Region Protected Areas
description: The Cape Floral Region is the smallest and most concentrated of the world's six floral kingdoms, packed into the south-western corner of South Africa. Dominated by the shrubland known as fynbos, it contains nearly 9,000 plant species, the majority found nowhere else on Earth. Iconic sites within it include Table Mountain, the Cape of Good Hope, and Kirstenbosch National Botanical Garden beneath the eastern slopes of Table Mountain. Its proteas, ericas and reeds make it one of the most botanically diverse places on the planet.
history: Fynbos evolved over millions of years in response to the region's nutrient-poor soils, Mediterranean climate and regular fires, which many species need to germinate. Indigenous Khoekhoe and San peoples used hundreds of these plants for food and medicine long before European arrival. The flora captivated 17th- and 18th-century botanists and helped shape the science of plant classification. UNESCO inscribed a serial set of eight protected areas in 2004, later expanded, to safeguard this exceptional concentration of life.

name: Khomani Cultural Landscape
description: The ‡Khomani Cultural Landscape lies in the red dunes of the Kalahari around the Kgalagadi Transfrontier Park, on South Africa's border with Botswana and Namibia. It is the ancestral home of the ‡Khomani San, whose deep ecological knowledge of this harsh desert spans many thousands of years. The landscape of shifting dunes, dry pans and hardy camelthorn trees supports gemsbok, springbok and the famous black-maned Kalahari lions. Visitors can join San-guided walks that reveal traditional tracking, foraging and survival skills.
history: The ‡Khomani San were dispossessed of their land during the 20th century, including when the Kalahari Gemsbok National Park was created in 1931. After the end of apartheid, a successful land claim in 1999 restored ancestral rights and reconnected the community to its territory. The story became a symbol of indigenous restitution in democratic South Africa. UNESCO inscribed the cultural landscape in 2017 in recognition of the ‡Khomani San's enduring relationship with the Kalahari.

name: Addo Elephant National Park
description: Addo Elephant National Park in the Eastern Cape is South Africa's third-largest national park and a conservation success story. Once reduced to a handful of survivors, its elephant population now numbers well over 600 in the main game area. The park has expanded to embrace five of South Africa's biomes and even a marine protected area, making it the only park where visitors might encounter the 'Big Seven' — the Big Five plus southern right whales and great white sharks. Dense thicket vegetation gives close, intimate elephant sightings on self-drive routes.
history: Addo was proclaimed in 1931 to save the last 11 elephants of the region, which had been hunted almost to extinction by farmers and ivory traders. A famous elephant-proof fence built from tram rails and lift cables in the 1950s finally kept the herds within the reserve. Decades of careful management allowed the population to recover dramatically. The park has since grown through land additions into one of the most ecologically diverse protected areas in the country.

name: Pilanesberg National Park
description: Pilanesberg National Park sits within the crater of a long-extinct volcano in North West province, its concentric ridges forming a striking natural amphitheatre. The malaria-free reserve is home to the Big Five and lies conveniently close to Johannesburg and Pretoria, making it one of the most popular day-trip safari destinations in the country. Well-maintained roads and hides allow excellent game viewing around its central lake. Hot-air balloon flights offer a sweeping view of the ancient volcanic landscape.
history: The Pilanesberg complex is one of only a few alkaline volcanic structures in the world, formed roughly 1.3 billion years ago. The modern park was created in the 1970s through Operation Genesis, one of the largest game-relocation exercises ever attempted, which restocked the area with thousands of animals. Land was consolidated from former farms and tribal grazing grounds. Today it is jointly important for conservation, tourism and the surrounding communities.

name: Karoo National Park
description: Karoo National Park lies near Beaufort West in the vast semi-desert that covers much of South Africa's interior. It protects a sweeping landscape of flat-topped koppies, plains and the Nuweveld mountains, with a clarity of light and silence that defines the Karoo. The park shelters black rhino, Cape mountain zebra, kudu, and a wealth of reptiles and tortoises — including an exceptional density of tortoise species. Its dark skies make it a superb spot for stargazing.
history: The Karoo's exposed sedimentary rocks preserve a globally important fossil record of mammal-like reptiles from before the age of the dinosaurs. The park was established in 1979 to conserve a representative sample of this unique arid ecosystem. Over the decades several locally extinct species, including black rhino, have been successfully reintroduced. The Karoo's fossil heritage and stark beauty continue to draw scientists and travellers alike.

name: Golden Gate Highlands National Park
description: Golden Gate Highlands National Park is named for the spectacular golden and ochre sandstone cliffs that glow at sunrise and sunset in the eastern Free State. Unlike most South African parks, its appeal is scenic rather than focused on big game, with rolling highland grasslands, deep ravines and the towering Brandwag buttress. It protects grazers such as black wildebeest, eland, zebra and the rare bearded vulture and bald ibis. Hiking trails and horse rides lead through some of the country's most photogenic mountain country.
history: The dramatic Clarens Formation sandstone here was laid down in desert conditions during the Triassic and Jurassic periods. The area has also yielded important dinosaur fossils, including some of the earliest known dinosaur eggs and embryos. The park was proclaimed in 1963 to preserve this highland landscape on the edge of the Maluti mountains. It remains a favourite escape for visitors drawn to crisp mountain air and golden light.

name: Mountain Zebra National Park
description: Mountain Zebra National Park near Cradock in the Eastern Cape was created to save the endangered Cape mountain zebra from extinction. Set in the rugged Bankberg mountains and surrounding plains, it offers open highland scenery quite different from the bushveld parks further north. Beyond its flagship zebra, the reserve now protects lion, cheetah, buffalo, black rhino and abundant plains game. Self-drive routes and guided cheetah-tracking walks are highlights for visitors.
history: By the 1930s the Cape mountain zebra had been hunted to the brink, with only a few dozen animals left alive. The park was proclaimed in 1937 with an initial group of just five zebra, a precariously small founder population. Careful protection and the addition of further animals from surviving herds gradually rebuilt the numbers into the thousands across protected areas. The recovery is one of South Africa's most celebrated conservation achievements.

name: West Coast National Park
description: West Coast National Park surrounds the sheltered turquoise lagoon of Langebaan, about an hour north of Cape Town. It is best known for the dazzling spring wildflower displays in the Postberg section, when fields of daisies and other annuals erupt across the landscape in August and September. The wetlands are internationally important for migratory shorebirds, drawing tens of thousands of waders each summer. Visitors enjoy birdwatching hides, kayaking on the lagoon and coastal walks among fynbos and strandveld.
history: The Langebaan lagoon preserves some of the oldest known fossilised human footprints, dated to around 117,000 years ago and nicknamed 'Eve's footprints'. The area's rich shellfish and birdlife sustained Khoekhoe communities for millennia. The park was proclaimed in 1985 to protect the lagoon and its surrounding wetlands and islands. Today it balances conservation, scientific research and seasonal tourism on a fragile stretch of coast.

name: Marakele National Park
description: Marakele National Park lies in the heart of the Waterberg mountains in Limpopo, its name meaning 'place of sanctuary' in Tswana. It encompasses dramatic mountain scenery where the dry western bushveld meets wetter eastern slopes, creating unusual plant and animal diversity. The park is home to elephant, rhino, big cats and one of the largest colonies of endangered Cape vultures in the world. A vertiginous mountain road climbs to viewpoints overlooking the breeding cliffs.
history: The Waterberg has been inhabited and used by people for thousands of years, with San rock art and later Iron Age settlement traces across the region. Marakele was established in 1994 and has since expanded through the incorporation of neighbouring land and private reserves. Game such as elephant and rhino were reintroduced as the protected area grew. The Waterberg as a whole is now a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve, with Marakele as one of its conservation cornerstones.

name: Ditsong National Museum of Natural History
description: The Ditsong National Museum of Natural History in Pretoria, long known as the Transvaal Museum, is South Africa's premier natural-history institution. Its galleries cover geology, palaeontology, mammals, birds and the story of human evolution, drawing on collections built over more than a century. The museum houses the original 'Mrs Ples' fossil skull and other landmark hominin specimens from the nearby Cradle of Humankind. Its Austin Roberts Bird Hall is a celebrated reference point for southern African ornithology.
history: Founded in 1892, the museum became the scientific home of the fossils that reshaped understanding of human origins in the 20th century. Robert Broom worked here while making his famous Sterkfontein discoveries. The institution was renamed Ditsong, a Setswana word meaning 'heritage' or 'legacy', as part of a broader reorganisation of South Africa's national museums. It remains a centre of research as well as public education.

name: Apartheid Museum
description: The Apartheid Museum in Johannesburg is widely regarded as one of the most powerful museums of its kind in the world. From the moment visitors are randomly assigned a 'white' or 'non-white' entrance, the building immerses them in the lived experience of South Africa's system of racial segregation. Photographs, film, artefacts and reconstructed spaces trace the rise of apartheid, the long resistance against it, and the transition to democracy. It is both a memorial and an unflinching record of injustice and its eventual defeat.
history: The museum opened in 2001, just seven years after South Africa's first democratic elections, while the wounds of apartheid were still fresh. It was developed alongside a casino complex whose licence required investment in a cultural project of national importance. A team of historians, designers and film-makers created an experience grounded in primary sources and survivor testimony. It has since become an essential destination for understanding the country's 20th-century history.

name: Origins Centre
description: The Origins Centre at the University of the Witwatersrand in Johannesburg explores the deep history of humankind and the artistic traditions of southern Africa's earliest peoples. Its exhibits trace the emergence of the human species, the development of symbolic thought, and the world's oldest known art. The museum holds an outstanding collection of San rock-art imagery and explains the spiritual beliefs behind the paintings. Interactive displays connect ancient origins to questions of identity and ancestry today.
history: The centre opened in 2006, drawing on Wits University's leading research in archaeology, palaeoanthropology and rock-art studies. It complements the nearby Cradle of Humankind by placing the fossil evidence within a wider story of human creativity and culture. The museum incorporates work by contemporary artists alongside ancient material. It has become an important venue for public engagement with the science of human origins.

name: Iziko South African National Gallery
description: The Iziko South African National Gallery in Cape Town is the country's premier art museum, set in the leafy Company's Garden beneath Table Mountain. Its collections span historical South African painting and sculpture, contemporary African art, and works on paper from Europe and beyond. The gallery is known for thought-provoking exhibitions that engage with the country's complex history and identity. Its location among museums and gardens makes it a centrepiece of Cape Town's cultural quarter.
history: The gallery's origins lie in a 19th-century bequest of paintings, and the present building opened in 1930. For much of the 20th century it focused on European art, but since the 1990s it has actively broadened its collection to reflect the full diversity of South African and African creativity. It now forms part of the Iziko Museums of South Africa group. The institution continues to reckon publicly with questions of representation and heritage.

name: Nelson Mandela National Museum
description: The Nelson Mandela Museum honours South Africa's first democratically elected president across sites in the Eastern Cape where he was born and raised. Its main exhibition is housed in the Bhunga Building in Mthatha, with further components at his childhood home of Qunu. The displays trace Mandela's life from rural boyhood through his long imprisonment to his presidency and global stature. Many of the exhibits are gifts presented to him from around the world.
history: The museum opened in 2000 at Mandela's own request that, rather than a static monument, it serve as a living institution benefiting the surrounding communities. Qunu, where Mandela spent his early years and was later buried, anchors the museum in the landscape that shaped him. The institution combines commemoration with development projects in one of the poorest regions of the country. It remains a place of pilgrimage for those inspired by his life.
photo_author: South African Tourism from South Africa
photo_author_orig: South African Tourism from South Africa

name: Sterkfontein Caves
description: The Sterkfontein Caves are the single most productive hominin fossil site in the world, lying at the heart of the Cradle of Humankind. Guided tours descend into a dramatic system of chambers and underground lakes where some of humanity's most important ancestors were discovered. The caves continue to yield fossils that push back and refine the human story. An adjacent visitor centre interprets the finds and the painstaking science of their excavation.
history: Lime miners first exposed the rich fossil deposits, and scientific excavation began in earnest in the 1930s. The 'Mrs Ples' Australopithecus skull was found here in 1947, and the near-complete 'Little Foot' skeleton, more than three million years old, was painstakingly extracted from the breccia over two decades. The site has produced hundreds of specimens spanning millions of years. Sterkfontein was central to the Cradle of Humankind's UNESCO inscription in 1999.

name: Blombos Cave
description: Blombos Cave is a modest coastal cave near Still Bay on South Africa's southern Cape coast that has transformed scientific understanding of the origins of the modern human mind. Excavations here have uncovered some of the earliest evidence of symbolic behaviour anywhere in the world. The finds include engraved ochre, shell beads and a 100,000-year-old pigment-processing toolkit. The cave overlooks the Indian Ocean from a steep coastal cliff and is an active research site rather than a public attraction.
history: Deposits at Blombos span roughly 70,000 to 100,000 years ago, a critical period in human cognitive evolution. The discovery of deliberately engraved ochre pieces, dated to about 75,000 years ago, provided striking evidence of abstract thought long before such behaviour was thought to exist. Marine-shell beads found there are among the oldest known personal ornaments. The site has reshaped debates about when and where modern human culture first emerged.

name: Border Cave
description: Border Cave sits high in the Lebombo Mountains on the boundary between South Africa and Eswatini, commanding sweeping views over the surrounding lowveld. Its deep sequence of deposits records human occupation stretching back hundreds of thousands of years. Excavations have revealed early burials, organic artefacts and rare evidence of plant and grass bedding. The cave is one of the key sites for understanding the long arc of human existence in southern Africa.
history: Among the most significant finds is the deliberate burial of an infant with a perforated shell ornament, dated to around 74,000 years ago. The famous Lebombo bone, a baboon fibula marked with notches and sometimes interpreted as an early tally stick, was recovered here. Layers of fossilised bedding show that people were managing their living spaces with surprising sophistication tens of thousands of years ago. The site continues to inform research into early modern human behaviour.

name: Wonderwerk Cave
description: Wonderwerk Cave, near Kuruman in the Northern Cape, is a vast horizontal cavern extending some 140 metres into a hillside on the edge of the Kalahari. Its name means 'miracle' in Afrikaans, fitting for a site that preserves one of the longest continuous records of human occupation known anywhere. The cave's deep floor deposits span almost two million years. Rock engravings and paintings adorn parts of the interior, adding to its layered human story.
history: Excavations have revealed what may be the earliest controlled use of fire by human ancestors, dated to roughly one million years ago, in the form of ash and burnt bone deep inside the cave. The deposits document the entire Stone Age sequence in southern Africa. Hunter-gatherers, and later herders, used the cave over an immense span of time. Its remarkable preservation makes Wonderwerk a touchstone for research into early human behaviour.

name: Makapansgat
description: Makapansgat is a complex of caves and a steep dolomite valley in Limpopo province, rich in both fossils and history. The Makapansgat limeworks have yielded important australopithecine fossils, helping to establish the antiquity of early hominins in the region. The valley also holds the famous Makapansgat pebble, a naturally face-like stone thought to have been collected by an early hominin. The site combines palaeontological importance with a dramatic and historically charged landscape.
history: The fossil deposits at Makapansgat date back more than three million years and were among the finds that helped confirm Africa as the cradle of humankind. The valley is also remembered for the 1854 siege in which the Kekana people sheltered in the caves during a brutal conflict with Boer commandos. Excavations through the 20th century established its scientific significance. The area is now protected as part of South Africa's rich record of human origins.

name: Twyfelfontein
description: Twyfelfontein, or /Ui-//aes, lies in the arid Damaraland of north-western Namibia and holds one of the largest concentrations of rock engravings in Africa. Thousands of petroglyphs are pecked into the red sandstone slabs, depicting rhinoceros, elephant, giraffe, lion and abstract motifs. A perennial spring once made this an important gathering place in an otherwise harsh desert. Guided walks lead visitors among the engraved panels in their dramatic rocky setting.
history: The engravings were made over a period of at least 2,000 years by San hunter-gatherer communities, with some images possibly far older. They are believed to be linked to shamanic rituals and the recording of important waterholes and animals. The site's Afrikaans name, meaning 'doubtful spring', reflects the uncertain reliability of its water for later farmers. Twyfelfontein became Namibia's first UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2007.

name: Skeleton Coast National Park
description: The Skeleton Coast is one of the most remote and forbidding shorelines on Earth, stretching along Namibia's Atlantic edge where desert dunes meet a cold, fog-bound sea. Its name comes from the whale and seal bones that once littered the beaches, and the wrecks of ships driven ashore by treacherous currents. Despite its desolation it teems with life adapted to extremes, from desert lions and brown hyenas to vast seal colonies. Fly-in safaris and guided expeditions reveal a landscape of stark, otherworldly beauty.
history: The cold Benguela current generates the persistent fog that has doomed countless vessels along this coast over the centuries. Indigenous Himba and San peoples knew its harsh patterns, while shipwrecked sailors faced near-impossible journeys inland for water. The Portuguese called it 'the Gates of Hell'. Namibia proclaimed the park in 1971, protecting both its eerie scenery and its surprisingly resilient desert ecosystems.

name: Fish River Canyon
description: Fish River Canyon in southern Namibia is one of the largest canyons in the world, a colossal gash up to 27 kilometres wide and 550 metres deep carved into the desert plateau. Viewpoints along the rim reveal a vast, fossilised landscape of meandering gorges glowing in shades of ochre and grey. The challenging multi-day Fish River hiking trail descends into the canyon and follows its dry riverbed past hot springs. Its scale and silence make it one of the great natural spectacles of southern Africa.
history: The canyon was formed over hundreds of millions of years by a combination of water erosion and the collapse of the valley along ancient fault lines. The Fish River, Namibia's longest, now flows only seasonally but once cut relentlessly through the hard rock. Indigenous peoples and later travellers used its few reliable water sources. The canyon and its surroundings are protected within the Ai-Ais/Richtersveld Transfrontier Park shared with South Africa.

name: Namib Sand Sea
description: The Namib Sand Sea is a vast field of star-shaped dunes along the Namibian coast, among the oldest and highest sand dunes on the planet. At Sossusvlei and Deadvlei, towering apricot-coloured dunes rise above white clay pans dotted with the blackened skeletons of ancient camelthorn trees. Coastal fog rolling in from the Atlantic sustains a surprising web of specialised desert life. The dramatic light and immense scale make it one of the world's most photographed landscapes.
history: The Namib is considered the oldest desert on Earth, having existed in arid or semi-arid form for some 55 million years. The sand is carried by the Orange River to the coast and then blown inland by the wind to build the great dune field. The dead trees of Deadvlei perished centuries ago when shifting dunes cut off the river that once nourished them. UNESCO inscribed the Namib Sand Sea in 2013 as the only coastal desert in the world shaped by fog.
photo_author_orig: Jeff Schmaltz, MODIS Land Rapid Response Team, NASA GSFC

name: Chobe National Park
description: Chobe National Park in northern Botswana is famous for hosting one of the greatest concentrations of elephants in Africa, with herds numbering in the tens of thousands. The Chobe River frontage draws enormous numbers of animals to drink and bathe, especially in the dry season, creating spectacular wildlife viewing from boats. Beyond elephants, the park supports buffalo, lion, hippo, crocodile and prolific birdlife. River cruises at sunset are among the iconic experiences of southern African safari.
history: The area was set aside as a game reserve in 1961 and proclaimed a national park in 1967, becoming Botswana's first. It protects four distinct ecosystems, from the lush riverfront to dry inland savanna. Conservation efforts, combined with Botswana's strong anti-poaching stance, allowed elephant numbers to grow dramatically. The park lies within the larger Kavango-Zambezi transfrontier conservation area linking five countries.

name: Tsodilo Hills
description: The Tsodilo Hills rise abruptly from the flat Kalahari sands of north-western Botswana, four quartzite outcrops that the San revere as a sacred place. They hold one of the highest concentrations of rock art in the world, with more than 4,500 paintings preserved across the hills. Local communities call the formations the Male, the Female, the Child and an unnamed elder, reflecting deep spiritual associations. Visitors hike marked trails to view the paintings and absorb the powerful atmosphere of the site.
history: People have lived around the Tsodilo Hills for at least 100,000 years, leaving traces of mining, settlement and ritual. The paintings, made over many millennia, depict animals, human figures and geometric forms believed to be linked to San and later Bantu-speaking spiritual practice. The San regard the hills as a resting place of ancestral spirits and the site of creation. UNESCO inscribed Tsodilo in 2001 as the 'Louvre of the Desert'.

name: Central Kalahari Game Reserve
description: The Central Kalahari Game Reserve is one of the largest protected areas in the world, a vast expanse of semi-arid savanna, grassland and ancient fossil river valleys in the heart of Botswana. Its emptiness and silence are part of its appeal, with horizons that stretch unbroken in every direction. After the summer rains, the Deception Valley fills with grazing herds and the predators that follow them, including the famous black-maned Kalahari lions. It offers a wild, self-reliant safari experience far from crowds.
history: The reserve was established in 1961, in part to protect the lands and lifeways of the San who had lived in the Kalahari for thousands of years. The relationship between the government and resident San communities later became the subject of prolonged legal disputes over residence and access. The Kalahari's seasonal cycles have shaped a finely balanced ecosystem of drought-adapted plants and animals. It remains one of Africa's last great wilderness areas.

name: Great Zimbabwe
description: Great Zimbabwe is the largest ancient stone structure in sub-Saharan Africa, a sprawling complex of mortarless granite walls in the southern highlands of Zimbabwe. Its towering Great Enclosure, with walls up to 11 metres high, and the hilltop Hill Complex testify to a powerful and wealthy medieval civilisation. The skill of the dry-stone masonry, fitted without mortar, still astonishes visitors today. The famous soapstone Zimbabwe Birds found here became the national emblem of the modern country.
history: Great Zimbabwe flourished between roughly the 11th and 15th centuries as the capital of a Shona kingdom that grew rich on cattle, gold and trade reaching the Indian Ocean coast. At its peak it may have housed up to 18,000 people. Colonial-era theories that attributed the ruins to outsiders have been firmly disproved; the site is unmistakably an African achievement. The nation of Zimbabwe took its name from the site at independence in 1980, and it was inscribed by UNESCO in 1986.
photo_author: Andrew Moore from Johannesburg, South Africa
photo_author_orig: Andrew Moore from Johannesburg, South Africa

name: Matobo Hills
description: The Matobo Hills south of Bulawayo are a haunting landscape of balancing granite boulders, whaleback domes and wooded valleys sacred to the peoples of the region. The hills shelter an exceptional density of San rock paintings and remain an active centre of spiritual and ritual life. They are home to a notable population of black and white rhino and one of the world's highest concentrations of black eagles. The grave of colonial figure Cecil Rhodes sits atop a hill he called 'World's View'.
history: People have lived among the Matobo Hills for tens of thousands of years, and the San left thousands of paintings in its caves and overhangs. The hills became central to the religion of the Ndebele and earlier peoples, who consulted oracles associated with the high god Mwari. The area saw fierce resistance during the colonial conquest of the 1890s. UNESCO inscribed the Matobo Hills in 2003 for both their rock art and their living cultural significance.

name: Hwange National Park
description: Hwange is Zimbabwe's largest national park, a vast expanse of Kalahari sand, teak woodland and open grassland in the country's west. It is renowned for its enormous elephant population and for the artificial waterholes, many pumped year-round, that concentrate game in the dry season. Lion, buffalo, giraffe, sable antelope and African wild dog are all found here. Hides overlooking the pans offer some of the finest waterhole game viewing in Africa.
history: The area was set aside as a game reserve in 1928 and became a national park, taking its name from a local Nhanzwa chief. Because the region has no permanent surface water, conservationists developed a network of boreholes and pumped pans that allowed wildlife to thrive. The park gained international attention in 2015 over the killing of a well-known lion named Cecil. It remains a stronghold for Africa's elephants and large predators.

name: Khami Ruins
description: The Khami Ruins, west of Bulawayo, are the impressive stone remains of the capital of the Torwa state that rose after the decline of Great Zimbabwe. The site is distinguished by its terraced platforms and beautifully decorated retaining walls patterned with chevron, herringbone and checkerboard designs. These dry-stone structures supported the dwellings of royalty above the surrounding settlement. Khami offers a quieter, less-visited counterpart to Great Zimbabwe in a scenic riverside setting.
history: Khami flourished between roughly 1450 and 1650 as the centre of the Torwa dynasty, heirs to the Great Zimbabwe tradition of stone building. Archaeological finds, including Chinese porcelain, Spanish silverware and European trade goods, show its connections to a wide world of commerce. The state eventually fell to invaders in the 17th century. UNESCO inscribed the Khami Ruins in 1986 as evidence of a sophisticated successor civilisation.

name: Mana Pools National Park
description: Mana Pools National Park lies along the Zambezi River in northern Zimbabwe, a remote floodplain wilderness famed for its towering trees and intimate wildlife encounters. The 'pools' are remnant channels of the river that hold water through the dry season, drawing elephant, buffalo, hippo and big cats. It is one of the few parks where visitors may walk unguided, lending a rare sense of immersion in the wild. Canoe safaris drift past basking crocodiles and herds gathered on the banks.
history: The name 'Mana' means 'four' in the Shona language, referring to the four large pools left by the shifting Zambezi. The area was set aside for conservation in the mid-20th century and proclaimed a national park in 1975. Its inaccessibility helped preserve a near-pristine ecosystem. UNESCO inscribed Mana Pools, together with the Sapi and Chewore safari areas, in 1984 for its wilderness and wildlife.

name: South Luangwa National Park
description: South Luangwa in eastern Zambia is one of the finest wildlife reserves in Africa and the birthplace of the modern walking safari. The Luangwa River and its oxbow lagoons sustain dense populations of game, including large herds of elephant and buffalo and a high density of leopard. Its diverse habitats range from riverine forest to open grassland and mopane woodland. Guided walks and night drives allow exceptionally close encounters with wildlife.
history: The Luangwa Valley was protected as game reserves from the 1930s and became a national park in 1972. The conservationist Norman Carr pioneered the walking safari here and helped establish a model of low-impact, guide-led tourism. The valley's wildlife was hard hit by poaching in the late 20th century but has recovered through dedicated conservation. South Luangwa remains a global benchmark for the safari experience.

name: Kafue National Park
description: Kafue is Zambia's oldest and largest national park, a vast wilderness roughly the size of Wales in the centre-west of the country. Its varied landscapes include the seasonally flooded Busanga Plains, miombo woodland and the broad Kafue River. The plains attract huge numbers of antelope and the predators that hunt them, including lion, leopard and cheetah. Its size and low visitor numbers make it one of the wildest safari destinations in southern Africa.
history: Kafue was established as a national park in 1950 under the guidance of the conservationist Norman Carr. Its enormous area has at times made it difficult to manage and protect, and parts suffered from poaching. Renewed conservation partnerships in recent years have begun to restore its wildlife and infrastructure. The park remains a cornerstone of Zambia's protected-area network.

name: Lower Zambezi National Park
description: Lower Zambezi National Park stretches along the northern bank of the Zambezi River opposite Zimbabwe's Mana Pools, forming a shared wilderness across the great river. A backdrop of escarpment mountains rises behind a floodplain rich in elephant, buffalo, lion and hippo. Canoeing and boating safaris on the Zambezi are among its signature experiences, gliding past wildlife at the water's edge. Its relative remoteness gives visitors a tranquil, uncrowded safari.
history: The land was once a private game reserve belonging to the Zambian president before being declared a national park in 1983. Its late protection meant it remained largely undeveloped, preserving a wild riverine ecosystem. Conservation here is closely tied to that of Mana Pools across the river. Together the two areas protect one of the most important stretches of the Zambezi valley.

name: Island of Mozambique
description: The Island of Mozambique is a small coral island connected to the mainland by a long bridge, once the capital of Portuguese East Africa. Its Stone Town is a dense, atmospheric maze of churches, mosques, a Hindu temple and grand colonial buildings reflecting centuries of Swahili, Arab, Indian and European exchange. The mighty Fort São Sebastião, among the oldest European forts in the southern hemisphere, guards the harbour. The island lent its name to the entire country of Mozambique.
history: Long a Swahili trading settlement, the island was reached by Vasco da Gama in 1498 and became a key Portuguese base on the route to India. For nearly four centuries it served as the capital of Portuguese East Africa, growing wealthy on trade in gold, ivory and enslaved people before declining when the capital moved to Lourenço Marques. Its layered architecture preserves this long cosmopolitan history. UNESCO inscribed the island in 1991 for its unique fusion of cultures.

name: Gorongosa National Park
description: Gorongosa National Park lies at the southern end of the Great Rift Valley in central Mozambique, a landscape of floodplains, savanna and forested mountains once celebrated as one of Africa's premier wildlife reserves. After near-total devastation, an ambitious restoration project has brought its lion, elephant, buffalo and antelope populations roaring back. The park is now also a hub of scientific research and community development. Its dramatic recovery has made it a symbol of hope for African conservation.
history: Gorongosa flourished as a game reserve and then national park through the mid-20th century, but Mozambique's long civil war from the 1970s to 1992 destroyed almost all of its large wildlife. A landmark partnership begun in 2008 between the Mozambican government and a private foundation set out to rebuild the entire ecosystem. Animals were reintroduced and protected, and local communities became partners in conservation. The project is now studied worldwide as a model of large-scale ecological restoration.

name: Bazaruto Archipelago
description: The Bazaruto Archipelago is a chain of idyllic islands off the coast of southern Mozambique, ringed by coral reefs and turquoise waters. Towering vegetated dunes, freshwater lakes and white beaches give the islands a distinctive beauty. The surrounding marine park protects dugongs, dolphins, whale sharks and migrating humpback whales, along with vibrant reef ecosystems. It is a sought-after destination for diving, snorkelling and beach escapes.
history: The islands have long been used by coastal fishing communities and lay on ancient Indian Ocean trade routes. The waters around Bazaruto harbour one of the last viable populations of dugong in the western Indian Ocean. The archipelago was proclaimed a national park in 1971, with protection later extended to the surrounding sea. Conservation now balances tourism, fishing livelihoods and the safeguarding of fragile marine life.
photo_author: Earth Science and Remote Sensing Unit, NASA Johnson Space Center
photo_author_orig: Earth Science and Remote Sensing Unit, NASA Johnson Space Center

name: Sehlabathebe National Park
description: Sehlabathebe is Lesotho's oldest and most remote national park, perched high in the Maloti mountains near the border with South Africa. Its landscape of rolling alpine grassland, weathered sandstone formations and clear tarns has an austere, windswept beauty. The park shelters the rare Maloti minnow and the endangered bearded vulture, along with grazing herds and abundant wildflowers in summer. Its isolation rewards hikers and riders with profound solitude.
history: The high Maloti grasslands have been used for centuries by Basotho herders moving livestock to summer pastures, and San rock art survives in the area. The park was proclaimed in 1969, becoming Lesotho's first. In 2013 it was added to South Africa's uKhahlamba-Drakensberg site to form the Maloti-Drakensberg transboundary World Heritage Site. It protects a fragile mountain ecosystem and an important water catchment.

name: Ts'ehlanyane National Park
description: Ts'ehlanyane National Park lies in a deep valley of the Maloti mountains in northern Lesotho, protecting one of the country's few remaining patches of indigenous high-altitude forest. The rare ouhout and berg bamboo woodlands cling to the rugged slopes alongside rivers and waterfalls. The park offers excellent hiking and pony trekking through dramatic mountain scenery. Its cool, moist gorges contrast with the open grasslands typical of the kingdom.
history: The high mountains of Lesotho have sustained Basotho herding communities for generations, and the surviving forest pockets are remnants of vegetation that once covered more of the highlands. The park was established to conserve this threatened woodland and its associated wildlife. It forms part of a broader effort to protect the Maloti-Drakensberg's biodiversity and water resources. Sustainable tourism supports the surrounding communities.

name: Hlane Royal National Park
description: Hlane Royal National Park is the largest protected area in Eswatini, a swathe of ancient hardwood and acacia savanna once reserved as a royal hunting ground. It is home to the kingdom's largest populations of elephant, lion, rhino and an impressive variety of birds, including a notable concentration of vultures. Game drives and self-guided trails bring visitors close to wildlife around its waterholes. Its name means 'wilderness' in siSwati.
history: The land was traditionally a royal hunting reserve of the Swazi monarchy, which helped preserve its wildlife and woodlands. It was formally established as a protected area in the 20th century and is held in trust for the nation. Conservation has reintroduced species such as lion and elephant that had been lost from the region. The park combines cultural heritage with the protection of Eswatini's natural wealth.

name: Malolotja Nature Reserve
description: Malolotja Nature Reserve protects a spectacular swathe of mountainous highveld grassland in north-western Eswatini. Deep valleys, tumbling rivers and the dramatic Malolotja Falls make it one of the most scenic wilderness areas in the kingdom. The reserve shelters rare highland birds, including blue swallows and bald ibis, and a rich diversity of wildflowers. A canopy zip-line tour and an extensive network of hiking trails draw adventurous visitors.
history: The reserve includes Ngwenya Mountain, site of the Lion Cavern, where hematite and specularite were mined as long as 43,000 years ago — among the oldest known mines in the world. The grasslands have been grazed and used by people for millennia. Malolotja was proclaimed as a protected area to conserve its montane ecosystems and water catchments. It remains one of Africa's best-preserved highland wildernesses.

name: Stone Town of Zanzibar
description: Stone Town is the historic heart of Zanzibar City, a labyrinth of narrow lanes, coral-stone buildings and ornately carved wooden doors on an island off the Tanzanian coast. Its architecture blends Swahili, Arab, Persian, Indian and European influences accumulated over centuries of Indian Ocean trade. Landmarks include the House of Wonders, the Old Fort, and the site of the former slave market. The town's vibrant markets, mosques and seafront are a sensory immersion in Swahili culture.
history: Zanzibar grew rich on the trade in spices, ivory and enslaved people, and in the 19th century became the seat of the Sultanate of Oman in East Africa. Stone Town was a major hub where African, Arab and Asian worlds met and mingled. The island was the birthplace of the musician Freddie Mercury and a centre of the East African slave trade until its abolition. UNESCO inscribed Stone Town in 2000 for its outstanding example of a Swahili coastal trading town.

name: Kilwa Kisiwani
description: Kilwa Kisiwani is a small island off the southern coast of Tanzania that was once one of the most powerful trading cities of the medieval Swahili world. Its evocative ruins include the Great Mosque, one of the earliest surviving mosques on the East African coast, and the fortified Husuni Kubwa palace. The coral-stone structures speak of a city that controlled the gold trade of the western Indian Ocean. Today the quiet, atmospheric ruins are reached by boat from the mainland.
history: From around the 11th to the 16th century Kilwa dominated the trade in gold flowing from inland Africa to Arabia, Persia, India and beyond. The Arab traveller Ibn Battuta praised the city's beauty during his 14th-century visit. The arrival of the Portuguese in the early 1500s broke Kilwa's monopoly and began its decline. UNESCO inscribed Kilwa Kisiwani, together with nearby Songo Mnara, in 1981 as evidence of the wealth of Swahili civilisation.

name: Tarangire National Park
description: Tarangire National Park in northern Tanzania is famous for its ancient baobab trees and its large dry-season concentrations of elephants. The Tarangire River draws huge numbers of animals as other water sources dry up, creating spectacular wildlife gatherings. The park's gently rolling savanna and woodland support lion, leopard, giraffe and an outstanding diversity of birds. It offers a quieter alternative to the more famous northern parks while still delivering classic safari scenes.
history: The area was set aside as a game reserve and proclaimed a national park in 1970. Its seasonal river has long been a lifeline for wildlife migrating across the wider Maasai Steppe ecosystem. Conservation has worked to keep migration corridors open between the park and surrounding lands. Tarangire forms part of Tanzania's celebrated northern safari circuit.

name: Ruaha National Park
description: Ruaha is Tanzania's largest national park, a vast and remote wilderness in the centre of the country where eastern and southern African species overlap. The Great Ruaha River sustains huge herds of elephant and buffalo along with one of Africa's most important lion populations. Its rugged landscape of baobab-studded hills and open plains sees relatively few visitors. The park is prized by those seeking a wild, untrammelled safari.
history: The area was protected as the Rungwa Game Reserve before being upgraded to a national park in 1964 and later greatly expanded. Its isolation has helped preserve large predator populations that have declined elsewhere. The Great Ruaha River, however, has faced pressures from upstream water use that affect the ecosystem. Conservation efforts focus on protecting both the wildlife and the river that sustains it.

name: Lake Manyara National Park
description: Lake Manyara National Park occupies a narrow strip between the steep wall of the Great Rift Valley and the shallow soda lake of the same name in northern Tanzania. Despite its small size it packs in remarkable diversity, from groundwater forest and open grassland to the lakeshore. It is famous for its tree-climbing lions and the flocks of flamingos that gather on the lake. Elephant, hippo, baboon troops and abundant birdlife make it a rewarding stop on the northern circuit.
history: The park was established in 1960 to protect the lake and its surrounding habitats beneath the Rift escarpment. The writer Ernest Hemingway praised the beauty of the area, helping to popularise it. Lake levels rise and fall dramatically with the seasons and longer climate cycles, reshaping the habitat. The park forms part of a biosphere reserve recognising its ecological importance.

name: Maasai Mara National Reserve
description: The Maasai Mara in south-western Kenya is one of Africa's most celebrated wildlife reserves, an open expanse of golden grassland that forms the northern reach of the Serengeti ecosystem. It is the stage for the dramatic river crossings of the annual wildebeest migration, when millions of animals brave crocodile-filled rivers. The Mara holds exceptional densities of lion, cheetah, leopard and elephant year-round. Hot-air balloon safaris over the plains at dawn are an iconic experience.
history: The reserve takes its name from the Maasai people, who have herded cattle across these plains for centuries, and from the Mara River that runs through it. It was established as a protected area in the 1960s and is managed by local county authorities rather than the national parks service. Community conservancies on its borders have expanded protected habitat and given the Maasai a stake in conservation. The migration that defines it depends on keeping corridors open across the Kenya-Tanzania border.

name: Lamu Old Town
description: Lamu Old Town on Kenya's northern coast is the oldest and best-preserved Swahili settlement in East Africa, a living town still inhabited and largely free of motor vehicles. Its narrow streets wind between coral-stone houses with carved doors, inner courtyards and rooftop terraces cooled by ocean breezes. Donkeys and dhows remain the principal means of transport, lending the town an unhurried, timeless character. The annual Lamu Cultural Festival celebrates its Swahili heritage with dhow races and music.
history: Lamu has been continuously inhabited for over 700 years and was an important port in the network of Swahili city-states trading across the Indian Ocean. Its culture fused African, Arab, Persian and Indian influences into a distinctive Swahili civilisation. The town avoided the heavy redevelopment that altered other coastal centres, preserving its historic fabric. UNESCO inscribed Lamu Old Town in 2001 as an outstanding example of Swahili architecture and urban life.

name: Tsavo East National Park
description: Tsavo East is one of the largest national parks in Kenya, a vast, semi-arid wilderness of open plains, scrub and the meandering Galana River. It is famous for its 'red elephants', which take on a rusty hue from dust-bathing in the park's distinctive red soil. The dramatic Yatta Plateau, one of the world's longest lava flows, runs through the landscape. Its scale and big-sky scenery offer a wild contrast to Kenya's smaller, busier reserves.
history: The Tsavo region became notorious in 1898 for the man-eating lions that disrupted the building of the Uganda Railway. The park was established in 1948 and later divided administratively into Tsavo East and Tsavo West. In the late 20th century its elephants were devastated by poaching, but populations have since recovered under improved protection. Tsavo remains one of Kenya's most important elephant strongholds.

name: Fort Jesus, Mombasa
description: Fort Jesus dominates the entrance to Mombasa's Old Harbour, a massive coral-stone fortress built by the Portuguese to control trade on the Swahili coast. Its angular bastions, designed in the latest Renaissance military style, enclose a complex of ramparts, cisterns and quarters now housing a museum. The fort's walls bear graffiti and inscriptions left by the many peoples who held it over the centuries. From its ramparts visitors look out over the Indian Ocean and the historic Old Town.
history: The Portuguese built Fort Jesus between 1593 and 1596 to secure their grip on the lucrative East African trade routes. Over the following centuries it changed hands repeatedly between Portuguese and Omani Arab forces, surviving a famous siege lasting nearly three years. It later served as a prison under British colonial rule. UNESCO inscribed Fort Jesus in 2011 as an outstanding example of 16th-century Portuguese military architecture.

name: Lake Nakuru National Park
description: Lake Nakuru National Park surrounds a shallow alkaline lake in Kenya's Rift Valley, long famous for the immense flocks of flamingos that once turned its shores pink. Beyond the birds, the fenced sanctuary is an important refuge for both black and white rhino, as well as Rothschild's giraffe, lion and leopard. Acacia woodland, grassland and rocky escarpments ring the lake. Viewpoints such as Baboon Cliff offer sweeping panoramas of the soda lake below.
history: The lake was protected as a bird sanctuary from the 1960s before becoming a national park, in part to conserve its spectacular flamingo populations. Fluctuating water levels, linked to climate and surrounding land use, have caused the flamingos to move between Rift Valley lakes over time. The park became a key rhino sanctuary as Kenya battled to save the species from poaching. It remains a flagship of the country's conservation efforts.

name: Queen Elizabeth National Park
description: Queen Elizabeth National Park in western Uganda spreads across savanna, wetlands, crater lakes and forest beneath the snow-capped Rwenzori Mountains. The Kazinga Channel linking lakes George and Edward teems with hippos, elephants and waterbirds, best seen on a boat cruise. The park is famous for the tree-climbing lions of its remote Ishasha sector. Its extraordinary range of habitats supports nearly 100 mammal species and a remarkable diversity of birds.
history: The park was established in 1952 and renamed two years later to mark a visit by Queen Elizabeth II. It sits within a region shaped by ancient volcanic activity, dotted with explosion craters. Decades of conflict and poaching in the late 20th century reduced its wildlife, which has since recovered. Today it is one of Uganda's most popular and biodiverse protected areas.
