name: Murchison Falls National Park
description: Murchison Falls is Uganda's largest national park, named for the spectacular cascade where the Nile is forced through a gap just seven metres wide before plunging into the valley below. Boat trips up the river to the foot of the falls pass crocodiles, hippos and elephants gathered on the banks. The surrounding savanna supports lion, giraffe, buffalo and a wealth of birds, including the prehistoric-looking shoebill. The thunder of the falls is one of the great spectacles of the Nile.
history: The falls were named by the explorer Samuel Baker in 1864 after the president of the Royal Geographical Society. The park was established in 1952 and was once among the most wildlife-rich in Africa. Poaching and instability during the 1970s and 1980s severely reduced its animal populations, which have since rebounded. The park is now central to Uganda's tourism and conservation.

name: Akagera National Park
description: Akagera National Park protects a swathe of savanna, wetland and lakes in eastern Rwanda along the border with Tanzania. It is the country's only big-game park and, following an ambitious restoration, once again hosts the Big Five. Rolling acacia woodland gives way to a string of papyrus-fringed lakes that draw elephants, hippos and a spectacular array of birds. Boat safaris and game drives reveal a landscape very different from Rwanda's famous misty highlands.
history: The park was established in 1934 but suffered greatly after the 1994 genocide, when returning refugees settled within its boundaries and wildlife was decimated. A management partnership begun in 2010 transformed its fortunes, reintroducing lions in 2015 and rhinos in 2017. Community engagement and effective anti-poaching turned Akagera into a conservation success story. It now generates significant revenue that supports both the park and neighbouring communities.

name: Simien Mountains National Park
description: The Simien Mountains in northern Ethiopia form one of the most dramatic landscapes in Africa, a high plateau dissected by sheer escarpments and pinnacles often described as a 'chess game of the gods'. The park protects rare wildlife found nowhere else, including the gelada with its distinctive chest patch, the endangered Ethiopian wolf and the walia ibex. Trekking routes follow the edge of vertiginous cliffs with views plunging thousands of metres. The thin mountain air and vast horizons create an unforgettable highland wilderness.
history: The Simiens were shaped over millions of years by erosion of a vast volcanic massif, leaving the jagged escarpments seen today. The mountains have long been home to highland farming communities cultivating the steep slopes. The park was established in 1969 to protect its unique fauna, and was among the first sites inscribed by UNESCO in 1978. Conservation now works to balance the needs of local people with the protection of the endemic wildlife.

name: Fasil Ghebbi, Gondar
description: Fasil Ghebbi is a fortified royal compound in the city of Gondar, once the capital of imperial Ethiopia. Within its walls stand a remarkable collection of castles, palaces, churches and a bath, built by successive emperors. The architecture fuses Ethiopian traditions with Portuguese, Indian and Moorish influences in a style unlike anywhere else. The stone castles rising from the highland landscape have earned Gondar the nickname 'the Camelot of Africa'.
history: Emperor Fasilides founded Gondar as a permanent capital in 1636, ending centuries of itinerant royal courts. His successors added further palaces and churches within the compound over the following two centuries. The city flourished as a centre of art, religion and learning before its decline in the 19th century. UNESCO inscribed Fasil Ghebbi in 1979 as a testament to Ethiopia's distinctive royal architecture.

name: Harar Jugol
description: Harar Jugol is the walled old city of Harar in eastern Ethiopia, considered by many Muslims to be the fourth-holiest city of Islam. Within its ancient defensive walls lie a dense maze of alleyways, distinctive multicoloured houses and dozens of mosques and shrines. The city is famous for its tradition of feeding wild hyenas by hand at night, a practice that has become a celebrated local custom. Its markets and crafts reflect a vibrant blend of African and Islamic culture.
history: Harar emerged as an important Islamic centre and trading hub from the medieval period and was an independent emirate for several centuries. Its protective walls, built between the 13th and 16th centuries, contain five historic gates. The French poet Arthur Rimbaud spent his final years here as a trader. UNESCO inscribed Harar Jugol in 2006 for its unique urban fabric and its standing as a centre of Islamic learning and culture in the Horn of Africa.

name: Lower Awash Valley (Hadar)
description: The Lower Awash Valley in the Afar region of Ethiopia is one of the most important palaeoanthropological sites in the world. Its eroded badlands have preserved a remarkable sequence of hominin fossils spanning millions of years. It was here, at Hadar, that the famous early human ancestor 'Lucy' was discovered. The harsh, sun-baked landscape continues to yield discoveries that illuminate the origins of humanity.
history: In 1974 a team led by Donald Johanson uncovered the partial skeleton of an Australopithecus afarensis, nicknamed Lucy and dated to about 3.2 million years ago, which transformed understanding of human evolution. Further finds, including the remains nicknamed the 'First Family', came from the same deposits. The valley's geology, with its layers of volcanic ash, allows precise dating of the fossils. UNESCO inscribed the Lower Valley of the Awash in 1980 for its outstanding evidence of human origins.

name: Djenné
description: Djenné, in central Mali, is celebrated for the Great Mosque, the largest mud-brick building in the world and a masterpiece of Sudano-Sahelian architecture. The mosque's earthen walls, studded with protruding wooden beams, are re-plastered each year in a festive community event. The surrounding old town preserves hundreds of traditional adobe houses along its sandy streets. Djenné's Monday market, held in the shadow of the mosque, is one of the most vibrant in West Africa.
history: Djenné and the nearby ancient site of Djenné-Djeno were among the oldest urban centres in sub-Saharan Africa, flourishing on trade across the Niger River from around the 9th century. The town became a centre of Islamic learning and a key link in the trans-Saharan gold and salt trade. The present Great Mosque was rebuilt in 1907 on the site of a much older structure. UNESCO inscribed the Old Towns of Djenné in 1988 for their architectural and historical importance.

name: Pyramids of Meroë
description: The Pyramids of Meroë rise from the desert beside the Nile in Sudan, a field of more than 200 steep-sided royal tombs unlike the larger pyramids of Egypt. Their narrow, sharply angled forms and small offering chapels mark the burial places of the kings and queens of the Kushite kingdom. Wind-rippled dunes lap against the ancient stones, often with hardly another visitor in sight. The site is one of the most evocative and least-known wonders of the ancient world.
history: Meroë was the capital of the Kingdom of Kush from around 300 BCE to 350 CE, an African civilisation that for a time even ruled Egypt as its 25th dynasty. The kingdom developed its own script, the still partly undeciphered Meroitic, and grew wealthy from iron-working and trade. Its rulers, including powerful queens known as Kandakes, were buried beneath the pyramids. UNESCO inscribed the Island of Meroe in 2011 for its outstanding testimony to Kushite civilisation.

name: Medina of Marrakesh
description: The medina of Marrakesh is a walled old city of red-earth ramparts, bustling souks and historic monuments at the foot of the Atlas Mountains. At its heart lies the great square of Jemaa el-Fnaa, which by night fills with food stalls, musicians, storytellers and snake charmers. Landmarks include the soaring Koutoubia minaret, the Saadian Tombs and the Bahia Palace. The labyrinth of markets offers spices, leather, lanterns and carpets in a sensory whirl.
history: Marrakesh was founded in 1070 by the Almoravids and became a great imperial capital, lending its name to the whole country of Morocco. Successive dynasties endowed it with mosques, palaces and gardens, and it long served as a southern trading hub linked to the Sahara. The Koutoubia Mosque, completed in the 12th century, remains its defining landmark. UNESCO inscribed the medina in 1985 for its outstanding Islamic architecture and living urban culture.

name: Aït Benhaddou
description: Aït Benhaddou is a fortified earthen village, or ksar, rising in tiered ochre against the foothills of the Atlas Mountains in southern Morocco. Its closely packed kasbahs of rammed earth and mud-brick climb a hillside above a river, crowned by a granary at the summit. The dramatic silhouette has made it one of the most photographed sites in Morocco and a favourite film location. A handful of families still live within the ancient walls.
history: The ksar grew along the old caravan route between the Sahara and Marrakesh, where traders crossed the Atlas with gold, salt and slaves. Its earthen architecture, vulnerable to rain and time, has required continual maintenance over the centuries. Its evocative appearance has drawn film-makers for productions ranging from epics to fantasy series. UNESCO inscribed Aït Benhaddou in 1987 as an outstanding example of southern Moroccan earthen architecture.

name: Carthage
description: The ruins of ancient Carthage spread across a coastal suburb of modern Tunis, overlooking the Gulf of Tunis. Visitors can explore the Antonine Baths, Punic ports, Roman villas, and the hilltop where the city's citadel once stood. Though much was destroyed in antiquity, the surviving remains evoke one of the greatest powers of the ancient Mediterranean. The site combines layers of Phoenician, Roman, and early Christian history in a dramatic seaside setting.
history: Founded by Phoenician settlers from Tyre, traditionally in 814 BCE, Carthage grew into a maritime empire that rivalled Rome. The two powers fought the three Punic Wars, culminating in Carthage's total destruction in 146 BCE. Rome later rebuilt the city, which became one of the largest in the empire and an important early Christian centre. UNESCO inscribed the site of Carthage in 1979 for its layered Mediterranean history.

name: Historic Centre of Rome
description: The historic centre of Rome is an open-air museum spanning nearly three millennia of Western civilisation. Within a compact area stand the Colosseum, the Roman Forum, the Pantheon and countless churches, fountains and palaces. The layering of ancient, medieval, Renaissance and Baroque Rome makes every street a journey through time. It remains a living capital where ruins and modern life intertwine.
history: According to legend Rome was founded in 753 BCE, growing into the centre of an empire that ruled much of the known world. After the empire's fall the city became the seat of the papacy, which rebuilt it with grand churches and palaces. The artistic flowering of the Renaissance and Baroque eras gave Rome much of its present splendour. UNESCO inscribed the historic centre in 1980, recognising its unmatched concentration of human achievement.

name: Venice and its Lagoon
description: Venice is a city built upon water, a network of more than a hundred islands threaded by canals and linked by bridges in a shallow Adriatic lagoon. Its palaces, churches and the great square of St Mark's rise directly from the water, reflecting the wealth of a former maritime empire. Gondolas and water buses replace cars on the canals that serve as streets. The whole improbable city is both an artistic treasure and a triumph of human ingenuity over a precarious environment.
history: Refugees fleeing mainland invasions settled the lagoon islands in the early medieval period, and Venice grew into a dominant trading power between East and West. For centuries the Venetian Republic controlled commerce across the Mediterranean and amassed extraordinary art and architecture. Its decline came with shifting trade routes and conquest, but its beauty endured. UNESCO inscribed Venice and its Lagoon in 1987, even as rising seas threaten its future.

name: Historic Centre of Florence
description: Florence is the cradle of the Renaissance, a compact city where art and architecture reached new heights in the 14th to 16th centuries. Its skyline is dominated by Brunelleschi's vast brick dome atop the cathedral, beside Giotto's bell tower and the Baptistery. The Uffizi, Ponte Vecchio and Palazzo Vecchio anchor a centre packed with masterpieces. Walking its streets means encountering the legacy of Dante, Michelangelo, Leonardo and the Medici at every turn.
history: Florence grew wealthy in the Middle Ages through banking and the wool trade, and the powerful Medici family became its patrons and rulers. Their support helped fuel an explosion of artistic and intellectual achievement that defined the Renaissance. The city was for a time the capital of a newly unified Italy in the 19th century. UNESCO inscribed the historic centre in 1982 as a unique artistic realisation.

name: Historic Centre of Prague
description: Prague's historic centre is one of the best-preserved medieval cityscapes in Europe, threaded by the Vltava River. Its skyline of spires, the famous Charles Bridge lined with statues, and the vast Prague Castle complex above the city create an atmosphere of fairy-tale beauty. The Old Town Square, with its astronomical clock and Gothic church towers, lies at the heart of the maze of cobbled streets. Layers of Romanesque, Gothic, Baroque and Art Nouveau architecture stand side by side.
history: Prague flourished as the capital of Bohemia and, under Emperor Charles IV in the 14th century, became a great imperial city of the Holy Roman Empire. It was a centre of religious reform, learning and, later, of European music and culture. The city escaped major destruction in the world wars, preserving its historic fabric remarkably intact. UNESCO inscribed the historic centre in 1992.

name: Historic Centre of Vienna
description: Vienna's historic centre reflects three eras of European grandeur, from medieval and Baroque splendour to the imperial capital of the Habsburgs. Magnificent palaces, the Gothic St Stephen's Cathedral, grand boulevards and concert halls fill the compact old city. It is inseparable from the music of Mozart, Beethoven, Schubert and the Strauss family who lived and worked here. Coffee houses, opera and a refined urban culture continue to define the city.
history: Vienna grew from a Roman frontier camp into the seat of the Habsburg dynasty, which ruled a vast central European empire for centuries. In the 19th century the old city walls were replaced by the grand Ringstrasse, lined with monumental public buildings. The city became the world capital of classical music and a centre of intellectual life around 1900. UNESCO inscribed the historic centre in 2001.

name: Tower of London
description: The Tower of London is a historic fortress on the north bank of the Thames at the heart of the British capital. Its central keep, the White Tower, has loomed over London for nearly a thousand years, surrounded by concentric walls and towers. The fortress has served as royal palace, prison, treasury and place of execution, and today guards the Crown Jewels. Yeomen Warders in distinctive uniforms and the resident ravens add to its enduring legend.
history: William the Conqueror began the White Tower around 1078 to dominate the newly conquered city of London. Successive monarchs expanded the fortress, which became notorious as a prison and place of execution for figures including two of Henry VIII's queens. It has held the Crown Jewels for centuries and survived bombing in the Second World War. UNESCO inscribed the Tower of London in 1988 as a symbol of Norman power and English history.

name: Edinburgh Old and New Towns
description: Edinburgh's Old and New Towns together form one of the most striking historic cityscapes in Britain. The medieval Old Town tumbles down a volcanic ridge from the castle along the Royal Mile, a dense warren of tall tenements and narrow closes. In contrast, the elegant Georgian New Town displays ordered streets, squares and classical façades from the Age of Enlightenment. The dramatic contrast between the two captures the city's distinctive character.
history: Edinburgh has been Scotland's capital since the 15th century, its Old Town crowded within defensive walls atop the castle rock. In the 18th century the cramped city expanded into the planned New Town, built during the intellectual ferment of the Scottish Enlightenment. The two areas embody very different eras of urban design. UNESCO inscribed the Old and New Towns of Edinburgh in 1995.

name: Brú na Bóinne (Newgrange)
description: Brú na Bóinne in Ireland's Boyne Valley is one of the most important prehistoric landscapes in Europe, dominated by the great passage tomb of Newgrange. The vast circular mound, ringed by kerbstones carved with spiral motifs, conceals a stone passage leading to a central chamber. Each year at the winter solstice, sunrise light streams through a roof box to illuminate the chamber's depths. Nearby Knowth and Dowth complete a remarkable group of Neolithic monuments.
history: Newgrange was built around 3200 BCE, predating Stonehenge and the Egyptian pyramids. The precise solstice alignment shows a sophisticated understanding of astronomy by its Neolithic builders. The tomb's megalithic art is among the finest in prehistoric Europe. UNESCO inscribed Brú na Bóinne in 1993 for its outstanding concentration of Neolithic monuments.

name: Kremlin and Red Square, Moscow
description: The Moscow Kremlin and adjacent Red Square form the symbolic and political heart of Russia. Behind the Kremlin's red brick walls and towers stand cathedrals with golden domes, palaces and the seat of government. Red Square is bordered by the fantastical, multicoloured domes of St Basil's Cathedral and the long façade of the GUM department store. The ensemble is one of the most recognisable urban spaces in the world.
history: A wooden fort stood on the Kremlin hill from the 12th century, rebuilt in stone and then brick as Moscow rose to dominate Russia. The cathedrals and palaces within were raised by grand princes and tsars from the 15th century onward. St Basil's was built in the 16th century to celebrate a military victory under Ivan the Terrible. UNESCO inscribed the Kremlin and Red Square in 1990 as the centre of Russian power and faith.

name: Historic Centre of Saint Petersburg
description: Saint Petersburg is one of the most beautiful planned cities in the world, laid out along the Neva River and a network of canals often compared to Venice. Grand palaces, cathedrals and the vast Winter Palace housing the Hermitage line its waterways and broad avenues. The harmonious Baroque and Neoclassical architecture reflects its origins as an imperial capital built to face Europe. During the summer 'white nights' the city glows in near-perpetual daylight.
history: Tsar Peter the Great founded the city in 1703 as Russia's 'window on the West' and moved the capital there from Moscow. Built on marshland at great human cost, it became the dazzling seat of the Russian Empire for two centuries. It witnessed the revolutions of 1917 and endured a devastating 900-day siege in the Second World War. UNESCO inscribed its historic centre in 1990.

name: Historic Centre of Kraków
description: Kraków's historic centre is one of the finest medieval cityscapes in Europe, centred on Europe's largest medieval market square. The Main Square is dominated by the Gothic St Mary's Basilica and the long Renaissance Cloth Hall, while the Wawel hill above holds the royal castle and cathedral. The compact old town, ringed by a green belt where the walls once stood, is alive with cafés, churches and history. It is one of the cultural capitals of Poland.
history: Kraków served as the capital of Poland for centuries and the coronation and burial place of its kings at Wawel Cathedral. The city was a major centre of learning, home to one of the oldest universities in central Europe, founded in 1364. It survived the Second World War largely intact, unlike many Polish cities. UNESCO inscribed the historic centre of Kraków in 1978 among its very first listings.

name: Bryggen, Bergen
description: Bryggen is the historic wharf of Bergen on the west coast of Norway, a row of brightly painted wooden gabled buildings facing the harbour. The narrow timber houses and passageways once housed the merchants and warehouses of a great trading port. Today they shelter craft workshops, shops and museums, retaining their distinctive medieval layout. The colourful waterfront is the enduring image of Bergen.
history: Bergen was a key trading town and, from the 14th century, an outpost of the Hanseatic League, the powerful network of German merchant towns. The wharf grew up around the lucrative trade in dried cod from northern Norway. Repeated fires destroyed and rebuilt the wooden buildings over the centuries while preserving the original plan. UNESCO inscribed Bryggen in 1979 as a reminder of the Hanseatic trading empire.

name: Geirangerfjord
description: The Geirangerfjord is one of the most spectacular fjords in Norway, a deep blue arm of water flanked by sheer cliffs rising thousands of feet. Waterfalls such as the Seven Sisters tumble dramatically down the rock faces into the fjord below. Abandoned farms cling to impossibly steep ledges high above the water. Cruise ships and sightseeing boats glide beneath the towering walls in summer.
history: The fjord was carved by glaciers over successive ice ages, gouging a steep-sided trench later flooded by the sea. Small farming communities once eked out a living on its precarious mountain shelves, accessible only by boat and ladder. The fjord became an early icon of Norwegian tourism in the 19th century. UNESCO inscribed the West Norwegian Fjords, including Geirangerfjord, in 2005 for their classic and outstanding beauty.

name: Old Town of Tallinn
description: The old town of Tallinn is among the best-preserved medieval cities in northern Europe, encircled by largely intact stone walls and towers. Cobbled streets wind between merchant houses, guild halls and Gothic churches up to the Toompea hill, where the castle and cathedral stand. The skyline of red-tiled roofs, spires and turrets evokes a Hanseatic past frozen in time. Its compact, atmospheric centre is the pride of the Estonian capital.
history: Tallinn rose as a key trading town of the Hanseatic League, linking Russia with western Europe across the Baltic. Its prosperity in the 13th to 16th centuries funded the walls, churches and merchant houses that survive today. The city changed hands among Danish, German, Swedish and Russian rulers over the centuries. UNESCO inscribed the historic centre of Tallinn in 1997.

name: Historic Areas of Istanbul
description: Istanbul straddles Europe and Asia across the Bosphorus, and its historic peninsula holds some of the world's greatest monuments. The vast domed Hagia Sophia, the Blue Mosque, Topkapı Palace and the ancient hippodrome stand close together in the old city. The skyline of domes and minarets above the Golden Horn captures the meeting of empires and faiths. The Grand Bazaar and bustling waterfront keep the historic centre vividly alive.
history: Founded as Byzantium and refounded as Constantinople by the emperor Constantine in 330 CE, the city was the capital of the Byzantine Empire for over a thousand years. It fell to the Ottomans in 1453 and became the heart of their empire, when churches such as Hagia Sophia were converted to mosques. The city's monuments reflect both Christian and Islamic imperial splendour. UNESCO inscribed the historic areas of Istanbul in 1985.

name: Pena Palace and Sintra
description: Sintra is a romantic hillside town near Lisbon, set among forested mountains dotted with palaces and gardens. The flamboyant Pena Palace crowns a peak in a riot of yellow, red and tiled towers, blending many architectural styles into a fairy-tale whole. Below lie the Moorish castle ruins, lush estates and the mysterious well of the Quinta da Regaleira. The cool, misty hills have drawn royalty and artists for centuries.
history: Sintra was long a favoured summer retreat of Portuguese royalty, who built and embellished palaces among its hills. The Pena Palace was created in the 19th century by King Ferdinand II as one of the earliest and most exuberant expressions of Romanticism in Europe. The town inspired writers including Lord Byron, who called it a 'glorious Eden'. UNESCO inscribed the Cultural Landscape of Sintra in 1995.

name: Historic Centre of Porto
description: Porto, Portugal's second city, rises in tiers above the Douro River where it meets the Atlantic. Its historic centre is a dense, atmospheric tangle of medieval lanes, Baroque churches and tiled façades climbing the steep riverbanks. The double-deck Dom Luís I Bridge spans the gorge to the port-wine lodges of Vila Nova de Gaia opposite. The riverside Ribeira district is the colourful heart of the old city.
history: Porto has been an important port since Roman times and gave its name to both the country and to port wine. The city grew wealthy on Atlantic trade and the export of fortified wine aged in the lodges across the river. Its merchants and guilds endowed it with fine churches and civic buildings over the centuries. UNESCO inscribed the historic centre of Porto in 1996.

name: Swiss National Park
description: The Swiss National Park in the eastern Alps is the country's only national park and one of the strictest nature reserves in Europe. Its forests, alpine meadows and rocky peaks are left almost entirely to nature, with visitors confined to marked trails. Red deer, chamois, ibex, marmots and bearded vultures roam the protected valleys. The park offers a rare chance to experience genuinely wild Alpine wilderness.
history: Founded in 1914, it was the first national park in the Alps and one of the earliest in Europe. Its guiding principle has always been strict non-intervention, allowing natural processes to unfold without human management. Over a century of protection has produced a valuable record of how Alpine ecosystems develop when left alone. The park is part of a UNESCO biosphere reserve.

name: Vatnajökull National Park
description: Vatnajökull National Park in Iceland is one of the largest national parks in Europe, built around the vast Vatnajökull ice cap. Beneath and around the glacier lie active volcanoes, thundering waterfalls, ice caves and black-sand plains shaped by fire and ice. Glacial lagoons studded with icebergs, such as Jökulsárlón, are among its most famous sights. The park showcases the raw geological forces that continue to build and reshape Iceland.
history: The park was created in 2008 by combining existing protected areas around the ice cap, and it now covers a large share of the country. The interplay of subglacial volcanic eruptions and glacial floods has carved a constantly changing landscape. The region has been shaped by eruptions recorded throughout Iceland's history. UNESCO inscribed Vatnajökull National Park in 2019 for its dynamic interaction of volcanoes and ice.

name: Cairngorms National Park
description: The Cairngorms National Park is the largest national park in Britain, protecting a vast highland landscape in the heart of Scotland. It encompasses the high Cairngorm plateau, ancient Caledonian pine forests, moorland and clear rivers. The park shelters rare wildlife including capercaillie, golden eagles, red squirrels and Scotland's only herd of wild reindeer. It is a haven for hill walkers, skiers and lovers of mountain wilderness.
history: The Cairngorm mountains were shaped by glaciers into rounded plateaux and deep glacial valleys. The surrounding straths have supported farming and the famous whisky distilleries of Speyside for generations. The national park was established in 2003 and later expanded, becoming the largest in the United Kingdom. It balances conservation with the working life of its highland communities.

name: Picos de Europa National Park
description: The Picos de Europa form a dramatic limestone massif rising steeply just inland from Spain's northern coast. Jagged peaks, deep gorges and high alpine pastures make this one of the most spectacular mountain ranges in the country. The Cares Gorge trail, cut into sheer cliffs above a rushing river, is among the finest walks in Europe. Chamois, vultures and the rare brown bear inhabit the wild upper reaches.
history: The name 'Peaks of Europe' is said to derive from their being the first sight of land for sailors returning from the Americas. The mountains sheltered the early Christian kingdom of Asturias, where resistance to Muslim expansion in Spain famously began at Covadonga in the 8th century. Spain's first national park was declared here in 1918 around Covadonga. The protected area was later expanded into the modern Picos de Europa National Park.

name: Forbidden City
description: The Forbidden City in the heart of Beijing is the largest and best-preserved palace complex in the world. Behind its high red walls and moat lie nearly a thousand surviving buildings arranged along a grand ceremonial axis. Golden-roofed halls, vast courtyards and intricate gardens once formed the exclusive world of the emperor. Now the Palace Museum, it draws enormous crowds eager to glimpse imperial China.
history: The palace was built between 1406 and 1420 and served as the home of Chinese emperors and the political centre of the country for nearly five centuries. Twenty-four emperors of the Ming and Qing dynasties ruled from within its walls, which ordinary people were forbidden to enter. The last emperor was expelled in 1924, and the complex became a public museum. UNESCO inscribed the Forbidden City in 1987.

name: Mausoleum of the First Qin Emperor (Terracotta Army)
description: The tomb complex of China's first emperor near Xi'an is guarded by the astonishing Terracotta Army, thousands of life-sized clay soldiers buried to protect him in the afterlife. Each figure is individually modelled, with distinct faces, hairstyles and armour, arranged in battle formation in vast pits. Horses, chariots and officials accompany the ranks of warriors. The discovery is one of the greatest archaeological finds of the 20th century.
history: The emperor Qin Shi Huang, who unified China in 221 BCE, ordered the construction of his colossal tomb complex over decades using a workforce of hundreds of thousands. The terracotta figures were intended to serve and guard him in death. The army lay hidden until 1974, when farmers digging a well stumbled upon the buried soldiers. UNESCO inscribed the mausoleum in 1987 as a monument of unique testimony to the first Chinese empire.

name: Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto
description: Kyoto was the imperial capital of Japan for over a thousand years and preserves an extraordinary concentration of historic temples, shrines and gardens. The golden pavilion of Kinkaku-ji, the rock garden of Ryoan-ji and the soaring wooden stage of Kiyomizu-dera are among its most famous sights. Refined Zen gardens, tea houses and the geisha district of Gion embody the essence of traditional Japanese culture. The seasonal beauty of cherry blossom and autumn maples enhances the historic setting.
history: Kyoto, then called Heian-kyo, became the imperial capital in 794 and remained the seat of the emperor until 1868. Over the centuries it became the centre of Japanese art, religion and courtly culture. The city was spared the atomic bombing of the Second World War, preserving its priceless heritage. UNESCO inscribed seventeen historic monuments of ancient Kyoto in 1994.

name: Himeji Castle
description: Himeji Castle is the finest surviving feudal castle in Japan, a brilliant white fortress often likened to a heron taking flight. Its main keep rises in elegant tiers of white plaster walls and curving gables atop a stone base. A complex maze of gates, courtyards and defensive passages was designed to confound attackers. It is one of the most beautiful and best-preserved examples of Japanese castle architecture.
history: The castle reached its present form in the early 17th century under the warlord Ikeda Terumasa, building on earlier fortifications. Remarkably, it survived the wars and bombings that destroyed most other Japanese castles. Extensive restoration completed in 2015 returned its plaster walls to their dazzling white. UNESCO inscribed Himeji Castle in 1993 as a masterpiece of wooden construction.

name: Mount Fuji
description: Mount Fuji is Japan's highest mountain and its most sacred and iconic natural symbol, a near-perfect volcanic cone often capped with snow. Rising in solitary majesty, it has inspired poets, pilgrims and artists for centuries, most famously in the prints of Hokusai. Tens of thousands climb to its summit each summer to watch the sunrise. Its graceful silhouette is visible from Tokyo on clear days.
history: Fuji is an active volcano whose last major eruption was in 1707, and it has been revered as a holy mountain in Shinto and Buddhist tradition for over a thousand years. Pilgrims have ascended its slopes for centuries, and shrines ring its base. The mountain's image is woven deeply into Japanese art and identity. UNESCO inscribed Fujisan in 2013 as a sacred place and source of artistic inspiration.

name: Bagan
description: Bagan in Myanmar is one of the richest archaeological sites in Asia, a vast plain studded with thousands of Buddhist temples and pagodas. The spires and domes of brick monuments stretch to the horizon, glowing red and gold at sunrise and sunset. Hot-air balloon flights and bicycle rides among the temples are unforgettable experiences. It rivals Angkor as one of the great temple landscapes of the world.
history: Bagan was the capital of the first kingdom to unify the regions of modern Myanmar, flourishing between the 11th and 13th centuries. Its rulers built more than ten thousand religious monuments during this golden age of Burmese Buddhism, of which over two thousand survive. The kingdom declined after Mongol invasions in the late 13th century. UNESCO inscribed Bagan in 2019 after long delays.

name: Sigiriya
description: Sigiriya is a dramatic rock fortress rising some 200 metres above the central plains of Sri Lanka. A royal palace once crowned its flat summit, reached by a stairway that passed between the paws of a colossal carved lion, from which the site takes its name. Vivid frescoes of celestial maidens adorn a sheltered rock face partway up. Landscaped water gardens at the base make it a marvel of ancient urban planning.
history: King Kashyapa built his fortified palace atop the rock in the 5th century CE after seizing the throne, fearing reprisal. After his death the site became a Buddhist monastery for several centuries before being abandoned. The frescoes and the mirror wall inscribed with ancient graffiti reveal its later visitors. UNESCO inscribed Sigiriya in 1982 as a masterpiece of ancient planning and art.

name: Bukhara
description: Bukhara in Uzbekistan is one of the great cities of the Silk Road, its old town a remarkably intact ensemble of mosques, madrasas and trading domes. The Po-i-Kalyan complex, with its towering minaret, and the ancient Ark fortress dominate the historic centre. Covered bazaars and caravanserais recall the city's role as a hub of commerce and learning. The earthen and tiled architecture glows beautifully in the desert light.
history: Bukhara has been inhabited for over two thousand years and became a major centre of Islamic scholarship, theology and culture, sometimes called the 'Pillar of Islam'. It flourished along the Silk Road, ruled by a succession of dynasties and khans. The city's historic core preserves its medieval form more completely than almost any other Central Asian city. UNESCO inscribed the historic centre of Bukhara in 1993.
photo_author_orig: Adam Jones from Kelowna, BC, Canada

name: Samarkand – Crossroad of Cultures
description: Samarkand is among the most legendary cities of the Silk Road, famed for the dazzling tiled monuments of its imperial age. The Registan, a square framed by three monumental madrasas with shimmering blue domes and portals, is one of the most magnificent public spaces in the world. The Gur-e-Amir mausoleum and the Bibi-Khanym Mosque add to its splendour. The intense turquoise tilework against the desert sky is unforgettable.
history: Samarkand prospered for over two millennia at the crossroads of trade between China, Persia and the Mediterranean. In the late 14th century it became the glittering capital of the conqueror Timur, who summoned artisans from across his empire to adorn it. The city was a centre of science under his grandson Ulugh Beg, an accomplished astronomer. UNESCO inscribed Samarkand as a 'crossroad of cultures' in 2001.
photo_author_orig:
Registan_-_Gusjer.jpg: Gustavo Jeronimo from Aranjuez, Spain
derivative work: MrPanyGoff

name: Hampi
description: Hampi in southern India is a vast and evocative ruined city scattered across a surreal landscape of giant granite boulders and the Tungabhadra River. Once the capital of a mighty empire, it preserves temples, palaces, markets and royal structures of extraordinary richness. The Virupaksha Temple still functions as a place of worship, while the stone chariot of the Vittala Temple is an icon of Indian art. The boulder-strewn setting gives the ruins a unique, otherworldly beauty.
history: Hampi was the capital of the Vijayanagara Empire, which dominated southern India from the 14th to the 16th century and grew immensely wealthy on trade. At its height it was one of the largest and richest cities in the world, visited by traders from Persia and Portugal. It was sacked and abandoned after a catastrophic defeat in 1565. UNESCO inscribed the Group of Monuments at Hampi in 1986.

name: Ajanta Caves
description: The Ajanta Caves in Maharashtra are a series of around thirty Buddhist cave-temples carved into a horseshoe-shaped cliff above a river gorge. They are celebrated above all for their ancient wall paintings, among the finest surviving examples of early Indian art. The murals depict the life of the Buddha and scenes of court and everyday life in glowing colour. Carved halls and shrines complete the spectacular rock-cut complex.
history: The caves were excavated in two phases, the earliest around the 2nd century BCE and the later, more elaborate group in the 5th century CE. They served as monasteries and prayer halls for Buddhist monks. The site was abandoned and forgotten until a British hunting party stumbled upon it in 1819. UNESCO inscribed the Ajanta Caves in 1983 for their masterpieces of Buddhist art.
