name: Khajuraho Group of Monuments
description: The temples of Khajuraho in central India are renowned for their exuberant and finely detailed sculpture covering every surface. Towering shikhara spires rise above shrines adorned with gods, celestial beings, dancers and famously sensuous figures. The intricate carving represents the high point of north Indian temple architecture. Only a fraction of the original temples survive, but they remain among the most remarkable in India.
history: The temples were built between roughly 950 and 1050 CE under the Chandela dynasty, dedicated to both Hindu and Jain worship. The site was largely abandoned and overgrown after the decline of the dynasty, which helped protect it from destruction. It was rediscovered by the outside world in the 19th century. UNESCO inscribed the Khajuraho Group of Monuments in 1986.

name: Gyeongju Historic Areas
description: Gyeongju in South Korea is often called a 'museum without walls' for the wealth of historic sites scattered across the city and its surroundings. Grassy royal burial mounds, the ancient Cheomseongdae observatory and the serene Anapji pond recall a former royal capital. Nearby stand the exquisite Bulguksa Temple and the Seokguram Grotto with its serene stone Buddha. The concentration of monuments makes it the heart of Korea's ancient heritage.
history: Gyeongju was the capital of the Silla kingdom, which ruled much of the Korean peninsula for nearly a thousand years until the 10th century. The city was a flourishing centre of Buddhist art, learning and royal power. Its tombs, temples and artefacts preserve a remarkable record of Silla civilisation. UNESCO inscribed the Gyeongju Historic Areas in 2000.

name: 古都 Hue Monuments
description: The former imperial capital of Hue in central Vietnam preserves the citadel, palaces and royal tombs of the country's last dynasty. The vast walled Imperial City, entered through the imposing Meridian Gate, contains throne halls, temples and gardens along a ceremonial axis. Elaborate royal tombs lie scattered in the countryside along the Perfume River. The monuments reflect a sophisticated blend of Vietnamese tradition and outside influence.
history: Hue became the capital of unified Vietnam in 1802 under the Nguyen dynasty, which built the citadel modelled in part on Beijing's Forbidden City. It remained the seat of the emperors until the dynasty ended in 1945. The city and its monuments suffered severe damage during the 20th-century wars, particularly the 1968 Tet Offensive. UNESCO inscribed the Complex of Hue Monuments in 1993.

name: Sukhothai Historical Park
description: Sukhothai Historical Park in central Thailand preserves the ruins of the first great capital of the Thai people. Graceful Buddha images, lotus-bud stupas and the remains of temples and palaces are set among lily ponds and lawns. The serene seated and walking Buddhas exemplify a refined artistic style that became a model for later Thai art. The peaceful, well-tended ruins are best explored by bicycle.
history: Sukhothai flourished as the capital of a powerful kingdom in the 13th and 14th centuries, a period often regarded as a golden age of Thai civilisation. It was here that the Thai alphabet is traditionally said to have been created and a distinctive Buddhist art developed. The kingdom was later absorbed by the rising power of Ayutthaya. UNESCO inscribed Sukhothai and associated towns in 1991.

name: Lumbini
description: Lumbini in southern Nepal is revered as the birthplace of the Buddha and one of the holiest sites in the Buddhist world. The sacred garden centres on the Maya Devi Temple, marking the spot where Queen Maya is believed to have given birth. A stone pillar erected by the emperor Ashoka and a sacred pool and bodhi tree complete the pilgrimage site. Monasteries built by Buddhist communities from many countries surround the sacred zone.
history: The Buddha, Siddhartha Gautama, is traditionally held to have been born here around the 6th or 5th century BCE. The emperor Ashoka visited and marked the site with a commemorative pillar in the 3rd century BCE, confirming its location. Lumbini became a major pilgrimage destination and was later lost and rediscovered in the 19th century. UNESCO inscribed Lumbini in 1997 as the birthplace of the Lord Buddha.

name: Khao Yai National Park
description: Khao Yai is Thailand's first and most famous national park, a vast expanse of monsoon forest and grassland not far from Bangkok. It shelters wild elephants, gibbons, hornbills and the occasional tiger, along with spectacular waterfalls. Forest trails, salt licks and viewpoints let visitors experience the rich wildlife of tropical Asia. The misty hills and dense jungle make it a haven for nature lovers.
history: The park was established in 1962 as the first in Thailand, protecting an important block of tropical forest. Its varied habitats support one of the country's most diverse assemblages of wildlife. Conservation efforts have worked to protect its elephant herds and combat poaching. UNESCO inscribed Khao Yai as part of the Dong Phayayen-Khao Yai Forest Complex in 2005.

name: Jim Corbett National Park
description: Jim Corbett National Park in the Himalayan foothills of northern India is the country's oldest national park and a celebrated tiger reserve. Its landscape of grassland, riverine forest and hills along the Ramganga River shelters tigers, elephants, leopards and abundant birdlife. Open-jeep safaris offer the chance to glimpse a wild Bengal tiger in its natural habitat. The dramatic mountain backdrop adds to its appeal.
history: The park was established in 1936 as Hailey National Park, the first in India, and was later renamed after the hunter-turned-conservationist Jim Corbett. It became the launch site of India's Project Tiger conservation programme in 1973. Corbett's writings did much to raise awareness of the need to protect the tiger. The park remains a flagship of Indian wildlife conservation.

name: Baalbek
description: Baalbek in Lebanon holds some of the grandest Roman temple ruins in the world, on a scale that dwarfs almost anything else from antiquity. The Temple of Jupiter once stood on a colossal podium, and its surviving columns are among the tallest the Romans ever raised. The remarkably well-preserved Temple of Bacchus nearby is richly decorated with intricate carving. The sheer size of the stonework, including some of the largest building blocks ever quarried, astonishes visitors.
history: The site was a place of worship long before the Romans, sacred to a local sun deity and known to the Greeks as Heliopolis. Under Roman rule from the 1st century CE, a vast temple complex was raised over more than two centuries. The temples were later converted and fortified in the Byzantine and Islamic periods. UNESCO inscribed Baalbek in 1984 as one of the finest examples of imperial Roman architecture.

name: Old City of Jerusalem
description: The Old City of Jerusalem is one of the most sacred and contested places on Earth, holy to Judaism, Christianity and Islam. Within its ancient walls lie the Western Wall, the Church of the Holy Sepulchre and the Dome of the Rock, sacred to the three faiths. Its narrow stone streets, divided into traditional quarters, teem with pilgrims, markets and history. Few places carry such concentrated spiritual and historical weight.
history: Jerusalem has been settled for thousands of years and conquered, destroyed and rebuilt many times by Israelites, Babylonians, Romans, Byzantines, Arabs, Crusaders and Ottomans. Each left a layer in the city, whose holy places became focal points for the world's monotheistic religions. The present walls were built by the Ottoman sultan Suleiman in the 16th century. UNESCO inscribed the Old City of Jerusalem, proposed by Jordan, in 1981, and it is listed as in danger.

name: Hegra (Mada'in Salih)
description: Hegra in north-western Saudi Arabia is the kingdom's first UNESCO World Heritage Site, a spectacular Nabataean city carved from desert rock. More than a hundred monumental tombs, their elaborate façades cut into golden sandstone outcrops, rise from the sands. As the southern counterpart of Petra, it shares the same striking rock-cut architecture in a remote and beautiful setting. The isolation of the site adds to its haunting atmosphere.
history: Hegra was the principal southern city of the Nabataean kingdom, prospering on the caravan trade in incense and spices around the 1st century CE. Its tombs bear inscriptions naming the families who commissioned them, offering rare insight into Nabataean society. The city was later incorporated into the Roman province of Arabia. UNESCO inscribed Hegra in 2008 as Saudi Arabia's first World Heritage Site.

name: Babylon
description: Babylon in Iraq is one of the most famous cities of the ancient world, its ruins spread along the Euphrates south of Baghdad. Reconstructed walls, the foundations of great temples and the processional way evoke a city that was once the largest on Earth. It is associated with legendary wonders such as the Hanging Gardens and monuments such as the Ishtar Gate. The site carries an immense weight of history and myth.
history: Babylon rose to greatness under King Hammurabi in the 18th century BCE and again under Nebuchadnezzar II in the 6th century BCE, who rebuilt it on a magnificent scale. It was a centre of learning, law and astronomy and a powerful symbol in religious tradition. The city declined after the rise of new empires and was gradually buried. UNESCO inscribed Babylon in 2019 as testimony to one of the world's most influential ancient cities.

name: Historic Centre of Mexico City
description: The historic centre of Mexico City is built directly atop the ruins of the Aztec capital of Tenochtitlan, layering empires upon empire. The vast Zócalo square is bordered by the colossal Metropolitan Cathedral and the National Palace with its Diego Rivera murals. Nearby, the excavated Templo Mayor reveals the heart of the Aztec sacred precinct. Grand colonial buildings and bustling streets fill one of the largest historic centres in the Americas.
history: The Aztecs founded Tenochtitlan on an island in a lake around 1325, building it into one of the great cities of the world. The Spanish under Hernán Cortés conquered and destroyed it in 1521, raising their own capital on its ruins. Centuries of colonial and modern building created the dense historic centre seen today. UNESCO inscribed the historic centre of Mexico City and nearby Xochimilco in 1987.

name: Historic Sanctuary of Cusco
description: Cusco, high in the Peruvian Andes, was the capital of the Inca Empire and remains one of the most beautiful historic cities in South America. Its streets are built on massive Inca stone foundations, topped by Spanish colonial churches and mansions in a striking architectural fusion. The great square of the Plaza de Armas and the temple-fortress of Sacsayhuamán above the city are highlights. It is the gateway to Machu Picchu and the Sacred Valley.
history: Cusco was the political and spiritual centre of the Inca Empire, laid out, according to tradition, in the shape of a puma. After the Spanish conquest in the 1530s, colonists built churches and palaces directly on top of the finely cut Inca masonry. The blending of the two cultures gives the city its unique character. UNESCO inscribed the City of Cusco in 1983.

name: Cartagena, Colombia
description: Cartagena on Colombia's Caribbean coast is a jewel of Spanish colonial architecture, its walled old city a maze of colourful balconied houses, plazas and churches. Massive stone ramparts and the great fortress of San Felipe de Barajas once defended the port against pirates and rival powers. The vibrant streets, bougainvillea-draped façades and lively squares make it one of the most romantic cities in the Americas. The sea breeze and warm light add to its charm.
history: Founded in 1533, Cartagena became one of the most important ports of the Spanish Empire, through which gold and silver flowed back to Spain. Its wealth made it a target for pirates and privateers, prompting the construction of its formidable fortifications. The port was also a major centre of the transatlantic slave trade. UNESCO inscribed the port, fortresses and monuments of Cartagena in 1984.

name: Historic Town of Ouro Preto
description: Ouro Preto in the mountains of Minas Gerais is the finest surviving colonial town in Brazil, a cascade of Baroque churches and pastel houses on steep cobbled streets. Its richly decorated churches, adorned with gilt carving and the sculpture of the master Aleijadinho, are masterpieces of Brazilian Baroque. The town's hilly setting and historic atmosphere make it a captivating destination. It preserves the legacy of Brazil's golden age.
history: Ouro Preto, meaning 'black gold', boomed in the 18th century at the heart of a vast gold rush that made it one of the largest and wealthiest towns in the Americas. The riches funded an extraordinary flowering of Baroque art and architecture. The town was also a cradle of Brazilian independence sentiment. UNESCO inscribed the historic town of Ouro Preto in 1980 as its first Brazilian listing.

name: Old Havana
description: Old Havana is the historic heart of the Cuban capital, a remarkable ensemble of Spanish colonial squares, churches, fortresses and faded mansions. Grand plazas such as the Plaza Vieja, the cathedral square and the harbour fortresses recall the city's former wealth and strategic importance. Vintage cars, music spilling from doorways and crumbling grandeur give it an unmistakable atmosphere. Extensive restoration has revived many of its finest buildings.
history: Founded in 1519, Havana became one of the principal ports of the Spanish Empire in the Americas and a key stop for treasure fleets returning to Spain. Its strategic harbour was defended by massive fortifications, some of the oldest in the Americas. The old city preserves a dense concentration of architecture from across the colonial centuries. UNESCO inscribed Old Havana and its fortifications in 1982.

name: Mesa Verde National Park
description: Mesa Verde in Colorado preserves the spectacular cliff dwellings of the Ancestral Puebloans, tucked into alcoves beneath the rim of high desert mesas. The largest, Cliff Palace, contains some 150 rooms built of sandstone within a vast natural recess. Guided tours descend ladders and trails to explore the remarkably preserved villages. The setting in the canyon country adds drama to the ancient architecture.
history: The Ancestral Puebloans lived on the mesas and in the cliff alcoves from around 600 to 1300 CE, farming the tops and building their distinctive stone dwellings. For reasons still debated, possibly including drought, they left the region by the end of the 13th century. The dwellings remained largely undisturbed until rediscovered by ranchers in the late 19th century. UNESCO inscribed Mesa Verde in 1978 among its very first listings.

name: Statue of Liberty
description: The Statue of Liberty stands on an island in New York Harbor as one of the most recognisable symbols in the world. The colossal copper figure, holding a torch aloft and a tablet of law, has welcomed generations of immigrants arriving by sea. Visitors can take a ferry to the island and climb to the crown for sweeping views of the harbour and skyline. It embodies ideals of freedom and opportunity.
history: The statue was a gift from the people of France to the United States, dedicated in 1886 to mark friendship and shared ideals of liberty. Designed by the sculptor Frédéric Auguste Bartholdi with an internal structure engineered by Gustave Eiffel, it was shipped across the Atlantic in pieces. For millions of immigrants passing through nearby Ellis Island, it was the first sight of their new home. UNESCO inscribed the Statue of Liberty in 1984.

name: Independence Hall
description: Independence Hall in Philadelphia is the birthplace of the United States, a handsome red-brick Georgian building where the nation was founded. In its modest assembly room the Declaration of Independence and the United States Constitution were both debated and adopted. Guided tours bring to life the momentous debates that took place within its walls. The building's influence on ideas of government has reached far beyond America.
history: Built in the 1730s and 1740s as the Pennsylvania State House, the building became the meeting place of the Continental Congress. There the Declaration of Independence was adopted in 1776 and, eleven years later, the Constitution was drafted. The principles articulated here shaped democratic movements around the world. UNESCO inscribed Independence Hall in 1979 for its global significance.

name: Old Quebec
description: Old Quebec is the historic heart of Quebec City, the only walled city north of Mexico in the Americas. Perched above the St Lawrence River, it is crowned by the fairy-tale Château Frontenac hotel and laced with narrow stone streets and squares. The Upper and Lower Towns preserve the atmosphere of New France, with French signage, cafés and culture. It is one of the most charming and distinctive cities in North America.
history: Quebec City was founded in 1608 by Samuel de Champlain and became the capital of the French colony of New France. Its strategic position above the river made it a key fortress, fought over by France and Britain, who captured it in 1759. The city retained its French character and is the centre of francophone culture in North America. UNESCO inscribed the Historic District of Old Quebec in 1985.

name: Los Glaciares National Park
description: Los Glaciares in Argentine Patagonia is a realm of immense glaciers, jagged peaks and ice-blue lakes. Its most famous feature, the Perito Moreno Glacier, is a towering wall of ice from which great blocks calve thunderously into the water below. The dramatic granite spires of Mount Fitz Roy and Cerro Torre draw climbers and trekkers from around the world. The park protects one of the largest expanses of ice outside the polar regions.
history: The park lies on the edge of the Southern Patagonian Ice Field, a remnant of the great ice sheets of the last ice age. Its glaciers and mountains have long fascinated explorers and mountaineers. The national park was established in 1937 to protect this extraordinary landscape. UNESCO inscribed Los Glaciares in 1981 for its outstanding glacial scenery.

name: National Museum of Anthropology (Mexico)
description: The National Museum of Anthropology in Mexico City is one of the greatest archaeological museums in the world. Its galleries present the civilisations of ancient Mexico, from the Olmec and Maya to the Aztec, in spectacular displays. The famous Aztec Sun Stone and a vast array of sculpture, jewellery and artefacts fill its halls around a striking central courtyard. It offers an unrivalled introduction to the pre-Hispanic heritage of the Americas.
history: The museum opened in its celebrated modernist building in 1964, designed to showcase the depth of Mexico's indigenous past. Its collections were assembled from archaeological sites across the country over more than a century. The building's vast central umbrella canopy is itself an architectural landmark. The museum is a source of national pride and a major centre of research.

name: Royal Ontario Museum
description: The Royal Ontario Museum in Toronto is the largest museum in Canada, combining world cultures and natural history under one roof. Its galleries range from dinosaurs and minerals to art and artefacts from civilisations around the globe. The striking crystalline glass-and-steel extension, the Michael Lee-Chin Crystal, gives the building a bold modern presence. It is one of the most visited museums in North America.
history: The museum opened in 1914 and grew through the 20th century into a major centre of collections and research. Its holdings number more than six million objects and specimens. The dramatic Crystal addition, completed in 2007, reshaped the museum's profile on the city skyline. It remains Ontario's flagship cultural institution.

name: Sydney Opera House
description: The Sydney Opera House is one of the most famous buildings of the 20th century, its soaring white sail-like shells rising above the harbour. The expressionist masterpiece is both a working performing-arts centre and an architectural icon recognised the world over. Its dramatic position on a harbour promontory, beside the Harbour Bridge, makes it the symbol of Sydney and of Australia. Performances, tours and the surrounding promenades draw millions of visitors.
history: The building was designed by the Danish architect Jørn Utzon, whose bold vision won an international competition in 1957. Its construction proved enormously challenging and controversial, and Utzon left the project before its completion. It was finally opened by Queen Elizabeth II in 1973. UNESCO inscribed the Sydney Opera House in 2007 as a masterpiece of modern architecture.
photo_author_orig: Bernard Spragg. NZ from Christchurch, New Zealand

name: Tongariro National Park
description: Tongariro in the central North Island is New Zealand's oldest national park, a dramatic volcanic landscape of active peaks, craters and emerald lakes. The famous Tongariro Alpine Crossing leads hikers past steaming vents, lava flows and brilliantly coloured volcanic lakes. The towering cones of Ngauruhoe and Ruapehu dominate the skyline and offer winter skiing. The stark, otherworldly terrain has featured in film as the land of Mordor.
history: The volcanic peaks at the heart of the park are sacred to the Māori, and in 1887 the paramount chief Te Heuheu Tukino IV gifted them to the nation to ensure their protection. This made Tongariro the first national park created through such an act of indigenous generosity. The park's volcanoes remain active. UNESCO inscribed Tongariro in 1990, and in 1993 it became the first site recognised for its associative cultural landscape values.

name: Blue Mountains National Park
description: The Blue Mountains, just west of Sydney, are a rugged landscape of sandstone plateaux, deep forested gorges and dramatic escarpments. The mountains take their name from the bluish haze given off by the vast eucalyptus forests that cloak them. Famous viewpoints overlook the Three Sisters rock formation and the Jamison Valley far below. Bushwalking trails, waterfalls and lookouts make it a beloved escape from the city.
history: The mountains long formed a barrier to European expansion inland until a route was found across them in 1813. The area is rich in Aboriginal heritage, with the Gundungurra and Darug peoples connected to the land for thousands of years. The eucalyptus forests harbour exceptional plant diversity, including the ancient Wollemi pine discovered nearby in 1994. UNESCO inscribed the Greater Blue Mountains Area in 2000.

name: Bagrati Cathedral and Gelati Monastery
description: The Gelati Monastery in western Georgia is a masterpiece of medieval Georgian architecture and a centre of Christian learning. Its churches are adorned with brilliant mosaics, frescoes and carved stone of exceptional quality. Set on a wooded hillside, the monastery complex includes an academy that was once a renowned seat of scholarship. The beauty of its decoration and setting make it a highlight of the Caucasus.
history: The monastery was founded in 1106 by King David the Builder, who is buried at its gate, during a golden age of the Georgian kingdom. Its academy attracted leading scholars and helped make Gelati a centre of Georgian culture and philosophy. The complex preserves outstanding examples of medieval art across several centuries. UNESCO inscribed Gelati in 1994, originally together with the now-delisted Bagrati Cathedral.

name: Old City of Sana'a
description: The old city of Sana'a in Yemen is one of the most distinctive urban landscapes in the world, a sea of tall tower-houses built of earth and brick. Their façades are decorated with intricate white gypsum tracery around windows and arches, creating a gingerbread-like beauty. Minarets, markets and bathhouses fill the dense old quarter, which has been inhabited for millennia. The skyline of decorated towers at dusk is unforgettable.
history: Sana'a has been continuously inhabited for more than 2,500 years and was an early centre of Islam in the Arabian Peninsula. Its multi-storey tower-houses, some dating back centuries, represent a remarkable indigenous architectural tradition. The city was a stop on the ancient incense and trade routes of southern Arabia. UNESCO inscribed the old city of Sana'a in 1986, and it is now listed as in danger amid conflict.

name: Mtskheta Historical Monuments
description: Mtskheta, near Tbilisi, is the ancient spiritual capital of Georgia and one of the holiest places in the country. Its great cathedral of Svetitskhoveli and the hilltop Jvari Monastery are masterpieces of medieval Georgian church architecture. The town sits at the confluence of two rivers in a beautiful mountain setting. It remains the seat of the Georgian Orthodox Church and a place of pilgrimage.
history: Mtskheta was the capital of the early Georgian kingdom of Iberia and the place where Georgia adopted Christianity as its state religion in the 4th century. According to tradition, the robe of Christ is buried beneath Svetitskhoveli Cathedral. The town's churches preserve over a thousand years of religious art and history. UNESCO inscribed the historical monuments of Mtskheta in 1994.

name: Bahla Fort
description: Bahla Fort in the interior of Oman is one of the most impressive mud-brick fortresses in the Arabian Peninsula. Its massive towers and walls of unbaked clay and stone rise above the oasis town of Bahla. The fort and the surrounding walled settlement, with its mosque and falaj irrigation channels, evoke the power of a former regional capital. The scale of the earthen fortifications is remarkable.
history: Bahla was the stronghold of the Banu Nebhan tribe, who dominated central Oman from around the 12th to the 17th century. The fort and the great wall encircling the oasis testify to their wealth and authority. Built largely of mud-brick, the structures required continual maintenance and underwent extensive restoration. UNESCO inscribed Bahla Fort in 1987 as a rare surviving example of Omani fortified architecture.

name: Butrint
description: Butrint in southern Albania is a beautiful archaeological site set on a wooded peninsula between a lagoon and the sea. Its layered ruins include a Greek theatre, Roman baths and villas, an early Christian baptistery and Venetian fortifications. The mingling of civilisations amid lush vegetation gives the site a tranquil, evocative atmosphere. It is one of the most rewarding ancient sites in the Balkans.
history: Butrint was settled by the Greeks and grew into an important Roman colony and port, before becoming a Byzantine and later Venetian stronghold. Its long history saw it pass through the hands of many powers around the Adriatic. The site was eventually abandoned and reclaimed by marsh and forest. UNESCO inscribed Butrint in 1992 for its rich record of Mediterranean civilisations.

name: Ohrid Region
description: The Ohrid region of North Macedonia centres on one of the oldest and deepest lakes in Europe, ringed by mountains and historic towns. The lakeside town of Ohrid is famed for its many medieval churches, including the iconic Church of St John at Kaneo on a clifftop above the water. The ancient lake harbours unique species found nowhere else. The combination of natural and cultural treasures makes it exceptional.
history: Lake Ohrid is estimated to be over a million years old, sheltering many endemic species in its depths. The town of Ohrid was an important centre of Slavic literacy and Christianity, once said to have had 365 churches. It played a key role in the spread of the Cyrillic alphabet and Orthodox culture. UNESCO inscribed the natural and cultural heritage of the Ohrid region in 1979 and 1980.

name: Wieliczka Salt Mine
description: The Wieliczka Salt Mine near Kraków is an astonishing underground world carved entirely from rock salt over many centuries. Its labyrinth of chambers, tunnels and lakes descends hundreds of metres below the surface. The most spectacular space is a vast underground chapel, complete with salt-carved altars, sculptures and chandeliers. Visitors descend through galleries lined with brine and salt to explore this subterranean cathedral.
history: Salt has been extracted at Wieliczka since the 13th century, making it one of the oldest operating mines in the world until commercial mining ceased in recent decades. The salt brought great wealth to the Polish crown for centuries. Generations of miners carved chapels and sculptures into the salt to mark their devotion. UNESCO inscribed Wieliczka, with the nearby Bochnia mine, in 1978 among its first listings.

name: Delphi
description: Delphi, on the slopes of Mount Parnassus in Greece, was the most important religious sanctuary of the ancient Greek world. Pilgrims came from across the Mediterranean to consult the famous Oracle of Apollo, whose pronouncements shaped decisions of war and state. The ruins include the Temple of Apollo, a theatre, a stadium and a fine museum of sculpture. The dramatic mountain setting overlooking a valley of olive groves is sublime.
history: The ancient Greeks regarded Delphi as the centre, or navel, of the world. From around the 8th century BCE the sanctuary grew enormously wealthy and influential through the prestige of its oracle. City-states competed to dedicate treasuries and monuments there. UNESCO inscribed Delphi in 1987 as a masterly blend of monuments and landscape that embodied the spiritual life of ancient Greece.

name: Herculaneum
description: Herculaneum, near Pompeii, is a smaller but even better-preserved Roman town buried by the same eruption of Vesuvius. Where Pompeii was buried in ash, Herculaneum was engulfed by superheated volcanic flows that carbonised and preserved organic material. Wooden furniture, doors and even food survive, along with elegant villas and intricate mosaics. The depth of preservation gives an intimate sense of Roman domestic life.
history: Herculaneum was a wealthy seaside resort favoured by prosperous Romans before its destruction in 79 CE. The volcanic flows that buried it preserved materials that decayed elsewhere, including charred scrolls from a famous villa library. Excavation has been slow and painstaking because the hardened deposits and the modern town above complicate digging. UNESCO inscribed Herculaneum alongside Pompeii in 1997.

name: Chan Chan
description: Chan Chan on the north coast of Peru is the largest adobe city in the ancient Americas, a vast complex of earthen walls, plazas and palaces. Its great walled compounds are decorated with friezes of fish, birds and geometric patterns moulded into the mud-brick. The scale of the ruined city, spread across many square kilometres of desert, is immense. It is a remarkable testament to a sophisticated pre-Inca civilisation.
history: Chan Chan was the capital of the Chimú kingdom, which dominated the Peruvian coast from around 900 CE until its conquest by the Inca in the 15th century. At its height it may have housed tens of thousands of people, sustained by advanced irrigation of the desert. Its earthen architecture is vulnerable to the rare but destructive rains brought by El Niño. UNESCO inscribed Chan Chan in 1986 and lists it as in danger.

name: Nazca Lines
description: The Nazca Lines are a vast collection of ancient designs etched into the desert of southern Peru, best appreciated from the air. The figures include enormous stylised animals such as a hummingbird, monkey and condor, alongside straight lines and geometric shapes stretching for kilometres. Created by removing the dark surface stones to reveal the pale ground beneath, they have survived for thousands of years in the arid climate. Flights over the plateau reveal their astonishing scale.
history: The lines were made by the Nazca culture between roughly 500 BCE and 500 CE, though their precise purpose remains debated. Theories link them to astronomical observation, water rituals and ceremonial pathways. The dry, windless desert has preserved the shallow markings remarkably well. UNESCO inscribed the Lines and Geoglyphs of Nasca in 1994.

name: Cahokia
description: Cahokia, near present-day St Louis, was the largest pre-Columbian city north of Mexico, centred on a complex of earthen mounds. The colossal Monks Mound, a terraced earthwork covering several hectares, was the largest such structure in the Americas. The site preserves the remains of plazas, dwellings and a remarkable woodhenge solar calendar. It offers a window into a sophisticated Mississippian civilisation.
history: Cahokia flourished from around 1050 to 1350 CE as the heart of the Mississippian culture, with a population that may have exceeded that of contemporary European cities. Its inhabitants built more than a hundred earthen mounds for ceremonial and elite use. The city was abandoned before European contact for reasons still studied. UNESCO inscribed the Cahokia Mounds in 1982.

name: Knossos
description: Knossos on the island of Crete is the largest and most famous palace of the ancient Minoan civilisation. Its sprawling complex of rooms, courtyards and storerooms, partly reconstructed, evokes a sophisticated Bronze Age society. Vivid frescoes of dolphins, bulls and elegant figures adorn its walls. The palace is associated with the legend of King Minos and the labyrinth of the Minotaur.
history: Knossos flourished as the centre of Minoan civilisation, Europe's earliest, from around 2000 to 1400 BCE. Its complex layout may have inspired the Greek myth of the labyrinth. The palace was excavated and controversially reconstructed by Sir Arthur Evans in the early 20th century. The site remains one of the most important in the study of Aegean prehistory.

name: Mycenae
description: Mycenae in southern Greece was the centre of one of the great civilisations of Bronze Age Europe. Its fortified citadel is entered through the famous Lion Gate, and its royal shaft graves yielded spectacular golden treasures. The massive 'Cyclopean' walls and the beehive-shaped Treasury of Atreus tomb testify to its power. The site lies in a dramatic landscape between two hills.
history: Mycenae gave its name to the Mycenaean civilisation that dominated the Aegean from around 1600 to 1100 BCE. According to Homer, it was the kingdom of Agamemnon, who led the Greeks against Troy. The discovery of golden burial masks by Heinrich Schliemann in 1876 brought the site worldwide fame. UNESCO inscribed Mycenae, with nearby Tiryns, in 1999.

name: Palmyra
description: Palmyra in the Syrian desert was one of the most splendid cities of the ancient world, an oasis of grand colonnaded streets and temples. Its monumental ruins, rising from the sands, blended Greco-Roman architecture with Persian and local influences. The Great Colonnade, the Temple of Bel and the towering funerary towers were among its glories. The site stands as a poignant symbol of cultural heritage at risk.
history: Palmyra grew rich from the 1st to the 3rd century CE as a caravan city linking Rome with Persia and the East. Under its famous queen Zenobia it briefly challenged Rome itself before being subdued. Long admired as one of the great ruins of antiquity, parts of the site were deliberately destroyed during the Syrian conflict in 2015. UNESCO inscribed Palmyra in 1980 and now lists it as in danger.

name: Hattusa
description: Hattusa in central Turkey was the capital of the Hittite Empire, one of the great powers of the ancient Near East. The extensive ruins include massive defensive walls, monumental gateways adorned with carved lions and sphinxes, and the foundations of temples and palaces. A nearby rock sanctuary is carved with processions of Hittite gods. The scale of the fortified city reflects the might of a vanished empire.
history: Hattusa was the centre of the Hittite Empire, which rivalled Egypt and Babylon in the second millennium BCE. The city's archives, written on clay tablets, included one of the earliest known peace treaties, made with Egypt. The empire collapsed around 1200 BCE and the city was destroyed and abandoned. UNESCO inscribed Hattusa in 1986 as the capital of a once-dominant ancient power.

name: Monte Albán
description: Monte Albán in the Mexican state of Oaxaca is a spectacular ancient city built atop a flattened mountain ridge. Its great central plaza is flanked by pyramids, palaces and a remarkable observatory, with sweeping views over three valleys. Carved stone slabs depicting figures known as the 'Danzantes' are among its enigmatic features. The setting on the levelled mountaintop is breathtaking.
history: Monte Albán was the capital of the Zapotec civilisation, flourishing from around 500 BCE to 750 CE as one of the earliest cities in Mesoamerica. Its rulers levelled the mountaintop and built a ceremonial centre that dominated the surrounding region. The city was later partly used by the Mixtec for elaborate burials. UNESCO inscribed Monte Albán, with the historic centre of Oaxaca, in 1987.

name: Palenque
description: Palenque in the jungle of Chiapas is one of the most beautiful and refined of all Maya cities. Its elegant temples and palace, set against forested hills, are renowned for their delicate stucco decoration and finely carved inscriptions. The Temple of the Inscriptions concealed the spectacular tomb of the ruler Pakal the Great. The harmony of architecture and rainforest setting gives Palenque a magical quality.
history: Palenque flourished between roughly 600 and 800 CE under powerful rulers, above all the long-reigning K'inich Janaab' Pakal. Its inscriptions provide some of the richest historical records of any Maya city. The discovery of Pakal's richly furnished tomb in 1952 was a landmark in Maya archaeology. UNESCO inscribed Palenque in 1987.

name: Copán
description: Copán in western Honduras is celebrated for the artistic brilliance of its Maya sculpture and hieroglyphic monuments. Its plazas are studded with intricately carved stelae and altars depicting the city's rulers in extraordinary detail. The famous Hieroglyphic Stairway, with the longest known Maya text, records the dynasty's history. The richness of its carving makes Copán a treasure of ancient American art.
history: Copán was a major Maya city-state that flourished from around 400 to 800 CE under a dynasty of sixteen kings. Its rulers commissioned some of the finest sculpture and longest inscriptions in the Maya world. The city declined and was abandoned, like other lowland Maya centres, around the 9th century. UNESCO inscribed the Maya site of Copán in 1980.
photo_author_orig: Adalberto Hernandez Vega from Copan Ruinas, Honduras

name: Anuradhapura
description: Anuradhapura is one of the ancient capitals of Sri Lanka and a sacred city of Buddhism. Its sprawling ruins include colossal brick stupas, monastic complexes, palaces and elaborate bathing pools. The sacred Bodhi tree, said to be grown from a cutting of the tree under which the Buddha attained enlightenment, is venerated here. The scale of the ancient monuments amid the surrounding parkland is impressive.
history: Anuradhapura was the capital of Sri Lanka for more than a thousand years, from around the 4th century BCE, and a great centre of Theravada Buddhism. Its rulers built immense stupas and sophisticated irrigation works that sustained a large population. The city was abandoned after invasions around the 11th century and reclaimed by the jungle. UNESCO inscribed the sacred city of Anuradhapura in 1982.

name: Polonnaruwa
description: Polonnaruwa, a later ancient capital of Sri Lanka, preserves a magnificent group of ruins in a compact and well-tended setting. Its highlights include the serene rock-carved Buddha statues of the Gal Vihara, royal palaces and finely decorated shrines. The remains reflect a flourishing medieval civilisation with sophisticated art and engineering. The site is easily explored by bicycle among the lakes and ruins.
history: Polonnaruwa became the capital of Sri Lanka in the 11th century after the fall of Anuradhapura, reaching its golden age under King Parakramabahu I in the 12th century. He built a vast reservoir and adorned the city with temples and palaces. The kingdom later declined and the capital shifted elsewhere. UNESCO inscribed the ancient city of Polonnaruwa in 1982.

name: Volubilis
description: Volubilis in Morocco is an exceptionally well-preserved Roman city set in fertile plains near Meknes. Its ruins include a triumphal arch, a basilica, a capitol and grand houses paved with remarkable mosaics still in place. The site evokes the reach of Rome to the far west of North Africa. The mosaics and standing columns amid green fields make it a beautiful and atmospheric site.
history: Volubilis began as a settlement of the indigenous Mauretanian kingdom before becoming an important Roman city from the 1st century CE, prospering on the trade in olive oil and grain. It remained inhabited long after Roman rule ended and was briefly a base for the founder of the Moroccan state. An earthquake and later quarrying damaged the ruins. UNESCO inscribed Volubilis in 1997.

name: Sarek National Park
description: Sarek in northern Sweden is one of Europe's oldest national parks and among its wildest and most remote. A roadless wilderness of glaciated peaks, deep valleys and rushing rivers, it has no marked trails or facilities. It is a destination for experienced hikers seeking true Arctic solitude amid dramatic mountain scenery. Reindeer, elk and the occasional bear and wolverine roam its valleys.
history: The park lies in the homeland of the indigenous Sami people, who have herded reindeer across this region for thousands of years. Sarek was established in 1909 as one of the first national parks in Europe. Its inaccessibility has preserved a near-pristine wilderness. Sarek forms part of the UNESCO-listed Laponian Area inscribed in 1996 for both natural and Sami cultural values.

name: Killarney National Park
description: Killarney National Park in south-west Ireland surrounds a chain of beautiful lakes beneath the country's highest mountains. Ancient oak and yew woodlands, waterfalls and the elegant Muckross House lie within its bounds. The park is home to Ireland's only native herd of red deer and a wealth of birdlife. The famous Gap of Dunloe and lakeside views draw visitors throughout the year.
history: The lakes and mountains of Killarney have drawn admirers and tourists since the 18th century, celebrated in poetry and painting. The core of the park was donated to the Irish state in 1932 with the gift of the Muckross estate. It became the first national park in the Republic of Ireland. The park protects some of the country's last remaining native woodland.

name: Doñana National Park
description: Doñana in southern Spain is one of Europe's most important wetlands, a vast mosaic of marshes, lagoons, dunes and woodland at the mouth of the Guadalquivir River. It is a crucial stopover for millions of migratory birds travelling between Europe and Africa. The park is also a last refuge for the critically endangered Iberian lynx and the Spanish imperial eagle. Its shifting dunes and rich birdlife make it a haven for naturalists.
history: The area was long a royal hunting ground, which helped preserve its wild character. Growing recognition of its ecological importance led to its protection as a national park in 1969. The wetlands face ongoing pressure from surrounding agriculture and water extraction. UNESCO inscribed Doñana in 1994 for its outstanding biodiversity.

name: Gran Paradiso National Park
description: Gran Paradiso in the Italian Alps is the oldest national park in Italy, centred on the only peak entirely within the country to exceed 4,000 metres. Its glaciers, alpine meadows and valleys shelter abundant wildlife, above all the Alpine ibex it was created to protect. Marmots, chamois and golden eagles also inhabit its slopes. Mountain trails and dramatic scenery make it a paradise for walkers.
history: The area was once a royal hunting reserve set aside to protect the Alpine ibex, which had been hunted almost to extinction across the Alps. In 1922 the king donated the land to the Italian state, creating the country's first national park. The ibex population recovered and helped repopulate other Alpine regions. The park remains a stronghold for Alpine wildlife.

name: Berchtesgaden National Park
description: Berchtesgaden in the Bavarian Alps is Germany's only national park in the high Alps, a landscape of towering peaks, deep valleys and the emerald Königssee lake. The fjord-like lake, hemmed by sheer cliffs and reached by electric boats, is among the most beautiful in the country. The park's mountains, including the imposing Watzmann, draw hikers and climbers. Alpine forests, meadows and rare wildlife fill its protected valleys.
history: The region has long been admired for its dramatic mountain scenery and was an early focus of nature protection in Germany. A nature reserve was established here in 1921, and the national park was created in 1978. Conservation focuses on allowing natural Alpine processes to continue undisturbed. The park forms part of a UNESCO biosphere reserve.

name: Royal Chitwan National Park
description: Chitwan National Park in the lowland Terai of Nepal protects a rich expanse of jungle, grassland and rivers at the foot of the Himalaya. It is one of the best places in Asia to see wild one-horned rhinoceros, along with tigers, elephants, gharial crocodiles and abundant birdlife. Jeep safaris, canoe trips and guided walks bring visitors into the heart of the wilderness. The park offers a striking contrast to Nepal's famous mountains.
history: The Chitwan valley was once a royal hunting reserve, which limited settlement and helped preserve its wildlife. As malaria was controlled and population grew, conservationists pushed to protect the area, and it became Nepal's first national park in 1973. Determined anti-poaching efforts have helped its rhino population recover. UNESCO inscribed Chitwan National Park in 1984.

name: Zion National Park
description: Zion in southern Utah is a landscape of towering sandstone cliffs, narrow slot canyons and the winding Virgin River. Its sheer walls of cream, pink and red rock rise dramatically above the canyon floor, creating one of the most striking landscapes in the American Southwest. Famous hikes such as Angels Landing and the Narrows draw adventurers from around the world. The interplay of rock, river and light is mesmerising.
history: The canyon was carved over millions of years by the Virgin River cutting through the uplifted Colorado Plateau. Indigenous peoples lived in and around the canyon for thousands of years, and Mormon settlers gave many features their biblical names in the 19th century. It was protected as a national monument in 1909 and became a national park in 1919. Zion is now one of the most visited parks in the United States.

name: Glacier National Park (U.S.)
description: Glacier National Park in Montana is a spectacular expanse of rugged peaks, alpine lakes and remnant glaciers along the Continental Divide. The famous Going-to-the-Sun Road climbs through breathtaking mountain scenery to high passes. The park shelters grizzly bears, mountain goats and other wildlife in a largely pristine wilderness. Its glacially sculpted valleys and clear lakes earn it the nickname 'Crown of the Continent'.
history: The landscape was carved by great glaciers during the ice ages, leaving the sharp peaks and U-shaped valleys seen today. The region holds deep significance for the Blackfeet and other Indigenous peoples. It was established as a national park in 1910 amid early conservation efforts. With its Canadian neighbour it forms the Waterton-Glacier International Peace Park, a UNESCO World Heritage Site inscribed in 1995.

name: Jasper National Park
description: Jasper is the largest national park in the Canadian Rockies, a vast wilderness of mountains, glaciers, lakes and rivers. The Columbia Icefield, the dramatic Maligne Lake and Athabasca Falls are among its many highlights. Wildlife including elk, bears, caribou and bighorn sheep roams its valleys. Designated a dark-sky preserve, it offers some of the finest stargazing in North America.
history: The park grew from a fur-trading and railway history, established as a forest park in 1907 and later a national park. The scenic Icefields Parkway linking it to Banff opened up the mountains to visitors. Conservation balances tourism with the protection of a vast wilderness. Jasper forms part of the UNESCO-listed Canadian Rocky Mountain Parks inscribed in 1984.

name: Manuel Antonio National Park
description: Manuel Antonio on Costa Rica's Pacific coast is a small but spectacular national park where rainforest meets idyllic white-sand beaches. Despite its modest size it teems with wildlife, including sloths, monkeys, iguanas and a brilliant array of birds. Trails wind through the forest to coves of turquoise water fringed by jungle. The combination of accessible beaches and abundant wildlife makes it one of Costa Rica's most popular parks.
history: Costa Rica became a pioneer of tropical conservation and ecotourism in the late 20th century, setting aside large areas of forest. Manuel Antonio was established as a national park in 1972 to protect this stretch of coastal rainforest. Its popularity has required careful management to limit visitor impact. The park exemplifies the country's commitment to protecting its rich biodiversity.

name: National Museum of Korea
description: The National Museum of Korea in Seoul is the flagship museum of the country and one of the largest in Asia. Its galleries present the long sweep of Korean history and art, from prehistoric artefacts to refined celadon ceramics, gilt-bronze Buddhas and elegant paintings. The serene ten-storey stone pagoda in its central hall is a celebrated treasure. The museum offers a comprehensive introduction to Korea's cultural heritage.
history: The museum traces its origins to the early 20th century and has occupied several homes before opening in its present grand building in Yongsan in 2005. Its collections were assembled and protected through periods of war and upheaval. It now ranks among the most visited museums in the world. The institution is central to the preservation and study of Korean culture.
photo_author: Richard Mortel from Riyadh, Saudi Arabia
photo_author_orig: Richard Mortel from Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

name: National Archaeological Museum, Athens
description: The National Archaeological Museum in Athens is the largest museum in Greece and one of the world's great collections of ancient art. Its galleries display the golden treasures of Mycenae, exquisite classical sculpture and the remarkable Antikythera mechanism, an ancient analogue computer. Bronze masterpieces such as the Artemision Zeus and the Jockey of Artemision are highlights. It offers an unparalleled survey of ancient Greek civilisation.
history: The museum was established in the 19th century to gather and protect antiquities from across Greece following the country's independence. Its collections grew through excavations at major sites including Mycenae, Santorini and sanctuaries throughout the land. The grand Neoclassical building has been expanded over the decades. It remains the premier repository of ancient Greek art.

name: Kunsthistorisches Museum
description: The Kunsthistorisches Museum in Vienna is one of the finest art museums in the world, housed in a palatial 19th-century building. Its picture gallery holds an extraordinary collection of European masters, including the world's largest holding of works by Bruegel. Treasures of the Habsburg imperial collection fill galleries of antiquities, sculpture and decorative arts. The opulent interiors are themselves a work of art.
history: The museum was built to house the vast collections accumulated by the Habsburg dynasty over centuries of patronage and rule. It opened in 1891 as part of the grand building programme along Vienna's Ringstrasse. Its imperial collections give it particular strength in the art of the lands the Habsburgs ruled. It forms part of Vienna's UNESCO-listed historic centre.

name: National Museum of Scotland
description: The National Museum of Scotland in Edinburgh brings together the nation's collections of art, science, nature and world cultures under one roof. Its galleries range from Scottish history and decorative arts to natural history, technology and ancient civilisations. Highlights include Dolly the cloned sheep and the medieval Lewis chessmen. A striking blend of a Victorian glass-roofed hall and a modern sandstone wing houses the collections.
history: The museum was formed in 2006 by merging the Royal Museum, with its Victorian iron-and-glass hall opened in 1866, and the Museum of Scotland, opened in 1998. The combined institution presents both Scotland's story and the wider world. A major refurbishment renewed its galleries and reopened them in 2011. It is one of the most visited attractions in the United Kingdom.

name: Topkapı Palace Museum
description: Topkapı Palace overlooks the Bosphorus in Istanbul, the opulent former residence of the Ottoman sultans. Its courtyards, pavilions and gardens once formed the centre of an empire that spanned three continents. The treasury dazzles with jewels, including the famous emerald-studded dagger and an enormous diamond, while the harem reveals the private world of the court. The setting above the meeting of seas is magnificent.
history: The palace was built by Sultan Mehmed II soon after the Ottoman conquest of Constantinople in 1453 and served as the seat of power for some four centuries. Generations of sultans expanded it into a sprawling complex of courtyards and chambers. It became a museum in 1924 after the founding of the Turkish Republic. Topkapı lies within Istanbul's UNESCO-listed historic areas.

name: National Museum of Australia
description: The National Museum of Australia in Canberra explores the land, nation and people of Australia through its social history collections. Its galleries tell the stories of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander cultures, the colonial past and the development of modern Australia. The bold, brightly coloured contemporary building on a peninsula in Lake Burley Griffin is itself a landmark. Interactive exhibits make it especially engaging for families.
history: Although long envisaged, the museum opened only in 2001, to mark the centenary of Australian federation. It was conceived to focus on social history and the diverse stories of the Australian people. Its architecturally adventurous building drew both praise and debate. The museum has become a key institution for reflecting on national identity.

name: Te Papa Tongarewa
description: Te Papa, the Museum of New Zealand in Wellington, is the country's national museum and a lively celebration of its land, culture and people. Its galleries cover Māori treasures, natural history, art and the story of the nation, with a strong emphasis on bicultural partnership. A preserved colossal squid and an interactive earthquake house are among its memorable exhibits. The waterfront building is a popular gathering place in the capital.
history: Te Papa, whose name means 'the container of treasures', opened in 1998, combining the former national museum and art gallery. It was designed around the principle of partnership between Māori and other New Zealanders. Admission to the main collections is free, reflecting its mission of public access. It quickly became one of the most visited attractions in the country.

name: National Museum of Anthropology (Manila)
description: The National Museum of Anthropology in Manila showcases the rich cultural heritage of the Philippine archipelago. Its galleries display artefacts of the country's many peoples, from ancient burial jars and gold ornaments to traditional textiles and the recovered cargo of historic shipwrecks. The treasures recount thousands of years of life across the islands. The grand Neoclassical building lends the collections a dignified setting.
history: The museum occupies a stately government building in the historic heart of Manila that has been repurposed for the national collections. It forms part of the National Museum complex devoted to the country's fine arts, natural history and anthropology. Its holdings preserve the diverse indigenous and colonial heritage of the Philippines. Admission is free as part of efforts to broaden public access to culture.

name: Krka National Park
description: Krka National Park in Croatia is famed for a series of beautiful travertine waterfalls along the Krka River. The cascades of Skradinski Buk and Roški Slap tumble over natural barriers into emerald pools amid lush greenery. Wooden walkways guide visitors among the falls, and an island monastery sits within the park. It is one of Croatia's most popular natural attractions, a quieter cousin of Plitvice.
history: The travertine barriers that create the falls are formed continuously as mineral-rich water deposits calcium carbonate, a process still shaping the river. The valley has been inhabited and used for water power since antiquity, with old mills preserved along the banks. The park was established in 1985 to protect the river and its falls. It remains a showcase of Croatia's karst landscapes.

name: Triglav National Park
description: Triglav National Park is Slovenia's only national park, encompassing the Julian Alps around the country's highest peak, Mount Triglav. Its landscape of soaring limestone mountains, glacial valleys and the turquoise Soča River is among the most beautiful in Europe. The idyllic Lake Bohinj and the nearby gorges and waterfalls draw hikers and nature lovers. Triglav itself is a national symbol that appears on the Slovenian flag.
history: The mountains have been central to Slovenian identity for centuries, and protection efforts date back to an alpine conservation park established in 1924. The modern national park was created and expanded through the 20th century. Mount Triglav holds such symbolic importance that climbing it is considered a rite of passage for Slovenes. The park safeguards a rich alpine ecosystem and cultural landscape.

name: Ranthambore National Park
description: Ranthambore in Rajasthan is one of the best places in India to see wild Bengal tigers, set in a dramatic landscape of dry forest, lakes and ancient ruins. A massive 10th-century hill fort crowns the reserve, and crumbling pavilions and stepwells lie scattered among the woods. Tigers are often seen prowling near the lakes, alongside leopards, sloth bears and abundant deer. The blend of wildlife and historic ruins gives the park a unique atmosphere.
history: The area was once the private hunting ground of the Maharajas of Jaipur, who pursued tigers across its forests. It became a wildlife sanctuary in 1955 and was brought under India's Project Tiger in 1973. The towering Ranthambore Fort, a UNESCO-listed hill fort of Rajasthan, recalls the region's medieval past. Conservation has helped its tiger population recover and made it a flagship reserve.

name: Kaziranga National Park
description: Kaziranga in the Indian state of Assam protects the floodplain of the Brahmaputra River and is home to the largest population of the great one-horned rhinoceros in the world. Its tall elephant grass, wetlands and forests also shelter tigers, wild water buffalo, swamp deer and a wealth of birds. Visitors explore the park by jeep or on elephant-back across its lush, watery landscape. The annual monsoon floods are central to the ecology of the reserve.
history: Kaziranga was set aside as a reserve forest in 1905, in part at the urging of a vice-regal visitor concerned by the decline of the rhino. Decades of strict protection allowed the rhino population to rebound dramatically from near extinction. The park became a stronghold for several threatened species of the region. UNESCO inscribed Kaziranga National Park in 1985 for its exceptional biodiversity.

name: Yakushima
description: Yakushima is a mountainous island off southern Japan cloaked in ancient, moss-draped cedar forest. Its most famous inhabitants are the yakusugi, immense cedar trees some thousands of years old, including the legendary Jomon Sugi. Misty trails wind through luxuriant green forest that inspired the landscapes of celebrated animated films. The island's steep peaks, waterfalls and abundant rainfall create a uniquely lush environment.
history: The island's ancient cedars were heavily logged for centuries, but the oldest and largest survived in the remote high forest. Growing appreciation of their value led to strong protection of the island's interior. Yakushima's near-pristine forests preserve a vertical range of ecosystems from subtropical to cool-temperate. UNESCO inscribed Yakushima in 1993 as one of Japan's first natural World Heritage Sites.

name: Victoria and Albert Museum
description: The Victoria and Albert Museum in London is the world's largest museum of decorative arts and design. Its vast galleries hold sculpture, ceramics, textiles, fashion, jewellery, furniture and objects spanning five thousand years and every continent. Treasures range from the plaster Cast Courts to Raphael's tapestry cartoons and a renowned collection of fashion. Admission is free, in keeping with its mission to inspire designers and the public alike.
history: The museum was founded in 1852 in the wake of the Great Exhibition, with the aim of improving design and education. It was renamed in 1899 in honour of Queen Victoria and Prince Albert, who championed its founding ideals. Its collections grew enormously through the era of empire and industry. The V&A remains a leading centre for the study of art, craft and design.

name: National Gallery, London
description: The National Gallery on Trafalgar Square holds one of the greatest collections of Western European painting in the world. Its rooms trace the development of art from the medieval period to the early 20th century, with masterpieces by Leonardo, Velázquez, Turner, Van Gogh and countless others. The collection is displayed for the public free of charge in a grand building overlooking the square. It is one of the most visited art museums on Earth.
history: The gallery was founded in 1824 when the British government purchased a collection of paintings to form a national collection for all to enjoy. Unlike many continental galleries, it was built up by acquisition rather than from a royal collection. Its present building on Trafalgar Square opened in 1838 and has been expanded many times. It continues to grow through gifts and purchases.

name: Galleria dell'Accademia, Florence
description: The Galleria dell'Accademia in Florence is famous above all as the home of Michelangelo's David, perhaps the most celebrated sculpture in the world. The towering marble figure stands beneath a purpose-built dome at the end of a gallery lined with the artist's unfinished 'Prisoners'. The museum also holds important Florentine paintings and a notable collection of musical instruments. It is one of the most visited museums in Italy.
history: The gallery was established in 1784 to provide students of the adjacent art academy with masterworks to study. Michelangelo's David was moved there in 1873 from its exposed position in the Piazza della Signoria to protect it from the elements. The unfinished sculptures around it offer rare insight into the artist's working method. The museum lies within Florence's UNESCO-listed historic centre.

name: Tiwanaku
description: Tiwanaku, near the shores of Lake Titicaca in Bolivia, is one of the most important pre-Columbian sites in South America. Its monumental remains include the Akapana pyramid, the sunken courtyard and the great Gateway of the Sun carved from a single block of stone. Massive precisely cut stone blocks testify to the skill of its builders at high altitude. The site lies in a stark, beautiful Andean landscape over 3,800 metres above sea level.
history: Tiwanaku was the centre of a powerful civilisation that dominated the southern Andes from around 500 to 1000 CE before the rise of the Inca. It developed sophisticated agriculture, monumental architecture and a far-reaching cultural influence. The reasons for its eventual collapse, possibly including drought, are still studied. UNESCO inscribed Tiwanaku in 2000 as a testament to a major Andean civilisation.

name: Sukur Cultural Landscape
description: The Sukur Cultural Landscape in north-eastern Nigeria preserves a remarkable traditional society in the Mandara Mountains. A chief's palace crowns a hill above terraced fields, stone-paved ways and sacred sites maintained over centuries. The landscape reflects a way of life little changed and rich in spiritual meaning. It is a rare surviving example of an intact traditional African settlement.
history: Sukur has been a centre of iron production and a politically organised community for many centuries, with its terraces and shrines reflecting a long-established social order. The hilltop palace and the stone infrastructure embody the authority of its traditional rulers. The landscape continued in use into modern times largely unaltered. UNESCO inscribed the Sukur Cultural Landscape in 1999, the first African cultural landscape to be listed.

name: Bordeaux, Port of the Moon
description: Bordeaux in south-western France is celebrated for its harmonious 18th-century architecture along a graceful bend of the Garonne River. Grand stone façades, elegant squares and the sweeping Place de la Bourse, mirrored in a shallow water feature, define the city centre. As the heart of one of the world's great wine regions, it combines architectural beauty with a rich gastronomic culture. The riverside city earned its nickname from the crescent shape of its port.
history: Bordeaux grew wealthy as a major Atlantic port, especially through the wine trade that made its name known across the world. In the 18th century the city was rebuilt with a unity of classical architecture that survives remarkably intact. Its prosperity was also tied, less proudly, to the transatlantic trade. UNESCO inscribed the Port of the Moon in 2007 for its outstanding urban and architectural ensemble.

name: Historic Centre of Bruges
description: Bruges in Belgium is one of the best-preserved medieval cities in Europe, a romantic maze of canals, cobbled streets and step-gabled houses. Its market square is dominated by a soaring medieval belfry, and boat trips glide beneath stone bridges past historic guild houses. The city's prosperity in the Middle Ages funded fine churches and a wealth of Flemish art. Its tranquil canals have earned it the nickname 'Venice of the North'.
history: Bruges flourished as one of the great commercial cities of medieval Europe, a centre of the cloth trade and finance connected to a network across the continent. Its decline came when its link to the sea silted up, which paradoxically froze the city in its medieval form. It was rediscovered as a tourist destination in the 19th century. UNESCO inscribed the historic centre of Bruges in 2000.

name: Tower of Pisa (Piazza dei Miracoli)
description: The Piazza dei Miracoli in Pisa is a stunning ensemble of medieval religious architecture in gleaming white marble. The world-famous Leaning Tower, the cathedral, the baptistery and the cemetery stand together on a broad green lawn. The tower's dramatic tilt, the result of unstable ground, has made it one of the most recognisable buildings in the world. Visitors climb its spiralling staircase for views over the city.
history: The buildings were raised between the 11th and 14th centuries during Pisa's golden age as a powerful maritime republic. The campanile, or bell tower, began to lean during its construction as its foundations settled into soft soil. Centuries of engineering efforts, culminating in a major stabilisation completed in 2001, have preserved its famous tilt without collapse. UNESCO inscribed the Piazza del Duomo in Pisa in 1987.

name: Mostar Old Bridge
description: The Old Bridge of Mostar gracefully arches over the emerald Neretva River in the heart of the historic Bosnian town. The single-span stone bridge, flanked by stone towers and surrounded by an old bazaar of Ottoman-era houses and mosques, is an iconic image of the region. Local divers famously leap from its parapet into the river far below. The bridge symbolises the meeting of cultures and the reconciliation of a divided land.
history: The original bridge was built by Ottoman engineers in the 16th century and stood for over four centuries as an engineering marvel. It was destroyed during the war in Bosnia in 1993, a loss felt far beyond the region. It was painstakingly rebuilt using traditional methods and reopened in 2004 as a symbol of recovery. UNESCO inscribed the Old Bridge area of Mostar in 2005.

name: Speyer Cathedral
description: Speyer Cathedral in Germany is the largest surviving Romanesque church in the world, a vast and majestic building of red sandstone. Its imposing towers and rounded arches dominate the small Rhineland city. The crypt beneath holds the tombs of medieval German emperors and kings. The cathedral's harmonious proportions make it a landmark of Romanesque architecture.
history: Construction began around 1030 under the Salian emperors, who intended it as a grand imperial burial church and a statement of their power. It became the resting place of eight emperors and kings of the medieval German empire. The building was damaged and restored over the centuries while retaining its Romanesque core. UNESCO inscribed Speyer Cathedral in 1981.

name: Cologne Cathedral
description: Cologne Cathedral is one of the greatest Gothic buildings in the world, its twin spires soaring above the German city beside the Rhine. The vast interior, with its towering nave and brilliant stained glass, is breathtaking in scale. The cathedral houses the Shrine of the Three Kings, a magnificent golden reliquary that drew medieval pilgrims. Climbing the tower rewards visitors with panoramic views over the city.
history: Construction began in 1248 to house the relics of the Three Kings but stalled for centuries, leaving the cathedral unfinished until the 19th century. It was finally completed in 1880, then briefly the tallest building in the world. The cathedral survived the heavy bombing of the city in the Second World War. UNESCO inscribed Cologne Cathedral in 1996.

name: Westminster Abbey
description: Westminster Abbey in London is one of the most significant religious buildings in Britain and the traditional setting for royal coronations. Its soaring Gothic architecture houses the tombs and memorials of monarchs, statesmen, scientists and poets. The Coronation Chair, Poets' Corner and the graves of figures such as Newton and Darwin draw visitors from around the world. The abbey has been at the heart of English national life for nearly a thousand years.
history: A church has stood on the site since the early medieval period, with the present building begun under Henry III in 1245. It has been the coronation church of English and British monarchs since 1066 and the setting for royal weddings and funerals. Generations of the nation's most celebrated figures are buried or commemorated within. UNESCO inscribed Westminster Abbey, with the Palace of Westminster and St Margaret's Church, in 1987.

name: Schönbrunn Palace
description: Schönbrunn Palace in Vienna was the magnificent summer residence of the Habsburg emperors. Its grand Baroque halls, lavishly decorated state rooms and vast formal gardens rival the splendour of Versailles. The gardens rise to the Gloriette pavilion on a hill overlooking the palace, and the grounds include the world's oldest surviving zoo. The yellow palace and its parterres are among Vienna's most visited attractions.
history: The palace was developed in its present form in the 18th century, above all under Empress Maria Theresa, whose family filled its rooms with imperial life. It was the setting for momentous events, including a famous performance by the child Mozart and the negotiations of the Congress of Vienna. It remained an imperial residence until the end of the monarchy in 1918. UNESCO inscribed Schönbrunn Palace and Gardens in 1996.

name: Brasília
description: Brasília, the capital of Brazil, is a landmark of 20th-century urban planning and modernist architecture. Laid out in the shape of an aeroplane, the city is filled with the boldly sculptural buildings of the architect Oscar Niemeyer. The soaring Cathedral, the twin towers of the National Congress and the Palácio da Alvorada are icons of modern design. The whole city was conceived and built as a unified work of art.
history: Brasília was built from scratch in just a few years and inaugurated in 1960 to move the capital inland and spur development of the country's interior. It was designed by the urban planner Lúcio Costa and the architect Oscar Niemeyer as a utopian modern city. The rapid construction in a remote region was a feat of ambition and engineering. UNESCO inscribed Brasília in 1987 as a masterpiece of modern urbanism.

name: Historic Centre of Lima
description: The historic centre of Lima preserves the grandeur of the former capital of Spanish South America. Its colonial heart is filled with ornate churches, balconied mansions and grand squares, above all the Plaza Mayor. The Monastery of San Francisco, with its catacombs and richly decorated cloisters, is a particular highlight. The wooden Moorish-style balconies that line many façades give the old city its distinctive character.
history: Lima was founded by Francisco Pizarro in 1535 and became the powerful capital of the Spanish Viceroyalty of Peru, controlling much of South America. Its wealth from Andean silver funded a flowering of Baroque architecture. The city survived devastating earthquakes that prompted repeated rebuilding. UNESCO inscribed the historic centre of Lima in 1988.

name: Quito Old Town
description: The old town of Quito, high in the Ecuadorian Andes, is one of the largest and best-preserved historic centres in the Americas. Its narrow streets are lined with ornate colonial churches, monasteries and plazas set against a backdrop of volcanic peaks. The dazzling gilded interior of the Compañía de Jesús church is among its greatest treasures. The city lies almost exactly on the equator at over 2,800 metres above sea level.
history: Quito was built by the Spanish in the 16th century on the ruins of an Inca city, itself an important regional centre. It became a thriving colonial city and a centre of religious art known as the Quito School. The historic centre survived largely intact through the centuries. UNESCO inscribed the City of Quito in 1978 as one of its very first World Heritage Sites.

name: Historic Centre of Salvador de Bahia
description: Salvador de Bahia in north-eastern Brazil preserves a vivid colonial centre in its historic Pelourinho district. Brightly painted Baroque churches, mansions and cobbled squares climb the hillside above the bay. The city is the heart of Afro-Brazilian culture, alive with music, religion and cuisine rooted in African heritage. Its golden church interiors, especially São Francisco, are among the most opulent in the Americas.
history: Salvador was the first capital of colonial Brazil, founded in 1549, and a major port of the transatlantic slave trade. The fusion of European, African and indigenous cultures created a uniquely rich heritage that endures in the city's life today. Its colonial architecture reflects the wealth of the sugar economy. UNESCO inscribed the historic centre of Salvador de Bahia in 1985.

name: Nahuel Huapi National Park
description: Nahuel Huapi is the oldest national park in Argentina, set in the lake district of northern Patagonia. Its landscape of deep glacial lakes, snow-capped Andean peaks and ancient forest is strikingly beautiful. The resort town of Bariloche on the shore of Lake Nahuel Huapi is a base for hiking, skiing and boating. The park's scenery has often been compared to the Alps.
history: The core of the park originated in a land donation in 1903 by the explorer Francisco Moreno, who gave it to the nation for protection. It was formally established as a national park in 1934, the first in Argentina. The region's lakes and mountains were sculpted by glaciers during the ice ages. The park remains one of Argentina's premier natural destinations.

name: Tijuca National Park
description: Tijuca National Park is one of the largest urban forests in the world, a swathe of regrown Atlantic rainforest within the city of Rio de Janeiro. Its lush green slopes are crowned by the Corcovado mountain, atop which stands the famous statue of Christ the Redeemer. Trails wind through the forest past waterfalls and viewpoints overlooking the city and sea. It is a green lung and a beloved escape within a great metropolis.
history: The forest was largely cleared for coffee plantations in the 19th century, leading to problems with the city's water supply. In a pioneering act of ecological restoration, the area was deliberately replanted with native trees from the 1860s. The reforestation regenerated a thriving Atlantic forest ecosystem in the heart of the city. Tijuca was designated a national park to protect this remarkable urban wilderness.

name: Chavín de Huántar
description: Chavín de Huántar high in the Peruvian Andes is one of the oldest and most influential ceremonial centres in the Americas. Its stone temple complex contains a labyrinth of underground galleries, at the heart of which stands the carved Lanzón monolith. Finely sculpted stone heads and reliefs of fanged deities reflect a powerful religious tradition. The site lies in a dramatic mountain valley between two rivers.
history: Chavín flourished from around 1500 to 400 BCE as the centre of a religious cult whose influence spread across much of the Andes. It predated and helped shape the great civilisations that followed, including the Inca. Pilgrims are thought to have travelled long distances to its oracle and temples. UNESCO inscribed the archaeological site of Chavín in 1985.

name: Joya de Cerén
description: Joya de Cerén in El Salvador is often called the 'Pompeii of the Americas', a Maya farming village preserved beneath volcanic ash. Unlike grand ceremonial sites, it captures the everyday life of ordinary people, with houses, gardens, tools and even food left in place. The remarkable preservation reveals how common Maya families lived and worked. It offers a rare and intimate glimpse of ancient daily life.
history: The village was buried around 600 CE when a nearby volcano erupted, sealing the settlement under metres of ash. The inhabitants appear to have fled in time, leaving their belongings behind. The site was discovered in 1976 during construction work and has since yielded extraordinary detail about Maya agriculture and domestic life. UNESCO inscribed Joya de Cerén in 1993.
